- Limitations:
- Heterogeneity in interventions, dosages, and timing
- Variation in outcome and biomarker assessment
- Limited direct comparability between studies
- Why this matters: Current immunomodulatory therapies do not provide clear benefit for neurological or survival outcomes after cardiac arrest, highlighting the need for more targeted research.

deep_dives.title: Effect of vascular access strategy on long-term outcomes in patients with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: a randomised clinical trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized clinical trial of adults with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, initial intraosseous versus intravenous vascular access resulted in similar 1-year survival and neurological outcomes, indicating no clinically significant difference between the two strategies for long-term patient outcomes.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Initial intraosseous and intravenous vascular access strategies yield similar long-term survival and neurological outcomes in adults with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.

- Study type: Randomised clinical trial
- Population/setting: Adults with non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest requiring vascular access
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Initial intraosseous vascular access versus initial intravenous vascular access
- Primary endpoints: Survival, survival with favourable neurological outcome (modified Rankin Scale 0-3), and health-related quality of life (EQ-5D-5L) at 6 months and 1 year
- Key results: At 1 year, survival was 11% for intraosseous and 9% for intravenous groups (risk ratio 1.24; 95% CI 0.91-1.67); favourable neurological outcome was 10% vs 8% (risk ratio 1.28; 95% CI 0.93-1.77); mean EQ-5D-5L score among survivors was 83 vs 76 (mean difference 7; 95% CI 1-13).
- Limitations:
- Three patients lost to follow-up for 1-year survival
- Confidence intervals include the possibility of no difference
- Results may not generalize to traumatic cardiac arrest or pediatric populations
- Why this matters: This study informs emergency care providers that either intraosseous or intravenous access can be used for vascular access during adult out-of-hospital cardiac arrest without significant differences in long-term outcomes.

deep_dives.title: Association of perioperative fluid balance and acute kidney injury in patients undergoing elective colorectal surgery: A pre-planned secondary analysis of a multicentre prospective observational study.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large multicenter prospective observational study of 1,139 patients undergoing elective colorectal surgery, higher perioperative fluid balance was strongly associated with an increased risk of postoperative acute kidney injury, highlighting the clinical importance of careful fluid management to reduce AKI risk in this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Higher positive perioperative fluid balance during the first 24 hours is associated with an increased risk of postoperative acute kidney injury in patients undergoing elective colorectal surgery.

- Study type: Prospective observational study; pre-planned secondary analysis of a multicentre study.
- Population/setting: Patients undergoing elective primary colorectal surgery with planned overnight stay in 80 hospitals in Spain between September and December 2017 (N=1139).
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Higher perioperative fluid balance (quartiles Q3 and Q4) compared to the lowest quartile (Q1).
- Primary endpoints: Occurrence of acute kidney injury (AKI) at 30 days following surgery, defined by KDIGO and EPCO guidelines.
- Key results: 6.4% (73/1139) developed AKI; adjusted relative risks for AKI were 4.10 (95% CI, 1.60–10.51) for Q3 and 4.81 (95% CI, 1.91–12.11) for Q4 compared to Q1; higher positive fluid balance was significantly associated with increased AKI risk (P < 0.01).
- Limitations:
- Secondary analysis of an observational study.
- Potential for residual confounding.
- Exclusion of patients with incomplete data or severe renal impairment.
- Why this matters: Understanding the relationship between perioperative fluid management and AKI risk can inform strategies to optimize fluid therapy and reduce postoperative complications in colorectal surgery patients.

deep_dives.title: Influence of Pre-emptive Haptoglobin on Postoperative Acute Kidney Injury in Cardiac Surgical Patients: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this small, single-center randomized controlled trial, pre-emptive haptoglobin therapy guided by serum-free hemoglobin did not show clear benefit in preventing postoperative acute kidney injury after cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, and the study was stopped early for safety concerns, suggesting limited clinical utility and the need for caution with this approach.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Pre-emptive haptoglobin administration in cardiac surgery patients increased postoperative creatinine and was associated with worse renal outcomes.

- Study type: Single-center, open-label, randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: Adult patients undergoing major cardiovascular surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass at a single center
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Pre-emptive haptoglobin therapy (4000 U when serum-free hemoglobin reached 0.05 g/dL) versus standard of care (4000 U haptoglobin only if hemolytic urine was confirmed)
- Primary endpoints: Difference between preoperative creatinine and maximum creatinine within 48 hours after surgery (ΔCr)
- Key results: Median ΔCr was 0.20 (0.05-0.44) in the pre-emptive group and 0.14 (0.04-0.19) in the standard care group (P = .05); pre-emptive haptoglobin significantly increased ΔCr in multivariable analysis (P = .03).
- Limitations:
- Study was terminated early due to safety concerns.
- Single-center design limits generalizability.
- Small sample size (34 and 33 patients per group).
- Open-label design may introduce bias.
- Why this matters: This study suggests that pre-emptive haptoglobin administration may worsen renal outcomes in cardiac surgery patients, challenging its use for preventing acute kidney injury in this setting.

deep_dives.title: The multifaceted neural architecture of pain modulation by emotion and cognition: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 100 neuroimaging studies (n=2735) reveals that emotion and cognition modulate pain through distinct neural pathways, with similar subjective pain changes arising from different brain activation patterns; this underscores the complexity of pain modulation and highlights the need for individualized approaches in pain management.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Pain modulation by emotion and cognition involves multiple neural mechanisms, with different brain activation patterns supporting diverse strategies for pain control.

- Study type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of functional neuroimaging studies
- Population/setting: Healthy individuals; 100 studies with 2735 participants
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Contextual modulation of pain (e.g., attentional, expectation, introspection/self-referential tasks) compared to baseline or control conditions
- Primary endpoints: Brain activity changes in response to pain modulation as measured by functional neuroimaging
- Key results: Three main neural patterns were identified: (1) prefrontal activation with sensory area modulation (deactivation in hypoalgesia, hyperactivation in hyperalgesia), (2) prefrontal activation without nociceptive area changes (perceptual decision bias), and (3) ventromedial prefrontal involvement with deactivation in dorsolateral/ventrolateral prefrontal areas during introspective/self-referential tasks.
- Limitations:
- Exclusion of studies on chronic pain
- Potential heterogeneity in neuroimaging methods and task paradigms
- Risk of bias assessed but not detailed in results
- Why this matters: Understanding the diverse neural mechanisms underlying pain modulation can inform the development of targeted behavioral and neuromodulation-based pain therapies.

deep_dives.title: Healthcare costs of expedited transport compared with standard care following out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: results from the EVIDENCE randomised controlled trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This randomized controlled trial found no significant difference in healthcare costs between expedited transport and standard care after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, but care during ECPR-available (business) hours was more expensive; these findings suggest that expedited transport does not increase costs, but timing of care impacts resource use.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Expedited transport and standard care for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest resulted in similar health system costs, but care during ECPR-available hours was more expensive.

- Study type: Prospective costing analysis alongside a randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: 197 randomized participants with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest in Greater Sydney, Australia
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Expedited transport versus standard care; comparison also by timing (inside vs. outside ECPR-available hours)
- Primary endpoints: Mean total and per-participant healthcare use and costs from paramedic contact to hospital discharge or death
- Key results: Mean costs per patient were AUD$60,780 for expedited transport and $67,579 for standard care (difference: -$6,799; 95% CI: -$44,014 to $30,416; not significant). Mean costs were $36,182 higher within ECPR hours compared to outside (95% CI: $1,554–$72,918).
- Limitations:
- Single metropolitan region may limit generalizability
- Cost differences not statistically significant
- Potential variability in ECPR availability and implementation
- Why this matters: Understanding the cost implications of different pre-hospital transport strategies can inform resource allocation and policy decisions for cardiac arrest care.

pubmed.title: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This moderate-certainty meta-analysis of 11 RCTs (n=5562) found that systemic prophylactic antibacterials in mechanically ventilated ICU patients probably reduce the incidence of VAP, but do not significantly impact mortality, duration of ventilation, or hospital stay, suggesting limited clinical benefit beyond VAP prevention.

pubmed.title: Enteral When Compared With IV Magnesium Replacement in the Critically Ill: A Noninferiority Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this single-center randomized noninferiority trial of 360 critically ill patients with hypomagnesemia, enteral magnesium replacement did not meet noninferiority criteria compared to IV replacement for serum magnesium correction at 24 hours, though it was associated with lower costs, waste, and environmental impact; thus, while enteral replacement offers sustainability benefits, IV administration remains more effective for rapid correction in this setting.

pubmed.title: An Exploration of "Near-Miss" Events in Non-Operating Room Anesthesia Locations.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective cohort study found a 3.2% incidence of near-miss events in non-operating room anesthesia (NORA) settings, with environmental factors—especially poor group dynamics—being the most common contributors; these findings highlight the need for targeted safety interventions and improved team communication in NORA environments.

pubmed.title: Regional Alveolar Damage Despite Lung Protective Ventilation Settings During Robotic-Assisted Laparoscopic Surgery.
pubmed.bottom_line: This small observational study found evidence of regional alveolar injury in dependent lung areas during robotic-assisted laparoscopic surgery, even when standard lung protective ventilation settings were used, suggesting that current ventilation strategies may not fully prevent perioperative lung damage in this context and may warrant reconsideration of intraoperative ventilatory management.

pubmed.title: Plant-Based Diet and Risk of Sepsis: A 16-Year Follow-Up Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large prospective UK Biobank cohort study found that greater adherence to a healthy plant-based diet was associated with a significantly lower risk of sepsis, while unhealthy plant-based diets increased risk, suggesting that diet quality—not just plant-based content—matters for sepsis prevention; evidence is strong due to the large sample size and robust adjustments, making these findings clinically relevant for dietary counseling.

pubmed.title: Does the Frailty Burden Impact ICU Performance Indicators Benchmarking? A Multicenter Cohort Study Using Two Severity-of-Illness Scores.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large multicenter cohort study, ICU frailty burden did not significantly impact standardized mortality or resource use indicators when using or recalibrating common severity-of-illness scores, suggesting that current benchmarking metrics remain valid regardless of frailty prevalence. This supports continued use of these performance indicators without adjustment for frailty in ICU benchmarking.

pubmed.title: Outcomes of cardiac arrest occurred in detention: a French nationwide study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective French study found that cardiac arrest survival at 30 days is significantly lower in incarcerated individuals compared to the general population, highlighting a need for targeted prevention and improved emergency response in detention settings.

pubmed.title: Postoperative hypotension in patients recovering from noncardiac surgery: a prospective, blinded observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large prospective observational study found that postoperative hypotension (MAP <65 mm Hg) is common but typically not severe or prolonged after noncardiac surgery; however, its presence is associated with increased rates of acute kidney injury and unplanned ICU admissions, highlighting the need for vigilant postoperative blood pressure monitoring.

pubmed.title: ETHER: a proposal for a European peri-operative data sharing and registry network.
pubmed.bottom_line: This article proposes the creation of a European peri-operative data sharing and registry network to standardize and integrate clinical data across Europe, aiming to improve outcomes research, quality benchmarking, and policy evaluation; while not presenting new clinical evidence, it highlights a significant infrastructure initiative that could enhance peri-operative care and research capabilities.

pubmed.title: Does peripartum intravenous calcium administration reduce the occurrence of uterine atony in caesarean sections?: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis found that peripartum intravenous calcium administration reduces the occurrence of uterine atony during caesarean sections, suggesting a potentially effective intervention to decrease postpartum hemorrhage risk; however, the strength of evidence and clinical recommendations depend on the quality and size of included studies.

pubmed.title: HEAVEN criteria to predict difficult in-hospital rapid sequence intubation: a prospective single-centre observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective single-centre observational study suggests that the HEAVEN criteria may be useful for predicting difficult in-hospital rapid sequence intubation, providing clinicians with a potentially valuable tool for airway management, though further validation in broader settings is needed.

pubmed.title: Telemedicine Use and Outcomes Following Discharge From the Emergency Department, 2020-2022.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective cohort study, telemedicine follow-up after emergency department discharge for chronic conditions was used infrequently but was not associated with higher rates of return hospitalization compared to in-person follow-up, suggesting telemedicine is a safe alternative for post-ED care in appropriately selected patients.

pubmed.title: Cardiopulmonary fitness in patients undergoing major hepato-pancreatobiliary surgery who are non-anaemic and iron replete vs. non-anaemic and iron deficient: an observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This observational study suggests that non-anaemic but iron-deficient patients undergoing major hepato-pancreatobiliary surgery may have reduced cardiopulmonary fitness compared to iron-replete counterparts, highlighting the potential clinical importance of assessing and addressing iron deficiency even in the absence of anaemia.

pubmed.title: Carbon emissions from total intravenous vs. volatile anaesthesia for elective cholecystectomy: a pilot observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This pilot observational study suggests that total intravenous anaesthesia produces lower carbon emissions compared to volatile anaesthesia for elective cholecystectomy, highlighting a potential environmental benefit that may inform anaesthetic choice, though larger studies are needed to confirm these findings.

pubmed.title: Adjuvant Nivolumab vs Observation in Resected Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, randomized phase 3 trial found that adjuvant nivolumab did not improve disease-free or overall survival compared to observation in patients with resected non-small cell lung cancer, leading to early trial termination for futility; these results suggest adjuvant nivolumab should not be routinely used in this setting.

pubmed.title: Perioperative serplulimab with neoadjuvant chemotherapy versus perioperative chemotherapy in PD-L1-positive gastric cancer (ASTRUM-006): a randomised, double-blind, multicentre, phase 3 study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large phase 3 trial, adding perioperative serplulimab (a PD-1 inhibitor) to neoadjuvant and adjuvant chemotherapy significantly improved event-free survival in patients with PD-L1-positive, locally advanced, resectable gastric or gastro-oesophageal junction adenocarcinoma, suggesting a new standard of care for this population.

foamed.title: Personalized Hemodynamic Resuscitation Targeting Capillary Refill Time in Early Septic Shock: The ANDROMEDA-SHOCK-2 Randomized Clinical Trial
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In the ANDROMEDA-SHOCK-2 trial, a personalized hemodynamic resuscitation protocol targeting capillary refill time in early septic shock was superior to usual care for a hierarchical composite outcome (Win ratio 1.16, 95% CI 1.04–1.28), but did not reduce 28-day mortality (26.5% vs. 26.6%).

foamed.title: Should I treat Strep throat?
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational discussion exploring the evidence and uncertainties surrounding the treatment of streptococcal pharyngitis, including the benefits and limitations of antibiotics, but does not present new clinical data or study results.

foamed.title: OpenAnesthesia – International Anesthesia Research Society
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This content is an informational announcement about membership benefits, including access to OpenAnesthesia, and does not present new clinical data or research findings.

deep_dives.title: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This moderate-certainty meta-analysis of 11 RCTs (n=5562) found that systemic prophylactic antibacterials in mechanically ventilated ICU patients probably reduce the incidence of VAP, but do not significantly impact mortality, duration of ventilation, or hospital stay, suggesting limited clinical benefit beyond VAP prevention.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** This moderate-certainty meta-analysis of 11 RCTs (n=5562) found that systemic prophylactic antibacterials in mechanically ventilated ICU patients probably reduce the incidence of VAP, but do not significantly impact mortality, duration of ventilation, or hospital stay, suggesting limited clinical benefit beyond VAP prevention.

BOTTOM LINE: Systemic prophylactic antibacterials probably reduce the incidence of early and overall VAP in mechanically ventilated patients, but do not reduce in-hospital mortality.

- Study type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
- Population/setting: Mechanically ventilated patients in ICUs; 11 RCTs with 5562 patients
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Systemic prophylactic antibacterials versus standard care or placebo
- Primary endpoints: Incidence of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP), in-hospital mortality, duration of mechanical ventilation, length of hospital stay, drug resistance, and adverse events
- Key results: Systemic prophylactic antibacterials reduced VAP incidence (RR 0.65, 95% CI 0.55-0.77) and early VAP (RR 0.52, 95% CI 0.41-0.66), but did not reduce in-hospital mortality (RR 0.85, 95% CI 0.68-1.06), duration of mechanical ventilation (MD -0.32 days, 95% CI -0.79 to 0.16), or length of hospital stay (MD -1.56 days, 95% CI -5.15 to 2.02); no increased risk of drug resistance or adverse events was observed.
- Limitations:
- Incomplete safety reporting
- Low certainty for some outcomes (e.g., length of hospital stay)
- Need for comprehensive resistance surveillance and cost-effectiveness analyses
- Why this matters: This study informs clinicians that while systemic prophylactic antibacterials can reduce VAP incidence in mechanically ventilated patients, they do not improve mortality, highlighting the need for careful consideration of benefits versus risks and further research on safety and cost-effectiveness.

deep_dives.title: Enteral When Compared With IV Magnesium Replacement in the Critically Ill: A Noninferiority Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this single-center randomized noninferiority trial of 360 critically ill patients with hypomagnesemia, enteral magnesium replacement did not meet noninferiority criteria compared to IV replacement for serum magnesium correction at 24 hours, though it was associated with lower costs, waste, and environmental impact; thus, while enteral replacement offers sustainability benefits, IV administration remains more effective for rapid correction in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** In this single-center randomized noninferiority trial of 360 critically ill patients with hypomagnesemia, enteral magnesium replacement did not meet noninferiority criteria compared to IV replacement for serum magnesium correction at 24 hours, though it was associated with lower costs, waste, and environmental impact; thus, while enteral replacement offers sustainability benefits, IV administration remains more effective for rapid correction in this setting.

BOTTOM LINE: Enteral magnesium replacement did not meet noninferiority criteria compared to IV replacement for correcting hypomagnesemia but offered advantages in cost, environmental impact, and reduced urinary magnesium loss.

- Study type: Prospective, open-label, randomized, noninferiority trial
- Population/setting: Critically ill patients with serum magnesium 0.35–0.7 mmol/L in a single-center mixed medical-surgical-trauma ICU
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Enteral magnesium replacement versus IV magnesium replacement
- Primary endpoints: Serum magnesium concentration at 24 hours after enrollment (noninferiority margin 0.1 mmol/L)
- Key results: At 24 hours, mean magnesium was 0.80 mmol/L (enteral) vs 0.92 mmol/L (IV), mean difference -0.12 mmol/L (95% CI, -0.16 to -0.07), not confirming noninferiority; enteral replacement reduced urinary magnesium, cost, waste, carbon emissions, and IV fluid use, with no differences in other clinical outcomes.
- Limitations:
- Single-center study
- Open-label design
- Noninferiority margin may affect interpretation
- Results may not generalize to severe hypomagnesemia or other settings
- Why this matters: Enteral magnesium replacement may be a cost-effective and environmentally sustainable alternative to IV therapy for mild-to-moderate hypomagnesemia, though it may be less effective in rapidly correcting serum magnesium.

deep_dives.title: Adjuvant Nivolumab vs Observation in Resected Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large, randomized phase 3 trial found that adjuvant nivolumab did not improve disease-free or overall survival compared to observation in patients with resected non-small cell lung cancer, leading to early trial termination for futility; these results suggest adjuvant nivolumab should not be routinely used in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Adjuvant nivolumab did not improve disease-free survival compared to observation in patients with resected non-small cell lung cancer after standard adjuvant therapy.

- Study type: Open-label, randomized phase 3 clinical trial
- Population/setting: Patients with resected non-small cell lung cancer (tumors ≥4 cm and/or lymph node positive, without EGFR/ALK sensitizing mutations), enrolled at 378 centers in the US National Clinical Trials Network from May 2016 to September 2019.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks for up to 1 year versus standard care observation.
- Primary endpoints: Co-primary endpoints were disease-free survival in the intention-to-treat population and in those with tumoral PD-L1 expression ≥50%.
- Key results: Median disease-free survival was 71.3 months with nivolumab and 68.8 months with observation (HR 0.97, 97% CI 0.79-1.20; 1-sided P = .39); in the PD-L1 ≥50% subgroup, median DFS was 89.8 vs 78.5 months (HR 0.86, 98% CI 0.55-1.34; 1-sided P = .22). The trial was stopped for futility at 75% information.
- Limitations:
- Trial stopped early for futility.
- Open-label design may introduce bias.
- Results may not generalize to patients with EGFR or ALK mutations.
- Why this matters: These findings suggest that adjuvant nivolumab should not be routinely used after surgery and standard adjuvant therapy in resected non-small cell lung cancer without EGFR/ALK alterations.

pubmed.title: Randomized Double-Blinded Clinical Trial of Oxytocin Bolus versus Infusion in Elective Cesarean (INBOX Trial).
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized, double-blind trial of 115 women undergoing elective cesarean delivery, oxytocin bolus and infusion resulted in similar rates of adequate uterine tone at 2 minutes and comparable patient satisfaction, suggesting no clear clinical advantage of bolus over infusion for uterine tone in this setting. This evidence supports flexibility in oxytocin administration method for postpartum hemorrhage prevention during elective cesarean.

pubmed.title: Personalized automatic management of tracheal cuff pressure and subglottic secretions drainage to prevent pneumonia in critically ill intubated patients. The MICROINHALO multicenter randomized controlled trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this multicenter randomized controlled trial, personalized automatic management of tracheal cuff pressure and subglottic secretion drainage did not reduce tracheal colonization compared to manual management, but it significantly lowered rates of ventilator-associated pneumonia, suggesting clinical benefit in pneumonia prevention despite no effect on colonization.

pubmed.title: Impact of Intravenous Lidocaine, Dexmedetomidine, and Intrathecal Morphine on Metastasis-Related Biomarkers and Cellular Immune Profiles in Colorectal Surgery: A Prospective, Randomized Controlled Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized controlled trial of patients undergoing colorectal cancer surgery, intravenous dexmedetomidine was associated with lower postoperative MMP-9 levels and favorable immune cell profiles compared to lidocaine and intrathecal morphine, suggesting it may better preserve anti-metastatic immune function; however, clinical outcome differences were not reported, so the findings are mechanistically important but not yet practice-changing.

pubmed.title: Nutrition support in the ICU: current evidence and evolving standards.
pubmed.bottom_line: Recent large randomized trials indicate that early full nutritional replacement in critically ill ICU patients may be harmful, supporting a shift toward cautious, individualized low-calorie and low-protein feeding during the acute phase, with careful monitoring and a multidisciplinary approach; this evolving standard matters clinically to reduce complications and improve recovery, though optimal macronutrient targets in later phases remain unclear.

pubmed.title: Agreement of minimally invasive pulse wave analysis with pulmonary artery and transpulmonary thermodilution cardiac output measurements in perioperative and intensive care medicine: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, low-bias meta-analysis found that minimally invasive pulse wave analysis for cardiac output and index has a high percentage error (44–49%) compared to thermodilution reference methods in perioperative and ICU patients, indicating limited reliability and suggesting these devices should be used with caution for critical clinical decisions.

pubmed.title: Synergistic Association of Prematurity and Preoperative Anemia with Neonatal Postoperative Mortality.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective cohort study found that prematurity and preoperative anemia synergistically increase the risk of postoperative mortality in neonates, with preterm anemic infants having a markedly higher adjusted relative risk; this underscores the importance of targeted preoperative optimization in this high-risk group.

pubmed.title: A Multi-Department Investigation of Occupational Well-Being, Systemic Features, and Organizational Outcomes in Anesthesiology Using the Well-Being Influencers Survey for Healthcare (WISH).
pubmed.bottom_line: This large multicenter study validates the Well-Being Influencers Survey for Healthcare (WISH) as a reliable tool for assessing systemic factors influencing occupational well-being in anesthesiology, demonstrating that better systemic well-being scores are strongly associated with lower intention to leave and higher organizational commitment; this evidence supports using WISH to guide targeted interventions to improve workforce stability and patient care.

pubmed.title: Perineural dexmedetomidine decreases the onset time of bupivacaine 0.5%-epinephrine-dexamethasone for infraclavicular block: a randomized trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This randomized trial found that adding perineural dexmedetomidine to bupivacaine-epinephrine-dexamethasone significantly shortens the onset time of infraclavicular block in a dose-dependent manner, without increasing adverse events, suggesting a clinically useful strategy to expedite block onset for upper limb surgery.

pubmed.title: Glucagon-like peptide 1 receptor agonist use and risk of arthroplasty for knee osteoarthritis: retrospective database analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective cohort study found that use of GLP-1 receptor agonists, especially newer agents like semaglutide or tirzepatide and with longer duration, was associated with a modest but statistically significant reduction in the risk of total knee arthroplasty among patients with knee osteoarthritis; while observational, these findings suggest a potential disease-modifying effect that could influence management strategies for knee OA.

foamed.title: JournalFeed Blog | High-Yield Medical Research Summaries
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: The provided content summarizes recent research indicating that carotid artery POCUS during out-of-hospital cardiac arrest by EMS providers results in shorter compression pauses and better imaging than subxiphoid cardiac POCUS, and also offers an educational review of current best practices and considerations in airway management for acute care settings.

foamed.title: Latest in Pediatric Emergency Care Journals - JournalFeed
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational review summarizing current best practices and ongoing questions regarding airway management and tracheal intubation in pediatric emergency and critical care settings, without presenting new clinical trial data or original research findings.

foamed.title: Needle-tip pressure monitoring detects intraneural injection earlier than ultrasound
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In a cadaveric study, needle-tip pressure monitoring detected intraneural injection during regional anesthesia earlier than ultrasound-based identification of nerve swelling, suggesting that real-time pressure monitoring may enhance safety by providing earlier warning of potentially harmful intraneural injection compared to ultrasound alone.

deep_dives.title: Impact of Intravenous Lidocaine, Dexmedetomidine, and Intrathecal Morphine on Metastasis-Related Biomarkers and Cellular Immune Profiles in Colorectal Surgery: A Prospective, Randomized Controlled Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized controlled trial of patients undergoing colorectal cancer surgery, intravenous dexmedetomidine was associated with lower postoperative MMP-9 levels and favorable immune cell profiles compared to lidocaine and intrathecal morphine, suggesting it may better preserve anti-metastatic immune function; however, clinical outcome differences were not reported, so the findings are mechanistically important but not yet practice-changing.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Intravenous lidocaine, dexmedetomidine, and intrathecal morphine have distinct effects on perioperative metastasis-related biomarkers and immune profiles in colorectal cancer surgery, with intrathecal morphine providing superior analgesia and minimal immune impact.

- Study type: Prospective, randomized, patient- and assessor-blinded controlled trial
- Population/setting: Adults undergoing elective laparoscopic or robotic colorectal cancer resection at a single center (N=109 analyzed)
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Intravenous lidocaine vs. dexmedetomidine vs. intrathecal morphine
- Primary endpoints: Plasma matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9) concentration at 1 hour postoperatively
- Key results: Lidocaine group had higher MMP-9 at 1 hour than dexmedetomidine (difference on log scale 0.333; 95% CI, 0.0642-0.601; P=0.009) and intrathecal morphine (0.424; 95% CI, 0.0248-0.823; P=0.033); dexmedetomidine increased CD73+CD8+ T cells vs. lidocaine (difference on logit scale 0.669; 95% CI, 0.000987-1.34; P=0.050); intrathecal morphine provided lower dynamic pain scores; rescue antiemetic use was less frequent with dexmedetomidine; other adverse events were mild and similar across groups.
- Limitations:
- Single-center study
- Short-term biomarker and immune profile assessment
- No long-term oncologic outcomes reported
- Why this matters: Understanding the immunologic and biomarker effects of anesthetic adjuvants may inform perioperative strategies to optimize cancer surgery outcomes and potentially influence tumor progression.

deep_dives.title: Personalized automatic management of tracheal cuff pressure and subglottic secretions drainage to prevent pneumonia in critically ill intubated patients. The MICROINHALO multicenter randomized controlled trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this multicenter randomized controlled trial, personalized automatic management of tracheal cuff pressure and subglottic secretion drainage did not reduce tracheal colonization compared to manual management, but it significantly lowered rates of ventilator-associated pneumonia, suggesting clinical benefit in pneumonia prevention despite no effect on colonization.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Personalized automatic management of tracheal cuff pressure and subglottic secretion drainage did not reduce tracheal colonization compared to manual management, but was associated with lower rates of ventilator-associated pneumonia.

- Study type: Cluster-randomized, international, open-label trial
- Population/setting: Adult critically ill intubated patients in 10 ICUs
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Automatic endotracheal tube cuff pressure management and automatic subglottic secretion drainage versus manual management of both
- Primary endpoints: Rate of bacterial tracheal colonization (> 10^3 CFU/mL) on day 3 after intubation
- Key results: Tracheal colonization on day 3 occurred in 37% (automatic) vs. 41.5% (manual) (absolute difference -4%, 95% CI -16 to 8, P=0.52); clinically diagnosed VAP was lower in the automatic group (12.6% vs. 24.4%, P=0.016), as was microbiologically confirmed VAP (10.2% vs. 19.5%, P=0.039); fewer cuff pressures outside safety range and higher daily SSD volume were observed in the automatic group.
- Limitations:
- Primary endpoint (tracheal colonization) showed no significant difference
- Open-label design may introduce bias
- Further research needed to confirm VAP reduction
- Why this matters: This study suggests that while automatic management does not reduce tracheal colonization, it may lower the incidence of ventilator-associated pneumonia, which is clinically significant for ICU patient outcomes.

pubmed.title: Plasma antioxidant capacity as a predictor of mortality in critically ill patients.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this prospective multicenter study of 464 critically ill postoperative patients, elevated plasma antioxidant capacity (FRAP) within 24 hours was independently associated with increased 90-day mortality and outperformed APACHE II in mortality prediction, suggesting FRAP may be a valuable prognostic biomarker for critically ill patients.

pubmed.title: Definitions and Prognoses of Persistent Inflammation, Immunosuppression, and Catabolism Syndrome: A Comparative Analysis via Systematic Review and Observational Study in Japan.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large Japanese cohort study and systematic review found substantial heterogeneity in definitions of persistent inflammation, immunosuppression, and catabolism syndrome (PICS), leading to wide variation in prevalence and outcomes; this highlights the urgent need for standardized diagnostic criteria to improve prognostication and management in critically ill patients.

pubmed.title: Impact of Time to Antibiotics on In-hospital Mortality in Neutropenic Sepsis: A Prospective Multicenter Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, prospective multicenter cohort study found that delays in antibiotic administration beyond 1 hour significantly increased in-hospital mortality in neutropenic sepsis, especially among patients with septic shock, hematologic malignancy, or elevated lactate, underscoring the critical importance of rapid antibiotic delivery in this population.

pubmed.title: Clinical characteristics and outcomes in the adult cystic fibrosis population in Europe from 2014 to 2024: analysis of the European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry.
pubmed.bottom_line: Analysis of over 55,000 European adults with cystic fibrosis from 2014 to 2024 shows substantial improvements in lung function, nutritional status, and reduced infection rates—especially after 2020—likely due to widespread use of CFTR modulators, but with a rising prevalence of age-related complications, highlighting the need for evolving adult-focused care strategies.

pubmed.title: All-Oral Treatment of Newly Diagnosed Acute Myeloid Leukemia.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: This phase 1-2 nonrandomized trial shows that an all-oral regimen of decitabine-cedazuridine plus venetoclax is feasible and effective for newly diagnosed AML patients ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, achieving a 47% complete response rate and median overall survival of 15.5 months, suggesting a promising, less burdensome alternative to parenteral therapy, though randomized trials are needed to confirm these findings.

pubmed.title: Phase 3 Trial of Secukinumab in Polymyalgia Rheumatica.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this phase 3 trial, secukinumab significantly increased sustained remission rates and reduced glucocorticoid exposure in patients with relapsed polymyalgia rheumatica compared to placebo, with similar rates of serious adverse events, suggesting it is an effective and potentially safer steroid-sparing option for this population.

pubmed.title: Do psychological assessment tools predict spinal cord stimulation treatment outcomes?
pubmed.bottom_line: This retrospective cohort study found that commonly used psychological assessment tools (depression, pain disability, and personality inventories) did not independently predict spinal cord stimulation (SCS) treatment success at 6 or 12 months, though depression may influence the relationship between baseline and follow-up pain; these findings suggest that routine psychological screening may have limited value in predicting SCS outcomes, highlighting the need for more nuanced approaches in patient selection.

foamed.title: Update on LVADs in the ED: Presentations, Evaluation, and Management
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article provides an educational overview of the emergency department presentations, evaluation, and management of patients with modern left ventricular assist devices (LVADs), including common complications and device parameters to assess, but does not present new clinical trial data or original research findings.

foamed.title: Updates from Emergency Medicine Journals - JournalFeed
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This review provides an educational summary of current best practices and ongoing questions in airway management for acute care settings, including preparation for difficult airways, use of checklists, optimal preoxygenation strategies, and patient positioning, but does not present new clinical trial data or original research findings.

deep_dives.title: All-Oral Treatment of Newly Diagnosed Acute Myeloid Leukemia.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: This phase 1-2 nonrandomized trial shows that an all-oral regimen of decitabine-cedazuridine plus venetoclax is feasible and effective for newly diagnosed AML patients ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, achieving a 47% complete response rate and median overall survival of 15.5 months, suggesting a promising, less burdensome alternative to parenteral therapy, though randomized trials are needed to confirm these findings.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: All-oral decitabine-cedazuridine plus venetoclax is effective and safe for newly diagnosed AML patients ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, achieving a complete response in nearly half of patients.

- Study type: Phase 1-2 open-label, multicenter, nonrandomized trial
- Population/setting: 189 patients with newly diagnosed AML who were 75 years or older or ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, enrolled at multiple centers
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Oral decitabine-cedazuridine plus oral venetoclax; no active comparator
- Primary endpoints: Venetoclax pharmacokinetics (AUC and Cmax) with or without decitabine-cedazuridine (phase 1-2a) and complete response rate (phase 2a-b)
- Key results: No drug-drug interactions were observed; in phase 2b, complete response rate was 47% (95% CI, 36-57), complete response or complete response with incomplete hematologic recovery was 63% (95% CI, 53-73), and median overall survival was 15.5 months (95% CI, 7.6 to not estimable); grade 3 or higher anemia (30%), neutropenia (26%), and febrile neutropenia (25%) were common; 30-day mortality was 3%, 60-day mortality was 10%.
- Limitations:
- Nonrandomized design
- No active comparator group
- Short follow-up for some endpoints
- Potential selection bias
- Why this matters: An all-oral regimen may reduce treatment burden and improve accessibility for older or frail AML patients who cannot receive intensive chemotherapy.

pubmed.title: Higher versus Routine Intraoperative Blood Pressure Targets in Noncardiac Surgery: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis with Trial Sequential Analysis of Randomized Trials.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large meta-analysis of randomized trials found that targeting higher intraoperative blood pressure during noncardiac surgery does not reduce acute kidney injury or myocardial injury compared to routine targets, with strong evidence supporting these findings; while there may be a reduction in postoperative delirium, the evidence is not yet sufficient to recommend higher targets for this outcome, suggesting current guideline thresholds remain appropriate.

pubmed.title: Multimodal Perfusion Assessment in Hemodynamically Unstable Patients: A Concise Definitive Review.
pubmed.bottom_line: This review highlights that multimodal perfusion assessment, integrating tools like capillary refill time with advanced monitoring, offers a more physiologically sound and clinically relevant approach than relying on single markers in hemodynamically unstable patients; while evidence is synthesized from diverse study types, the recommendation is based on expert consensus and physiologic rationale rather than new randomized trial data.

pubmed.title: Implementation of a Remote Respiratory Therapy in a Donor Center ICU Using a Telecritical Care Platform.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective observational study found that comprehensive remote respiratory therapy, including full ventilator management via telecritical care, was safely and effectively implemented in a donor ICU, reducing bedside staffing needs and costs without compromising donor outcomes; these results support broader adoption of remote RT models, pending regulatory considerations.

pubmed.title: Dose-response relationship of early out-of-bed mobilization with functional decline and adverse events in patients after cardiovascular surgery requiring prolonged intensive care: a retrospective cohort study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this retrospective cohort study of post-cardiovascular surgery ICU patients, higher doses of early out-of-bed mobilization were associated with significantly less functional decline at discharge without an increase in adverse events, suggesting that more intensive early mobilization is both safe and beneficial for functional recovery in this population.

pubmed.title: Variation in postinduction hypotension incidence and patient characteristics depending on the definition used: Application of literature-derived definitions to a prospective cohort.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective cohort study found that the reported incidence of postinduction hypotension during general anesthesia varies widely (10–89%) depending on which of 56 literature-derived definitions is used, highlighting significant inconsistencies in patient identification and suggesting a need for standardized definitions to improve clinical relevance and comparability.

pubmed.title: Single-injection adductor canal block with perineural dexamethasone versus continuous adductor canal catheter for total knee arthroplasty: a randomized controlled trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized controlled trial of 94 TKA patients, single-injection adductor canal block with perineural dexamethasone provided similar opioid-sparing effects during the 24–48 hour postoperative period as continuous adductor canal catheter infusion, suggesting that the simpler single-injection approach may be sufficient for pain control in this setting.

pubmed.title: US Department of Defense neuromodulation guidelines in active-duty service members.
pubmed.bottom_line: This article presents new US Department of Defense guidelines for neuromodulation in active-duty service members, providing standardized recommendations that may improve consistency and safety in the use of these therapies within military populations; however, the strength of evidence underlying these guidelines is not specified in the abstract.

foamed.title: Neuraxial anesthesia linked to safer recovery after hip fracture surgery
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In a large national database study of over 90,000 geriatric hip fracture cases, neuraxial anesthesia was associated with lower 30-day readmission rates, reduced in-hospital mortality, decreased incidence of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, and lower hospitalization costs compared to general anesthesia after hip arthroplasty.

foamed.title: ED Return Visits: Separating Signal from Noise
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational commentary discussing the complexities of emergency department return visits, emphasizing that such revisits result from a variety of factors—including natural disease progression, follow-up barriers, and occasional errors—and highlighting the importance of distinguishing meaningful safety signals from expected or unavoidable returns rather than treating all revisits as equivalent indicators of care quality.

foamed.title: Best Online POCUS Courses
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational summary that reviews and compares key features, pros and cons, and pricing options of various online point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) courses, but does not present new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.title: JC: Ambient AI scribes in the ED
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This retrospective observational study of ambient AI scribe implementation in a US academic ED found low and highly concentrated adoption among clinicians, and while it described changes in documentation and EHR time metrics, it did not establish causality or determine whether the tool safely saves time across the emergency department.

deep_dives.title: Higher versus Routine Intraoperative Blood Pressure Targets in Noncardiac Surgery: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis with Trial Sequential Analysis of Randomized Trials.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large meta-analysis of randomized trials found that targeting higher intraoperative blood pressure during noncardiac surgery does not reduce acute kidney injury or myocardial injury compared to routine targets, with strong evidence supporting these findings; while there may be a reduction in postoperative delirium, the evidence is not yet sufficient to recommend higher targets for this outcome, suggesting current guideline thresholds remain appropriate.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Targeting higher intraoperative blood pressure values during elective noncardiac surgery does not improve major postoperative outcomes compared to routine management, though a possible reduction in postoperative delirium requires further study.

- Study type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized trials
- Population/setting: Adults undergoing elective noncardiac surgery with general anesthesia across 15 randomized trials (15,603 patients)
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Higher intraoperative blood pressure targets (fixed absolute thresholds or personalized to preoperative baseline) versus routine blood pressure management
- Primary endpoints: In-hospital or 30-day mortality, postoperative delirium, acute kidney injury (AKI), 30-day major cardiovascular events (MACE), acute myocardial injury, stroke, length of stay, and intraoperative hypotension
- Key results: Higher targets did not reduce AKI (RR = 0.95; 95% CI = 0.85–1.06), acute myocardial injury (RR = 1.02; 95% CI = 0.94–1.12), or mortality (RR = 1.00; 95% CI = 0.75–1.34); higher targets were associated with reduced postoperative delirium (RR = 0.73; 95% CI = 0.54–0.98), but evidence was insufficient for firm conclusions.
- Limitations:
- Insufficient cumulative evidence for some outcomes (e.g., postoperative delirium, MACE, stroke, length of stay)
- Heterogeneity in blood pressure targets and definitions across included trials
- Potential for publication bias and variation in outcome assessment
- Why this matters: This analysis informs perioperative blood pressure management by showing that higher intraoperative blood pressure targets do not improve most major outcomes, supporting current guideline recommendations and highlighting the need for further research on delirium.

deep_dives.title: Multimodal Perfusion Assessment in Hemodynamically Unstable Patients: A Concise Definitive Review.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This review highlights that multimodal perfusion assessment, integrating tools like capillary refill time with advanced monitoring, offers a more physiologically sound and clinically relevant approach than relying on single markers in hemodynamically unstable patients; while evidence is synthesized from diverse study types, the recommendation is based on expert consensus and physiologic rationale rather than new randomized trial data.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Multimodal perfusion assessment should be integrated and individualized, using capillary refill time as a clinical anchor and escalating monitoring based on patient risk and uncertainty.

- Study type: Concise definitive review of physiologic and clinical studies
- Population/setting: Critically ill, hemodynamically unstable patients in clinical settings
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Multimodal perfusion assessment tools (e.g., capillary refill time, lactate, peripheral indices) compared to reliance on single markers
- Primary endpoints: Evaluation of the clinical applicability, strengths, and limitations of various perfusion monitoring modalities
- Key results: No single marker is superior; capillary refill time is pragmatic, and complementary tools refine assessment when physiologic coherence is uncertain.
- Limitations:
- Narrative review design limits quantitative synthesis
- Potential selection bias in included studies
- Lack of new primary data
- Why this matters: Guides clinicians to use a stepwise, physiology-driven approach to perfusion assessment, supporting individualized resuscitation and avoiding unnecessary interventions.

foamed.title: JC: Prehospital resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta (REBOA) in non-traumatic out of hospital cardiac arrest: The REBOARREST Trial
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article discusses the REBOARREST Trial, the first randomized controlled trial investigating whether prehospital resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta (REBOA) improves outcomes in non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, but does not present new data or results within the provided excerpt.

foamed.title: AI Scribes for Clinical Practice
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational article reviews the current use, benefits, and limitations of AI scribes in clinical practice, highlighting concerns about accuracy—particularly in complex cases where error rates can reach 35%—and emphasizing the need for clinician awareness regarding accuracy, consent, and safety, but does not present new clinical study data.

pubmed.title: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound : 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations.
pubmed.bottom_line: This 2025 international consensus, based on a rigorous Delphi process and review of 1775 publications, provides updated, evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound (PoCLUS) as a standalone diagnostic tool, reflecting significant advances since 2012 and offering strong guidance for its clinical application in intensive care settings.

pubmed.title: Rapid Sequence Induction Practices and Outcomes in Abdominal Surgery Patients: A Multicenter Observational Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large multicenter observational study found that while rocuronium use for rapid sequence induction in abdominal surgery has increased and largely replaced succinylcholine, there was no significant difference in 30-day mortality or major perioperative complications between the two agents, supporting the safety of either choice in clinical practice.

foamed.title: Intrathecal adjuvants in cesarean delivery
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: A 2026 systematic review and network meta-analysis of 166 randomized controlled trials (14,925 patients) provides a comprehensive comparison of various intrathecal adjuvants—including opioids, alpha-2 agonists, benzodiazepines, neostigmine, ketamine, dexamethasone, and combinations—used to enhance spinal anesthesia efficacy and safety during cesarean delivery.

foamed.title: Rural EM: PREOXI Trial Protocol In Rural Life
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational article discusses the application of the PREOXI trial protocol in rural emergency departments, highlighting that positive pressure preoxygenation before intubation significantly reduced post-intubation cardiac arrest and hypoxemia rates without increasing aspiration risk, but does not present new data beyond summarizing existing PREOXI trial findings and implementation considerations.

foamed.title: Meta-Analysis of Norepinephrine vs Epinephrine After Cardiac Arrest
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In a meta-analysis of 3,458 post-cardiac arrest patients, norepinephrine and epinephrine showed no significant difference in survival to hospital discharge or favorable neurological outcomes, but norepinephrine was associated with lower odds of recurrent cardiac arrest, particularly benefiting those with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.

foamed.title: Advances in Critical Care Medicine Insights - JournalFeed
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In cases of suspected but unidentified poisoning, clinical management should prioritize patient stabilization, recognition of toxidromes, anticipation of toxicokinetics, and ongoing reassessment rather than delaying intervention while awaiting a definitive diagnosis, according to best practice guidelines in emergency care.

foamed.title: Article dump 2026
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an informal collection of brief, non-definitive summaries of recent medical papers, without full critical appraisal or new data analysis, and readers are explicitly advised to consult the original studies themselves before making any clinical practice changes.

foamed.title: The First Five Minutes: Human Factors and the Hidden Risk at ED Triage
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational article discusses how human factors such as cognitive load and decision fatigue can impact clinical judgment and patient safety during emergency department triage, emphasizing the importance of proper training, system use, and awareness of these risks rather than presenting new clinical data.

pubmed.title: Comparison of Intensive Versus Conventional Glycemic Control Targets: An Updated Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of the 2024 Society of Critical Care Medicine Guidelines on Glycemic Control for Critically Ill Adults.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, updated meta-analysis of 45 RCTs (32,215 patients) found no mortality benefit for intensive versus conventional glycemic control in critically ill adults, but intensive targets increased severe hypoglycemia risk and only showed subgroup benefits (e.g., neurologic outcomes in neuro ICU, mortality in cardiac surgery), with many findings limited by study bias and inconsistency; thus, routine intensive glycemic control is not broadly supported and should be individualized

pubmed.title: Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of the Venous Excess Ultrasound Grading System: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis found that the VExUS grading system shows moderate to good diagnostic accuracy for detecting elevated central venous and right atrial pressures, and is prognostically associated with acute kidney injury and mortality in cardiac patients (moderate to low certainty), but not in general critically ill populations; this suggests VExUS may be most clinically useful for risk stratification in cardiac patients rather than broadly in critical care.

pubmed.title: The Impact of Centrally Acting Drug Burden at Discharge on Long-Term Outcomes Among ICU Survivors.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this substudy of three prospective cohorts involving ICU survivors, higher centrally acting drug burden at hospital discharge was not significantly associated with long-term cognitive impairment, physical disability, or 90-day mortality, suggesting that discharge drug burden may not independently impact these outcomes in this population.

pubmed.title: Continuing Vs. Withholding Home Beta-Blockers at Admission for Suspected Infection: A Target Trial Emulation.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective cohort study emulating a target trial, continuation of home beta-blockers within 48 hours of hospital admission for suspected infection was associated with significantly lower 90-day mortality and shorter hospital stays compared to withholding them, suggesting that maintaining beta-blocker therapy may improve outcomes in this population.

pubmed.title: Short-Term and Long-Term Mortality of Pulmonary Embolism Patients Admitted to the ICU in the Netherlands.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large Dutch ICU cohort study, high-risk pulmonary embolism patients had significantly higher hospital (25.4% vs 2.8%) and 1-year mortality (HR 3.98) compared to non-high-risk patients, and this elevated risk persisted after discharge, highlighting the need for ongoing vigilance and tailored management in this population.

pubmed.title: Hospital-Level Variation in Early Tracheostomy and Withdrawal of Life-Sustaining Treatment in Severe Traumatic Brain Injury: A Nationwide Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective cohort study found that hospitals with a higher tendency to perform early tracheostomy in severe traumatic brain injury patients also had increased rates of withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment post-tracheostomy, suggesting that institutional practice patterns may influence critical care decisions and outcomes; clinicians should be aware of this variability when considering early tracheostomy.

foamed.title: Catalyst is coming… 23rd July 2026
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an announcement and overview of the upcoming Catalyst: Critical Care Ideas in Action event scheduled for July 23, 2026, highlighting its focus on resuscitation, critical care performance, and interprofessional collaboration, but it does not present new clinical data or research findings.

deep_dives.title: Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of the Venous Excess Ultrasound Grading System: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis found that the VExUS grading system shows moderate to good diagnostic accuracy for detecting elevated central venous and right atrial pressures, and is prognostically associated with acute kidney injury and mortality in cardiac patients (moderate to low certainty), but not in general critically ill populations; this suggests VExUS may be most clinically useful for risk stratification in cardiac patients rather than broadly in critical care.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: The VExUS grading system shows moderate to good diagnostic accuracy for detecting increased central venous and right atrial pressures, and is prognostically valuable for AKI and mortality in cardiac patients, but its prognostic value in critically ill patients is less clear.

- Study type: Systematic review and meta-analysis
- Population/setting: 3142 patients from 32 studies, including cardiac and critically ill patients in acute and critical care settings
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: VExUS grading system assessment versus standard measures for venous congestion and outcomes
- Primary endpoints: Diagnostic accuracy for increased central venous and right atrial pressures; association of VExUS grades with acute kidney injury (AKI) and mortality
- Key results: VExUS sensitivity 78-95% and specificity 80-90% for diagnosing increased venous pressures; in cardiac patients, VExUS ≥2 associated with AKI (OR 4.44, 95% CI 2.34-8.43) and mortality (OR 3.17, 95% CI 1.30-7.75); in critically ill patients, no significant association with AKI (OR 1.45, 95% CI 0.70-2.99) or mortality (OR 1.25, 95% CI 0.77-2.03).
- Limitations:
- No meta-analysis performed for diagnostic studies due to heterogeneity.
- Certainty of evidence for prognostic outcomes in critically ill patients is low to very low.
- Potential variability in VExUS assessment and study designs.
- Why this matters: Understanding the diagnostic and prognostic utility of VExUS can guide fluid management and risk stratification, especially in cardiac patients, but caution is needed when applying findings to broader critically ill populations.

deep_dives.title: Comparison of Intensive Versus Conventional Glycemic Control Targets: An Updated Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of the 2024 Society of Critical Care Medicine Guidelines on Glycemic Control for Critically Ill Adults.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large, updated meta-analysis of 45 RCTs (32,215 patients) found no mortality benefit for intensive versus conventional glycemic control in critically ill adults, but intensive targets increased severe hypoglycemia risk and only showed subgroup benefits (e.g., neurologic outcomes in neuro ICU, mortality in cardiac surgery), with many findings limited by study bias and inconsistency; thus, routine intensive glycemic control is not broadly supported and should be individualized
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Intensive glycemic control in critically ill adults does not reduce ICU or hospital mortality compared to conventional targets but increases the risk of severe hypoglycemia and offers only mild to moderate improvements in some morbidity outcomes.

- Study type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
- Population/setting: Critically ill adult patients on insulin infusions in ICU settings; 45 RCTs with 32,215 patients
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Intensive blood glucose targets versus conventional blood glucose targets
- Primary endpoints: Hospital mortality and ICU mortality
- Key results: No difference in hospital or ICU mortality between groups; intensive targets reduced ICU length of stay, infections, and critical illness polyneuropathy but increased severe hypoglycemia risk 3.6-fold; subgroup benefits seen in neurologic and cardiac surgery ICU patients.
- Limitations:
- Serious inconsistencies or risk of bias in most studies with significant differences
- Studies before 2000 excluded, possibly limiting historical context
- Some outcomes summarized narratively due to lack of numerical data
- Why this matters: These findings support current guidelines to avoid routine use of intensive glycemic targets in critically ill adults due to increased hypoglycemia risk, except in select centers with proven safety protocols.

pubmed.title: Superficial Cervical Plexus Block and Quality of Recovery after Thyroidectomy: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, adding a bilateral superficial cervical plexus block to multimodal analgesia for thyroidectomy did not improve overall quality of recovery or most secondary outcomes, though it modestly reduced PACU opioid use and antiemetic requirements; these findings suggest limited additional clinical benefit for routine use of this block in enhanced recovery pathways.

pubmed.title: Pain Trajectories after Orthopedic Surgery in Older Adults: An Analysis of Predictors and Associations with Postoperative Outcomes.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large secondary analysis of older adults after orthopedic surgery, four distinct postoperative pain trajectories were identified, with preoperative analgesic use and dexmedetomidine in self-controlled analgesia influencing trajectory risk; these findings highlight the importance of individualized pain management to potentially improve postoperative outcomes in this population.

pubmed.title: High-Dose Intravenous Vitamin C and Mortality and Organ Dysfunction in Severe Burn Injury: The VICTORY Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter randomized trial, high-dose intravenous vitamin C did not reduce 28-day mortality or persistent organ dysfunction in adults with severe burn injury and showed a trend toward possible harm, indicating it should not be used routinely in this population.

pubmed.title: Carbocisteine or Hypertonic Saline for Acute Respiratory Failure.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter randomized trial, neither carbocisteine nor nebulized hypertonic saline reduced the duration of mechanical ventilation in critically ill patients with acute respiratory failure and difficult-to-clear secretions, and carbocisteine was associated with an increased risk of upper gastrointestinal bleeding; these findings do not support routine use of these mucoactive agents in this setting.

pubmed.title: Integrated Telehealth Rehabilitation and Quality of Life in Mechanically Ventilated Adults: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial, an integrated telehealth-based rehabilitation program for mechanically ventilated adults significantly improved health-related quality of life at 90 days post-discharge compared to usual care, suggesting that such interventions can meaningfully enhance recovery after acute respiratory failure.

pubmed.title: Hospital Policy of Tranexamic Acid to Reduce Transfusion in Major Noncardiac Surgery.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter, cluster-randomized trial, implementing a hospital policy of intraoperative tranexamic acid for major noncardiac surgery significantly reduced red-cell transfusion rates without increasing venous thromboembolism risk, providing strong evidence to support its routine use in high-risk surgical patients.

pubmed.title: Conservative Oxygen for Unresponsive Patients after Cardiac Arrest.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large randomized trial, conservative oxygen therapy did not improve survival with favorable functional outcome at 180 days compared to liberal oxygen therapy in unresponsive adults after cardiac arrest, suggesting no clinical benefit to restricting oxygen in this setting.

foamed.title: Genicular nerve block without steroids may be enough for knee osteoarthritis pain
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: A randomized clinical trial indicates that ultrasound-guided genicular nerve blocks using local anesthetic alone may provide pain relief for symptomatic knee osteoarthritis comparable to, or better than, blocks with added corticosteroids, potentially avoiding the additional risks associated with steroid use.

foamed.title: Pre-hospital acute behavioural disturbance — a guest post by Jack Lyon
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This blog post is an educational commentary discussing the challenges and management strategies for pre-hospital acute behavioural disturbance, referencing recent literature and guidelines, but does not present new clinical data or original research findings.

deep_dives.title: Carbocisteine or Hypertonic Saline for Acute Respiratory Failure.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter randomized trial, neither carbocisteine nor nebulized hypertonic saline reduced the duration of mechanical ventilation in critically ill patients with acute respiratory failure and difficult-to-clear secretions, and carbocisteine was associated with an increased risk of upper gastrointestinal bleeding; these findings do not support routine use of these mucoactive agents in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Carbocisteine and hypertonic saline did not reduce the duration of mechanical ventilation in critically ill patients with acute respiratory failure and were each associated with increased adverse events.

- Study type: Multicenter, open-label, randomized trial with a 2-by-2 factorial design
- Population/setting: Critically ill, mechanically ventilated patients aged 16 years or older with acute respiratory failure and difficult-to-clear secretions in multiple centers
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Carbocisteine (750 mg three times daily enterally), nebulized hypertonic saline (6% or 7%, 4 ml four times daily), both interventions, or usual care alone
- Primary endpoints: Duration of mechanical ventilation (from randomization to first successful unassisted breathing)
- Key results: Median duration of mechanical ventilation was similar across groups: carbocisteine (186.1 hours) vs. no carbocisteine (172.7 hours), adjusted hazard ratio 0.96 (95% CI, 0.87 to 1.05; P = 0.34); HTS (184.5 hours) vs. no HTS (174.3 hours), adjusted hazard ratio 1.00 (95% CI, 0.91 to 1.10; P = 0.98). Carbocisteine increased upper GI bleeding (1.4% vs. 0.2%, risk ratio 6.51; P = 0.01), and HTS increased bronchoconstriction (2.4% vs. 0.4%, risk ratio 5.73; P = 0.001) and hypoxemia during nebulization (4.1% vs. 0.3%, risk ratio 13.29; P<0.001).
- Limitations:
- Open-label design may introduce bias.
- Adverse events may be underreported.
- Study population limited to those with difficult-to-clear secretions.
- Why this matters: This large randomized trial provides evidence that commonly used mucoactive agents do not improve ventilation outcomes and may cause harm in critically ill patients with acute respiratory failure.

deep_dives.title: Hospital Policy of Tranexamic Acid to Reduce Transfusion in Major Noncardiac Surgery.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter, cluster-randomized trial, implementing a hospital policy of intraoperative tranexamic acid for major noncardiac surgery significantly reduced red-cell transfusion rates without increasing venous thromboembolism risk, providing strong evidence to support its routine use in high-risk surgical patients.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: A hospital policy of intraoperative tranexamic acid administration in major noncardiac surgery reduced red-cell transfusion rates without increasing venous thromboembolism risk.

- Study type: Multicenter, double-blind, cluster-randomized, placebo-controlled trial
- Population/setting: 8273 patients at high risk for red-cell transfusion undergoing major noncardiac surgery across 10 Canadian hospitals
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Hospital-wide policy of intraoperative tranexamic acid administration versus placebo
- Primary endpoints: Transfusion of red cells during index hospitalization (effectiveness) and diagnosis of venous thromboembolism within 90 days (safety)
- Key results: Red-cell transfusion occurred in 7.4% of the tranexamic acid group vs. 9.8% of the placebo group (RR 0.73, 95% CI 0.61–0.86); venous thromboembolism occurred in 2.1% of both groups (RR 0.96, 95% CI 0.65–1.38), meeting the noninferiority criterion.
- Limitations:
- Study conducted only in Canadian hospitals
- Majority of surgeries were oncologic, which may limit generalizability
- Why this matters: This study supports the safety and effectiveness of a hospital-wide tranexamic acid policy to reduce transfusion needs in major noncardiac surgery without increasing thromboembolic risk.

deep_dives.title: Superficial Cervical Plexus Block and Quality of Recovery after Thyroidectomy: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, adding a bilateral superficial cervical plexus block to multimodal analgesia for thyroidectomy did not improve overall quality of recovery or most secondary outcomes, though it modestly reduced PACU opioid use and antiemetic requirements; these findings suggest limited additional clinical benefit for routine use of this block in enhanced recovery pathways.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** In this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, adding a bilateral superficial cervical plexus block to multimodal analgesia for thyroidectomy did not improve overall quality of recovery or most secondary outcomes, though it modestly reduced PACU opioid use and antiemetic requirements; these findings suggest limited additional clinical benefit for routine use of this block in enhanced recovery pathways.

BOTTOM LINE: Adding bilateral superficial cervical plexus block to a multimodal analgesic regimen for thyroidectomy did not improve postoperative quality of recovery but reduced PACU opioid use and antiemetic requirements.

- Study type: Prospective, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial
- Population/setting: 160 adults undergoing thyroidectomy with general anesthesia at a single center
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Bilateral superficial cervical plexus block with 0.25% bupivacaine versus saline placebo
- Primary endpoints: Quality of recovery measured by the QoR-40 survey on postoperative day 1
- Key results: Mean QoR-40 scores on POD 1 were similar between groups (bupivacaine: 174, saline: 173; adjusted mean difference 0.91, 95% CI -3.57 to 5.40; p=0.688); PACU opioid use was lower in the bupivacaine group (median 0 [0,8] vs. 2 [0,20]; p=0.017), and fewer required rescue antiemetics (3.8% vs. 16%; p=0.011).
- Limitations:
- Single-center design limits generalizability
- All participants received multimodal analgesia, potentially masking additional effects of the block
- Why this matters: This study suggests that in the context of modern multimodal analgesia, adding a superficial cervical plexus block may not further improve recovery quality after thyroidectomy but can reduce opioid and antiemetic use in the immediate postoperative period.

deep_dives.title: High-Dose Intravenous Vitamin C and Mortality and Organ Dysfunction in Severe Burn Injury: The VICTORY Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter randomized trial, high-dose intravenous vitamin C did not reduce 28-day mortality or persistent organ dysfunction in adults with severe burn injury and showed a trend toward possible harm, indicating it should not be used routinely in this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: High-dose intravenous vitamin C did not reduce 28-day mortality or persistent organ dysfunction in severe burn patients and may be harmful.

- Study type: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 3 trial
- Population/setting: Adults (≥18 years) with deep second- and/or third-degree burns covering ≥20% total body surface area requiring skin grafting, across 24 burn centers in North, Central, and South America; Europe; and Asia.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: High-dose intravenous vitamin C (50 mg/kg every 6 hours for 96 hours) versus matched placebo.
- Primary endpoints: Composite of 28-day mortality and persistent organ dysfunction (dependence on mechanical ventilation, kidney replacement therapy, or vasopressor/inotrope support at day 28).
- Key results: The primary composite outcome occurred in 40.8% of the vitamin C group vs 29.7% of placebo (adjusted RR 1.28, 95% CI 0.99-1.65; P = .06). Twenty-eight-day mortality was higher with vitamin C (15.0% vs 7.6%; adjusted RR 1.96, 95% CI 1.32-2.90; P = .001), and hospital mortality was also higher (23.3% vs 16.1%; adjusted RR 1.44, 95% CI 1.03-2.00; P = .03).
- Limitations:
- Trial was stopped early after interim analysis for futility/harm.
- Sample size smaller than initially planned due to early termination.
- Generalizability may be limited to similar burn populations.
