- Why this matters: This study provides strong evidence against the use of high-dose intravenous vitamin C in severe burn patients, as it does not improve outcomes and may increase mortality.

pubmed.title: Coagulopathy in obstetric bleeding: a narrative review and comparison with trauma-induced coagulopathy.
pubmed.bottom_line: This narrative review highlights that coagulopathy in postpartum hemorrhage has distinct pathophysiology—particularly acute obstetric coagulopathy involving pathological fibrinogen destruction—compared to trauma-induced coagulopathy, suggesting that current resuscitation guidelines based on trauma data may not optimally address the unique needs of obstetric patients. Clinicians should be aware of these differences to improve management and outcomes in obstetric bleeding.

pubmed.title: Clinical Prediction Models for Prognostication After Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 39 cohorts (95,037 patients) found that the CAHP and OHCA scores have moderate sensitivity and specificity for predicting poor functional outcomes after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, with the CAHP score supported by moderate certainty evidence; however, overall certainty is limited, highlighting the need for cautious clinical use and further validation.

pubmed.title: Dreaming during anaesthesia: a scoping review.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large scoping review finds that dreaming during anaesthesia is common, especially with ketamine and propofol, is usually pleasant, and is not a marker of intraoperative awareness; however, evidence is limited by inconsistent definitions and assessment methods, and more research is needed to clarify clinical significance and mechanisms.

pubmed.title: Gabapentin and Opioid Prescribing Trends among Adolescents Undergoing Common Procedures: A National Retrospective Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective cohort study found that while opioid-only prescriptions for adolescents after common surgeries declined from 2012 to 2022, gabapentin prescribing (alone or with opioids) increased, and adolescents who received both gabapentin and opioids were significantly more likely to obtain opioid refills, raising concerns about the effectiveness and safety of gabapentin as an opioid-sparing strategy in this population.

pubmed.title: Antidotes for Anticoagulation Reversal.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: This review highlights that while specific antidotes exist for some anticoagulants (e.g., idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitors), evidence gaps and practical challenges—such as optimal dosing, monitoring, and safety—limit their use, underscoring the need for stronger clinical data and updated guidelines to improve anticoagulation reversal in bleeding emergencies.

pubmed.title: Sodium Bicarbonate for In-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, intravenous sodium bicarbonate administered during in-hospital cardiac arrest did not significantly improve rates of sustained return of spontaneous circulation or 30-day survival compared to placebo, indicating no clinical benefit for routine use in this setting.

pubmed.title: IVUS-Guided versus Angiography-Guided PCI in Unprotected Left Main Coronary Disease.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized multicenter trial, IVUS-guided PCI did not improve clinical outcomes compared to angiography-guided PCI for unprotected left main coronary artery disease over a median 2.9-year follow-up, suggesting no added benefit of IVUS guidance in this setting.

pubmed.title: Subretinal Gene Therapy for X-Linked Retinoschisis.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this small phase 1/2 trial, subretinal AAV8-RS1 gene therapy for X-linked retinoschisis in children and adolescents was safe over 52 weeks, led to significant anatomical improvements and modest visual acuity gains, suggesting potential clinical benefit but requiring further study to confirm efficacy.

pubmed.title: Exa-cel in Children with Transfusion-Dependent β-Thalassemia or Sickle Cell Disease.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this phase 3, open-label study, exa-cel gene editing therapy resulted in transfusion independence in all evaluable children with transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia and elimination of vaso-occlusive crises in all evaluable children with sickle cell disease over a median 16–17 months of follow-up, though all experienced at least one serious adverse event; these findings suggest exa-cel may offer a transformative, though not

foamed.title: Update on total intravenous anesthesia in children
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This 2026 review summarizes recent advances in pediatric total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA), including improved pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic models, target-controlled infusion technology, EEG-guided dosing, and new agents like remimazolam, highlighting TIVA’s potential to reduce emergence delirium, postoperative nausea and vomiting, respiratory complications, and environmental impact compared to inhalational anesthesia.

foamed.title: Foreign body pulmonary embolisation
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This case report describes migration of a subdermal contraceptive implant (Implanon NXT) from the upper arm to the right pulmonary artery, presenting as loss of implant palpability and irregular bleeding, with diagnosis confirmed by imaging and successful endovascular retrieval; clinicians should consider migration in patients with non-palpable implants.

foamed.title: What does a good emergency physician look like?
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational commentary reflecting on the diverse qualities that define an excellent emergency physician, emphasizing that outstanding clinicians may excel in different domains such as diagnosis, procedures, communication, or situational awareness, but does not present new research data or study results.

foamed.title: The Performance Art of Medicine
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This blog post is an educational commentary discussing the concept of medicine as a performance art, emphasizing the value of incorporating performance skills into medical education to foster empathy, adaptability, and resilience in clinicians, but it does not present new clinical data or original research findings.

deep_dives.title: IVUS-Guided versus Angiography-Guided PCI in Unprotected Left Main Coronary Disease.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized multicenter trial, IVUS-guided PCI did not improve clinical outcomes compared to angiography-guided PCI for unprotected left main coronary artery disease over a median 2.9-year follow-up, suggesting no added benefit of IVUS guidance in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: IVUS-guided PCI did not provide additional benefit over angiography-guided PCI in patients with unprotected left main coronary artery disease regarding major clinical outcomes at a median follow-up of 2.9 years.

- Study type: International, multicenter, open-label, randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: 806 patients with unprotected left main coronary artery disease from multiple international centers
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: IVUS-guided PCI versus angiography-guided PCI
- Primary endpoints: Composite of any stroke, any myocardial infarction, any revascularization, or death from any cause at the longest follow-up
- Key results: Primary endpoint occurred in 33.7% of the IVUS group and 30.9% of the angiography group (hazard ratio, 1.11; 95% CI, 0.87 to 1.42; P = 0.40); incidence of death, myocardial infarction, or revascularization and safety events were similar between groups.
- Limitations:
- Open-label design may introduce bias
- Median follow-up of 2.9 years may not capture long-term differences
- Study powered for composite endpoint, not individual components
- Why this matters: This study suggests that routine use of IVUS guidance during PCI for unprotected left main coronary artery disease may not improve major clinical outcomes compared to standard angiographic guidance.

deep_dives.title: Sodium Bicarbonate for In-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, intravenous sodium bicarbonate administered during in-hospital cardiac arrest did not significantly improve rates of sustained return of spontaneous circulation or 30-day survival compared to placebo, indicating no clinical benefit for routine use in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Sodium bicarbonate did not significantly improve sustained return of spontaneous circulation compared to placebo in adults with in-hospital cardiac arrest.

- Study type: Randomized, parallel-group, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial
- Population/setting: Adults with in-hospital cardiac arrest at 21 hospitals in Denmark who received at least one dose of epinephrine
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate (up to 100 mmol) versus placebo
- Primary endpoints: Sustained return of spontaneous circulation
- Key results: Sustained return of spontaneous circulation occurred in 39% of the sodium bicarbonate group and 37% of the placebo group (risk ratio 1.05, 95% CI 0.88-1.24; P = .62); 30-day survival was 12% vs 9.1% (risk ratio 1.25, 95% CI 0.84-1.88), and favorable neurologic outcome at 30 days was 8.1% vs 5.4% (risk ratio 1.39, 95% CI 0.82-2.34); alkalosis and hypernatremia were more common with sodium bicarbonate.
- Limitations:
- Single-country study may limit generalizability
- Only patients who received at least one dose of epinephrine were included
- Potential for unmeasured confounding factors
- Why this matters: This study provides evidence against the routine use of sodium bicarbonate during in-hospital cardiac arrest, informing clinical guidelines and practice.

pubmed.title: Electroencephalographic Monitoring in the Recovery Room for Identification of Patients at Risk for Postoperative Delirium.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective observational study found that reduced alpha- and beta-band EEG power in the recovery room is associated with increased risk of postoperative delirium in older adults, suggesting that simple EEG monitoring postoperatively may help identify patients at risk; however, the moderate discriminatory power (AUC 0.69) indicates further validation is needed before routine clinical adoption.

foamed.title: Current controversies in pediatric regional anesthesia
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This 2026 review discusses ongoing controversies in pediatric regional anesthesia, focusing on issues such as optimal local anesthetic dosing, toxicity risks, nerve catheter safety, enhanced recovery protocols, compartment syndrome, and the use of awake techniques, but does not present new clinical data or outcomes.

foamed.title: AI in Cardiac CT
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article provides an educational overview of the integration, benefits, and challenges of artificial intelligence in cardiac CT imaging, highlighting current applications, workflow implications, and clinician responsibilities, but does not present new clinical data or original research findings.

pubmed.title: Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events After Hip Fracture Surgery with Remimazolam or Propofol Total Intravenous Anesthesia: A Retrospective Cohort Study Emulating a Target Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective cohort study emulating a target trial, remimazolam did not reduce 30-day major adverse cardiovascular events or in-hospital mortality compared to propofol in older adults undergoing hip fracture surgery, suggesting no clinical advantage of remimazolam over propofol for these outcomes in this population.

pubmed.title: Effect of Pericapsular Nerve Group Block on Prehabilitation in Femoral Neck Fractures: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of adults with femoral neck fractures, the pericapsular nerve group (PENG) block reduced movement-related pain and the need for rescue analgesia during prehabilitation but did not increase completion rates of mobilization steps or improve postoperative outcomes, suggesting its main benefit is improved analgesia rather than enhanced functional prehabilitation.

pubmed.title: What Is New in Postoperative Nausea and Vomiting? Promise and Paradox in the Fifth Consensus Guidelines!
pubmed.bottom_line: The fifth consensus guidelines on postoperative nausea and vomiting introduce updated recommendations and highlight ongoing challenges, offering promise for improved management but also underscoring persistent gaps in evidence and implementation that are clinically relevant for optimizing patient outcomes.

pubmed.title: Accuracy and precision of continuous, non-invasive blood pressure devices: a systematic review and meta-analyses.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large systematic review and meta-analysis found that most continuous non-invasive blood pressure devices do not meet established accuracy and precision standards compared to invasive measurements, with only select applanation tonometry and photoplethysmography devices meeting criteria for both bias and standard deviation; clinicians should be cautious when relying on these technologies for perioperative blood pressure management.

pubmed.title: Determinants of Delayed Recovery of Consciousness After Analgosedation Discontinuation in the ICU: Insights From Patients With COVID-19 Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective cohort of critically ill COVID-19 patients, over one-third of those who regained consciousness did so later than expected based on sedative pharmacokinetics, with older age, hypoxemia, and acute kidney injury associated with delayed recovery; this suggests that factors beyond sedative clearance significantly contribute to prolonged unconsciousness, highlighting the need for broader clinical assessment in ICU sedation management.

foamed.title: Psychological Safety After Paediatric Cardiac Arrest
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational commentary highlighting the often-overlooked psychological impact and emotional burden experienced by paediatric emergency staff, particularly nurses, following paediatric cardiac arrest and death, emphasizing the importance of psychological safety and support during the post-resuscitation phase; it does not present new clinical study data.

foamed.title: Essential Updates from POCUS Journals - JournalFeed
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In a retrospective study of acute myocardial infarction patients, the presence of regional wall motion abnormalities detected by bedside echocardiography was associated with higher rates of coronary occlusions, suggesting that point-of-care ultrasound can aid in identifying occlusion MI in the emergency department setting.

foamed.title: Will whole blood data end up wholey negative?
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is a commentary reviewing recent evidence, including the SwiFT and TOWAR trials, on the use of whole blood versus blood components in prehospital trauma resuscitation, but does not present new primary data and primarily discusses skepticism regarding the clinical benefit of whole blood based on existing studies.

deep_dives.title: Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events After Hip Fracture Surgery with Remimazolam or Propofol Total Intravenous Anesthesia: A Retrospective Cohort Study Emulating a Target Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large retrospective cohort study emulating a target trial, remimazolam did not reduce 30-day major adverse cardiovascular events or in-hospital mortality compared to propofol in older adults undergoing hip fracture surgery, suggesting no clinical advantage of remimazolam over propofol for these outcomes in this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Remimazolam and propofol total intravenous anesthesia resulted in similar rates of 30-day in-hospital major adverse cardiovascular events and mortality after hip fracture surgery in older adults.

- Study type: Retrospective cohort study emulating a target trial
- Population/setting: Adults aged 65 to 99 years undergoing hip fracture surgery with total intravenous anesthesia at multiple Japanese hospitals (N=14,027 analyzed) between September 2020 and September 2024
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Remimazolam versus propofol total intravenous anesthesia
- Primary endpoints: 30-day in-hospital major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE)
- Key results: MACE occurred in 2.86% of the remimazolam group and 2.74% of the propofol group (risk ratio 1.04, 95% CI 0.80-1.38; P=0.76); 30-day all-cause in-hospital mortality was also similar (risk ratio 1.03, 95% CI 0.61-1.80; P=0.91).
- Limitations:
- Retrospective observational design
- Potential for residual confounding despite propensity score weighting
- Administrative database may lack detailed clinical variables
- Why this matters: These findings suggest that the choice between remimazolam and propofol for anesthesia in older adults undergoing hip fracture surgery may not impact short-term cardiovascular outcomes, informing anesthetic selection in this high-risk population.

deep_dives.title: Effect of Pericapsular Nerve Group Block on Prehabilitation in Femoral Neck Fractures: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of adults with femoral neck fractures, the pericapsular nerve group (PENG) block reduced movement-related pain and the need for rescue analgesia during prehabilitation but did not increase completion rates of mobilization steps or improve postoperative outcomes, suggesting its main benefit is improved analgesia rather than enhanced functional prehabilitation.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** In this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of adults with femoral neck fractures, the pericapsular nerve group (PENG) block reduced movement-related pain and the need for rescue analgesia during prehabilitation but did not increase completion rates of mobilization steps or improve postoperative outcomes, suggesting its main benefit is improved analgesia rather than enhanced functional prehabilitation.

BOTTOM LINE: PENG block before prehabilitation in femoral neck fracture patients reduced movement-related pain and rescue analgesic use but did not improve prehabilitation completion or postoperative functional recovery.

- Study type: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial
- Population/setting: Adults with Garden III to IV femoral neck fractures awaiting hip arthroplasty at a single center (N=100)
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Ultrasound-guided PENG block with 20 mL 0.375% ropivacaine versus saline placebo
- Primary endpoints: Completion of all five predefined mobilization steps during the initial prehabilitation session
- Key results: Prehabilitation completion rates were similar between groups (44% PENG vs 48% placebo, P=.841); PENG block reduced maximum movement pain (median 7 vs 8, P=.022) and rescue analgesia use (4% vs 20%, P=.028); no differences in postoperative outcomes or adverse events.
- Limitations:
- Single-center study
- Limited to initial prehabilitation session
- No long-term follow-up reported
- Why this matters: Effective pain control alone may not be sufficient to enhance prehabilitation participation or postoperative recovery in femoral neck fracture patients, highlighting the need for more comprehensive analgesic strategies.

pubmed.title: Early high-dose vitamin C for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: the VITaCCA randomized clinical trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this multicenter, double-blind phase 2 RCT, early high-dose (10 g) intravenous vitamin C after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest did not reduce organ dysfunction at 96 hours and was associated with worse organ function, higher troponin release, and poorer neurological outcomes, indicating that high-dose vitamin C may be harmful in this setting.

pubmed.title: Current knowledge and challenges of sepsis-associated encephalopathy.
pubmed.bottom_line: Sepsis-associated encephalopathy (SAE) is common, increases mortality and long-term cognitive disability, and currently lacks targeted therapies; this review highlights the need for better pathophysiological understanding and biomarker development to enable specific treatments, emphasizing a significant unmet clinical need.

pubmed.title: Concordance Between Bedside and Electronic Detection of Intracranial Pressure Crises: Insights From the Brain Tissue Oxygen Monitoring and Management in Severe Traumatic Brain Injury II Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this post hoc analysis of the BOOST II trial, less than half of bedside-identified intracranial pressure (ICP) crises in severe TBI patients were confirmed by continuous electronic ICP data, suggesting significant discrepancies that could impact clinical decision-making and research outcomes; the evidence is moderate and highlights the need for improved ICP crisis detection methods.

pubmed.title: Preoperative Blood Augmentation in Jehovah's Witness Patients Undergoing Transfusion-Free Surgery for Gastrointestinal or Hepato-biliary-pancreatic Cancer: Retrospective Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this retrospective cohort study of Jehovah's Witness patients undergoing transfusion-free surgery for gastrointestinal or hepato-biliary-pancreatic cancer, a preoperative blood augmentation protocol (iron, vitamins, erythropoiesis-stimulating agent) in anemic patients resulted in comparable 30-day postoperative mortality and complication rates to non-anemic patients, suggesting this protocol is a clinically effective strategy for managing preoperative anemia when transfusion is not an option.

pubmed.title: Diagnostic performance of package insert-based screening for potential drug-drug interactions in ICU patients receiving injectable medications: a retrospective diagnostic accuracy study using Lexi-Interact as the comparator in Japan.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this retrospective cohort study of ICU patients in Japan, package insert-based screening for potential drug-drug interactions showed high negative predictive value (0.98) but only moderate sensitivity (0.80) and low positive predictive value (0.51) compared to Lexi-Interact, suggesting that while package inserts are reliable for ruling out interactions, they may miss or underreport clinically significant interactions, highlighting the need for more comprehensive tools in critical care settings

pubmed.title: Impact of Molecular Adsorbent Recirculating System Therapy on Piperacillin Concentration in Critically Ill Patients: An Observational Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this retrospective cohort study of critically ill patients receiving MARS therapy, piperacillin plasma concentrations dropped by an average of 34% per session, with nearly one-third of post-session levels falling below therapeutic thresholds, indicating that MARS significantly reduces piperacillin exposure and may necessitate dose adjustments to maintain efficacy.

pubmed.title: Butyrylcholinesterase activity and prolonged duration of action of mivacurium in elderly patients (≥80 years): A secondary analysis of a clinical trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This secondary analysis found that elderly patients (≥80 years) have reduced butyrylcholinesterase activity, leading to a prolonged duration of action of mivacurium; clinicians should be aware of this when dosing mivacurium in this population to avoid prolonged neuromuscular blockade.

pubmed.title: Association of Preoperative Cerebrospinal Fluid Tumor Necrosis Factor-Related Apoptosis-Inducing Ligand With 1-Year Postsurgical Pain Severity in Older Adults: A Trial Secondary Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This secondary analysis found that higher preoperative cerebrospinal fluid levels of TNF-related apoptosis-inducing ligand were associated with greater pain severity one year after surgery in older adults, suggesting a potential biomarker for long-term postsurgical pain risk. This finding may help identify patients at higher risk for chronic postsurgical pain, though further validation is needed.

foamed.title: ECG Cases 62 – ACLS Arrhythmia Pitfalls, Part 5: Stable Narrow Complex Tachycardias
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational blog reviews clinical pitfalls and key considerations in diagnosing and managing stable narrow complex tachycardias, focusing on differentiating primary from secondary causes and determining appropriate responses to ACLS algorithms, but does not present new clinical study data.

deep_dives.title: Concordance Between Bedside and Electronic Detection of Intracranial Pressure Crises: Insights From the Brain Tissue Oxygen Monitoring and Management in Severe Traumatic Brain Injury II Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this post hoc analysis of the BOOST II trial, less than half of bedside-identified intracranial pressure (ICP) crises in severe TBI patients were confirmed by continuous electronic ICP data, suggesting significant discrepancies that could impact clinical decision-making and research outcomes; the evidence is moderate and highlights the need for improved ICP crisis detection methods.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Clinical identification of bedside intracranial pressure (B-ICP) episodes in severe TBI patients often does not align with electronically recorded ICP data, suggesting a need for improved, synchronized electronic detection methods.

- Study type: Post hoc comparison study using data from a randomized trial
- Population/setting: Seventy severe TBI patients with complete datasets from ten U.S. ICUs enrolled in the BOOST II trial
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Bedside clinician-identified ICP episodes versus background-collected electronic ICP data
- Primary endpoints: Concordance between bedside-identified ICP crises and electronically recorded ICP episodes
- Key results: Of 509 minimalist B-ICP episodes identified by clinicians, only 47% were confirmed by electronic ICP data; 38% of concordant episodes and 76% of discordant episodes had average electronic ICP values below 20 mm Hg.
- Limitations:
- Lack of a rigid end-of-B-ICP episode definition
- Inability to fully control for temporal synchrony confounding
- Insufficient data to evaluate temporal asynchrony between clinical and electronic data points
- Why this matters: Accurate identification of ICP crises is crucial for TBI management and research; reliance on bedside detection alone may lead to misclassification, highlighting the need for standardized, real-time electronic monitoring.

deep_dives.title: Early high-dose vitamin C for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: the VITaCCA randomized clinical trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this multicenter, double-blind phase 2 RCT, early high-dose (10 g) intravenous vitamin C after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest did not reduce organ dysfunction at 96 hours and was associated with worse organ function, higher troponin release, and poorer neurological outcomes, indicating that high-dose vitamin C may be harmful in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** In this multicenter, double-blind phase 2 RCT, early high-dose (10 g) intravenous vitamin C after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest did not reduce organ dysfunction at 96 hours and was associated with worse organ function, higher troponin release, and poorer neurological outcomes, indicating that high-dose vitamin C may be harmful in this setting.

BOTTOM LINE: Early intravenous vitamin C, especially at a high dose (10 g/day), did not reduce organ dysfunction after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest and may worsen outcomes, including organ function, renal injury, and neurological status.

- Study type: Double-blind, multicenter, randomized, placebo-controlled, phase 2 trial
- Population/setting: Comatose adults (≥18 years) resuscitated from shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest at six hospitals in the Netherlands
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Intravenous vitamin C (3 g/day or 10 g/day for 96 hours) versus placebo, all groups received intravenous thiamine
- Primary endpoints: Change in resuscitation-sequential organ failure assessment (R-SOFA) score at 96 hours
- Key results: Mean change in R-SOFA at 96 h was -3.2 (placebo), -2.3 (3 g), and -0.8 (10 g); 10 g vitamin C resulted in 2.5 points less improvement than placebo (95% CI 0.5–4.5; p=0.01). The 10 g group had higher troponin T, more need for renal replacement therapy (OR 10.4, 95% CI 1.2–87.0), worse neurological outcomes (CPC at day 28 OR 0.4, 95% CI 0.2–0.9), and higher 96 h mortality (19% vs 9% for placebo; OR 2.7, 95% CI 1.1–6.8).
- Limitations:
- Study powered for organ dysfunction, not mortality or rare adverse events.
- Exclusion of patients without deferred consent may introduce bias.
- Findings may not generalize to non-shockable rhythms or non-comatose patients.
- Potential baseline differences in disease severity despite randomization.
- Why this matters: This study raises safety concerns about high-dose vitamin C in post-cardiac arrest care and challenges current recommendations for routine vitamin C supplementation in this setting.

pubmed.title: Intraoperative Hemodynamic Management during Liver Transplantation and Postoperative Morbidity: A Multicenter Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large multicenter cohort study of 852 liver transplant recipients, neither intraoperative fluid balance nor vasopressor dose was significantly associated with early allograft dysfunction or primary graft nonfunction at 7 days, suggesting that variations in these hemodynamic management strategies may not impact short-term graft outcomes. This finding provides moderate-strength evidence to inform intraoperative management decisions during liver transplantation.

pubmed.title: Large Language Models Provide Accurate but Potentially Unsafe Answers to Multimodal Critical Care Medicine Board Review Questions.
pubmed.bottom_line: ChatGPT-4o performed slightly better than clinicians on multimodal critical care board questions (74.9% vs. 71.1% correct), but a third of its answers carried potential for clinical harm, especially due to errors in image interpretation and treatment advice, highlighting that despite strong overall accuracy, current large language models are not yet safe for unsupervised clinical decision-making.

pubmed.title: Effect of Weight-based versus Absolute Norepinephrine Dosing on Mortality Risk in Obese Patients with Septic Shock: An Observational, Multicohort, Retrospective Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective multicenter study, weight-based norepinephrine dosing in obese septic shock patients was associated with significantly higher predicted mortality compared to non-obese patients, suggesting that absolute dosing may provide a more accurate assessment of severity and guide therapy more appropriately in this population. This finding is clinically important for optimizing vasopressor management and mortality risk estimation in obese patients with septic shock.

pubmed.title: The analgesic efficacy of intrathecal morphine compared to peripheral regional analgesia in total hip arthroplasty: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This moderate- to low-quality meta-analysis of eight RCTs (471 patients) found that intrathecal morphine and peripheral regional analgesia provide similar pain relief at 24 hours after total hip arthroplasty, but intrathecal morphine reduces opioid consumption and early pain scores while increasing pruritus; these findings can guide clinicians in tailoring postoperative analgesia based on patient risk and preference.

pubmed.title: Prehospital Resuscitation with Type O Whole Blood for Trauma and Hemorrhage.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter, cluster-randomized trial, prehospital transfusion with type O whole blood did not reduce 30-day mortality compared to blood component therapy in trauma patients with hemorrhagic shock, and storage age of whole blood had no impact on outcomes, suggesting no clinical advantage of whole blood over components in this setting.

pubmed.title: Towards Conversational AI for Disease Management.
pubmed.bottom_line: In a randomized, blinded virtual OSCE study, the AMIE conversational AI system demonstrated non-inferior and sometimes superior disease management reasoning compared to primary care physicians, particularly in guideline adherence and medication reasoning, suggesting that advanced LLMs could become valuable tools in clinical disease management, though real-world validation is still needed.

pubmed.title: Towards autonomous medical artificial intelligence agents.
pubmed.bottom_line: In simulated real patient cases, the autonomous AI agent MIRA, integrated with the EHR, outperformed physicians in diagnostic accuracy and made safe, guideline-concordant clinical decisions, suggesting strong potential for AI as an effective decision-support partner; however, real-world validation is still needed to confirm safety and generalizability.

pubmed.title: Prone Positioning in Infants With Acute Bronchiolitis: The PROPOSITIS Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large multicenter randomized trial, prone positioning in infants with moderate to severe acute bronchiolitis on high-flow nasal cannula did not reduce the need for escalation to noninvasive or invasive ventilation compared to supine positioning, indicating no clinical benefit for routine prone positioning in this setting.

pubmed.title: Cefazolin for Methicillin-Susceptible Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, international randomized trial, cefazolin was noninferior to antistaphylococcal penicillins for 90-day mortality in methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia and was associated with a lower risk of acute kidney injury, supporting cefazolin as a preferred treatment option.

pubmed.title: Endovascular Therapy for Post-Thrombotic Syndrome - A Randomized Trial.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized trial, endovascular therapy for patients with moderate or severe post-thrombotic syndrome and iliac-vein obstruction significantly reduced disease severity and improved quality of life at 6 months compared to standard care alone, though bleeding risk was increased; these findings provide strong evidence supporting endovascular intervention in selected patients with post-thrombotic syndrome.

pubmed.title: Management of Differentiated Thyroid Cancer.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: This review highlights a modern, risk-adapted, and personalized approach to differentiated thyroid cancer management, emphasizing dynamic risk stratification—including molecular profiling—and shared decision-making to tailor surveillance and treatment intensity; while not presenting new primary evidence, it provides a clinically relevant framework for optimizing patient-centered care.

pubmed.title: Comparison of intrathecal morphine versus thoracic paravertebral block for video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery: a randomized non-inferiority trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized non-inferiority trial, intrathecal morphine did not achieve non-inferior analgesic efficacy compared to thoracic paravertebral block for patients undergoing video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery, with higher opioid consumption and more frequent rescue analgesia in the ITM group; these findings support continued preference for TPVB as the regional technique of choice for postoperative pain control in this setting.

foamed.title: To squeeze or not to squeeze. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article provides commentary and initial impressions on the ARISE-FLUIDS trial, which investigated early vasopressors with restricted intravenous fluids for sepsis, but does not present new primary data or detailed results; readers are encouraged to review the full trial publication for definitive findings.

foamed.title: Low back pain remains the world’s leading cause of disability
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: A major new review highlights that low back pain remains the leading global cause of disability, affecting approximately 619 million people in 2020, with most cases being nonspecific and not attributable to serious underlying disease, and emphasizes the importance of updated epidemiology, diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment insights for clinicians and patients.

foamed.title: EMCrit Wee – Phil and Rory on the 4 Interface Model for Shock Physiology
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This content is an educational discussion between Phil Rola and Rory Spiegel on the 4 Interface Model for shock physiology and does not present new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.title: Must-Read Emergency Department Articles Today - JournalFeed
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: The Pediatric Sepsis Event (PSE) monitoring algorithm was developed and validated for EHR-based surveillance, identifying a pediatric sepsis incidence of 1.3% among approximately 3.9 million pediatric hospital admissions or emergency department deaths, according to national estimates published in JAMA.

foamed.title: Beyond postdural puncture headache
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational review highlights that while postpartum headaches are common and often benign, clinicians must remain vigilant for rare but potentially life-threatening secondary causes—such as cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome, and others—that may mimic postdural puncture headache, as delayed diagnosis is frequent due to overlapping postpartum symptoms.

foamed.title: Age-Adjusted D-Dimer Cutoff Levels to Rule Out Deep Vein Thrombosis
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In a multicenter prospective study, using an age-adjusted D-dimer cutoff safely ruled out lower extremity proximal DVT in emergency department patients with low pretest probability, with a 3-month VTE rate of 0% (95% CI, 0.0%–2.3%) among those with D-dimer values between 500 and their age-adjusted cutoff.

deep_dives.title: Cefazolin for Methicillin-Susceptible Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, international randomized trial, cefazolin was noninferior to antistaphylococcal penicillins for 90-day mortality in methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia and was associated with a lower risk of acute kidney injury, supporting cefazolin as a preferred treatment option.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Cefazolin is noninferior to antistaphylococcal penicillins for 90-day mortality in methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia and is associated with less acute kidney injury.

- Study type: Open-label, randomized, international Bayesian adaptive platform trial
- Population/setting: Adult patients with penicillin-resistant, methicillin-susceptible S. aureus bacteremia enrolled internationally between February 2022 and August 2024
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Cefazolin versus antistaphylococcal penicillin (flucloxacillin or cloxacillin)
- Primary endpoints: Death from any cause within 90 days after enrollment
- Key results: 90-day mortality was 15.0% in the cefazolin group and 17.0% in the antistaphylococcal-penicillin group (adjusted odds ratio, 0.81; 95% credible interval, 0.59 to 1.12; probability of noninferiority, 99.2%; probability of superiority, 89.8%). Acute kidney injury occurred in 13.9% with cefazolin versus 19.6% with antistaphylococcal penicillins (adjusted odds ratio, 0.67; 95% credible interval, 0.50 to 0.89; probability of superiority, 99.7%).
- Limitations:
- Open-label design may introduce bias.
- Ongoing trial status may limit long-term follow-up data.
- Generalizability may be limited to similar patient populations.
- Why this matters: This study supports cefazolin as a safe and effective alternative to antistaphylococcal penicillins for treating methicillin-susceptible S. aureus bacteremia, with a lower risk of acute kidney injury.

deep_dives.title: Prehospital Resuscitation with Type O Whole Blood for Trauma and Hemorrhage.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter, cluster-randomized trial, prehospital transfusion with type O whole blood did not reduce 30-day mortality compared to blood component therapy in trauma patients with hemorrhagic shock, and storage age of whole blood had no impact on outcomes, suggesting no clinical advantage of whole blood over components in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Prehospital transfusion with whole blood did not reduce 30-day mortality compared to blood components in trauma patients with hemorrhagic shock.

- Study type: Pragmatic, multicenter, phase 3, cluster-randomized trial with an observational substudy.
- Population/setting: Trauma patients with hemorrhagic shock transported by 44 air medical bases.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Prehospital transfusion with up to 2 units of whole blood versus as-indicated blood components (plasma, red cells, or both).
- Primary endpoints: Death from any cause within 30 days after randomization.
- Key results: 30-day mortality was 25.9% in the whole-blood group and 20.5% in the component group (adjusted odds ratio 1.24, 95% CI 0.87–1.76; P=0.24); no substantial differences in adverse events; storage age of whole blood did not affect mortality.
- Limitations:
- Cluster-randomized design may introduce confounding.
- Study limited to air medical transport settings.
- Potential for unmeasured variables affecting outcomes.
- Why this matters: This study informs prehospital transfusion protocols by showing no mortality benefit of whole blood over blood components for trauma patients with hemorrhagic shock.

deep_dives.title: Endovascular Therapy for Post-Thrombotic Syndrome - A Randomized Trial.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized trial, endovascular therapy for patients with moderate or severe post-thrombotic syndrome and iliac-vein obstruction significantly reduced disease severity and improved quality of life at 6 months compared to standard care alone, though bleeding risk was increased; these findings provide strong evidence supporting endovascular intervention in selected patients with post-thrombotic syndrome.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Endovascular therapy for patients with moderate or severe post-thrombotic syndrome and iliac-vein obstruction reduced syndrome severity and improved quality of life at 6 months, but increased bleeding risk.

- Study type: Randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: 225 patients with moderate or severe post-thrombotic syndrome and imaging-confirmed iliac-vein obstruction
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Endovascular therapy (iliac-vein stent placement and enhanced antithrombotic therapy) plus standard care vs. standard post-thrombotic syndrome care alone
- Primary endpoints: Severity of post-thrombotic syndrome at 6 months assessed by the Venous Clinical Severity Score (VCSS)
- Key results: At 6 months, mean VCSS was 8.1±5.1 in the endovascular group vs. 10.0±4.9 in the control group (adjusted difference, -2.0; P=0.001); venous disease-specific and overall quality of life scores were significantly better in the endovascular group; bleeding occurred in 11.6% vs. 3.6% (P=0.03).
- Limitations:
- Short follow-up duration (6 months)
- Increased bleeding risk with endovascular therapy
- Generalizability may be limited to patients with moderate or severe post-thrombotic syndrome and iliac-vein obstruction
- Why this matters: This study provides evidence that endovascular therapy can improve symptoms and quality of life in patients with significant post-thrombotic syndrome, informing treatment decisions for this population.

deep_dives.title: Intraoperative Hemodynamic Management during Liver Transplantation and Postoperative Morbidity: A Multicenter Cohort Study.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large multicenter cohort study of 852 liver transplant recipients, neither intraoperative fluid balance nor vasopressor dose was significantly associated with early allograft dysfunction or primary graft nonfunction at 7 days, suggesting that variations in these hemodynamic management strategies may not impact short-term graft outcomes. This finding provides moderate-strength evidence to inform intraoperative management decisions during liver transplantation.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Higher intraoperative fluid balance and vasopressor doses were not associated with increased risk of early allograft dysfunction or primary graft nonfunction after liver transplantation.

- Study type: Multicenter cohort study
- Population/setting: Consecutive liver transplant recipients (N=852, 836 with complete data) at eight centers in Canada and France between January 2021 and May 2023.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Intraoperative fluid balance and vasopressor doses; compared across varying levels of each exposure.
- Primary endpoints: 7-day early allograft dysfunction or primary graft nonfunction.
- Key results: Incidence of 7-day early allograft dysfunction or primary graft nonfunction was 28% (236 patients); adjusted risk ratio for fluid balance was 1.02 (95% CI, 0.97 to 1.06) per liter, and for vasopressor dose was 1.03 (95% CI, 0.99 to 1.06) per 25 μg/kg norepinephrine equivalent, indicating no significant association.
- Limitations:
- Observational design limits causal inference.
- Potential for residual confounding despite adjustment.
- Study limited to centers in Canada and France, which may affect generalizability.
- Why this matters: These findings suggest that more liberal intraoperative fluid or vasopressor use may not increase the risk of early graft dysfunction, informing perioperative management strategies in liver transplantation.

deep_dives.title: Prone Positioning in Infants With Acute Bronchiolitis: The PROPOSITIS Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large multicenter randomized trial, prone positioning in infants with moderate to severe acute bronchiolitis on high-flow nasal cannula did not reduce the need for escalation to noninvasive or invasive ventilation compared to supine positioning, indicating no clinical benefit for routine prone positioning in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Prone positioning did not significantly reduce escalation to noninvasive or invasive ventilation in infants with moderate to severe bronchiolitis receiving HFNC support.

- Study type: Multicenter, randomized, open-label trial
- Population/setting: Infants aged 6 months or younger with moderate to severe acute bronchiolitis requiring HFNC support, admitted to 15 pediatric intermediate or intensive care units in France.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Prone positioning for 24 hours or longer during the first 48 hours versus supine positioning; both groups received standardized HFNC support.
- Primary endpoints: Need for escalation of care to noninvasive or invasive ventilation within the first 72 hours.
- Key results: Escalation of care occurred in 15.0% of the prone group and 20.8% of the supine group (adjusted odds ratio, 0.66 [95% CI, 0.40-1.07]; P = .09), with no significant difference; secondary outcomes and adverse events were similar between groups.
- Limitations:
- Wide 95% confidence interval around the observed odds ratio.
- Study may not have been powered to detect smaller differences.
- Open-label design could introduce bias.
- Why this matters: This study informs clinicians that prone positioning does not significantly reduce escalation of respiratory support in infants with bronchiolitis on HFNC, highlighting the need for further research before changing clinical practice.

pubmed.title: The AI dispatcher copilot: beyond cardiac arrest recognition to dynamic large language model-assisted Tele-CPR.
pubmed.bottom_line: This narrative synthesis proposes a shift from traditional AI recognition of cardiac arrest to a dynamic, large language model–assisted "AI dispatcher copilot" for Tele-CPR, potentially improving out-of-hospital cardiac arrest outcomes through adaptive, multimodal support; however, clinical impact remains theoretical and requires prospective validation and careful ethical oversight before implementation.

pubmed.title: How I Do It: Counseling Patients About Outdoor Exercise During Air Pollution Events.
pubmed.bottom_line: This article introduces a practical, individualized four-step framework for counseling patients about outdoor exercise during air pollution events, emphasizing risk stratification, real-time air quality data, and tailored mitigation strategies rather than blanket exercise cessation; while not based on new clinical trial data, it synthesizes existing evidence and guidelines into actionable advice, supporting safer physical activity and shared decision-making in clinical practice.

pubmed.title: Association Between Emergency Department-to-ICU Transfer Time and Hospital Mortality Across ICU Admission Diagnoses: A Post Hoc Subgroup Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective cohort study found that longer emergency department-to-ICU transfer times were associated with higher hospital mortality for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest patients in academic hospitals and for nonoperative trauma overall, but not for other common ICU diagnoses; these findings suggest that timely ICU transfer may be particularly critical for certain patient groups, though the strength of association varies by diagnosis and hospital type.

pubmed.title: Concordance Between Bedside and Electronic Detection of Intracranial Pressure Crises: Insights From the Brain Tissue Oxygen Monitoring and Management in Severe Traumatic Brain Injury II Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this post hoc analysis of the BOOST II trial, less than half of bedside-identified intracranial pressure (ICP) crises in severe TBI patients were confirmed by continuous electronic ICP monitoring, highlighting substantial discordance that may impact clinical decision-making and research outcomes; this suggests a need for improved standardization and reliability in ICP crisis detection.

pubmed.title: Association of Serum Lactate with Outcome After Pediatric Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Secondary Analysis of the Therapeutic Hypothermia After Pediatric Cardiac Arrest (THAPCA) Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this secondary analysis of the THAPCA-OH trial, higher serum lactate levels within 6 hours of return of spontaneous circulation after pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest were strongly associated with worse 12-month neurobehavioral outcomes and increased hospital mortality, suggesting that early lactate measurement may be a useful adjunct for early prognostication in this population.

pubmed.title: Improving End-of-Life Screening in the Emergency Department With Collaborative Artificial Intelligence.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this prospective cohort study of older ED patients, an AI-based tool (GEST) outperformed physician judgment (Surprise Question) in predicting 6-month mortality, and combining both (GEST+SQ) further improved predictive accuracy, suggesting that collaborative AI can enhance end-of-life screening and potentially guide timely interventions in the emergency department.

pubmed.title: Comparison of Intraosseous Line Placement Location and Rates of Return of Spontaneous Circulation and Survival to Discharge Among Patients with Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest.
pubmed.bottom_line: This retrospective study found that humeral intraosseous (IO) line placement in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest was associated with significantly higher rates of return of spontaneous circulation and survival to hospital discharge compared to tibial IO placement, suggesting humeral IO may be preferable, though further controlled studies are needed to confirm these findings and inform protocol changes.

pubmed.title: Portal vein Doppler in peri-operative and critical care medicine: Physiology, measurement and clinical applications.
pubmed.bottom_line: Portal vein Doppler ultrasound shows promise for assessing haemodynamics and venous congestion in peri-operative and critical care settings, but current evidence is mainly observational; larger prospective studies are needed before it can be routinely recommended for clinical decision-making.

pubmed.title: Accuracy of Point-of-Care Ultrasound Versus Consultative Echocardiography to Identify Right Ventricular Dysfunction in Emergency Department Patients With Pulmonary Embolism.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this retrospective cohort of 194 ED patients with pulmonary embolism, emergency physician-performed POCUS demonstrated moderate sensitivity (76.8%) and high specificity (85.9%) compared to consultative echocardiography for detecting right ventricular dysfunction, with accuracy improving for moderate to severe dysfunction; these findings support the clinical utility of POCUS for rapid risk stratification in PE, though some diagnostic limitations remain.

pubmed.title: From P-values to priors: revisiting the VERITAS randomized controlled trial in light of Bayes' theorem.
pubmed.bottom_line: This article reanalyzes the VERITAS randomized controlled trial using Bayesian methods, highlighting how incorporating prior probabilities can alter the interpretation of trial results; while not providing new clinical data, it underscores the importance of Bayesian approaches for more nuanced evidence assessment in clinical decision-making.

pubmed.title: Prehospital Whole Blood in Traumatic Hemorrhage - A Randomized Controlled Trial.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter randomized controlled trial, prehospital whole-blood transfusion did not improve 24-hour survival or reduce massive transfusion rates compared to standard component therapy in traumatic hemorrhage, indicating no clinical advantage for whole blood in this setting. This high-quality evidence suggests that current standard component therapy remains appropriate for prehospital management of traumatic hemorrhage.

foamed.title: The Kids Are(n’t) Alright: Distinguishing Necessary from Unnecessary Discomfort in Medical Training
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational commentary discussing the distinction between necessary and unnecessary discomfort in medical training, emphasizing the importance of developing resilience and emotional capacity through repeated exposure to challenging situations, but it does not present new research data or clinical study results.

deep_dives.title: Prehospital Whole Blood in Traumatic Hemorrhage - A Randomized Controlled Trial.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter randomized controlled trial, prehospital whole-blood transfusion did not improve 24-hour survival or reduce massive transfusion rates compared to standard component therapy in traumatic hemorrhage, indicating no clinical advantage for whole blood in this setting. This high-quality evidence suggests that current standard component therapy remains appropriate for prehospital management of traumatic hemorrhage.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Prehospital transfusion of whole blood was not superior to standard care with blood components in reducing death or massive transfusion within 24 hours in patients with major traumatic hemorrhage.

- Study type: Pragmatic, phase 3, multicenter, unblinded, randomized, superiority trial
- Population/setting: Patients with major traumatic hemorrhage attended by 10 air ambulance services in England (N=616 analyzed)
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Prehospital transfusion of up to 2 units of whole blood versus standard care with up to 2 units each of red cells and plasma
- Primary endpoints: Composite of death from any cause or massive transfusion (≥10 units of blood components or products) within 24 hours after randomization
- Key results: Primary outcome occurred in 48.7% of the whole-blood group and 47.7% of the standard-care group (relative risk, 1.02; 95% CI, 0.80 to 1.31; P = 0.84); secondary outcomes and adverse events were similar between groups, though more serious adverse events occurred in the standard-care group.
- Limitations:
- Unblinded design
- Exclusion of patients with nontraumatic hemorrhage or traumatic cardiac arrest
- Study limited to prehospital setting and up to 2 units transfused
- Why this matters: This study provides high-quality evidence that prehospital whole blood transfusion does not improve early survival or reduce massive transfusion compared to standard component therapy in traumatic hemorrhage.

deep_dives.title: Concordance Between Bedside and Electronic Detection of Intracranial Pressure Crises: Insights From the Brain Tissue Oxygen Monitoring and Management in Severe Traumatic Brain Injury II Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this post hoc analysis of the BOOST II trial, less than half of bedside-identified intracranial pressure (ICP) crises in severe TBI patients were confirmed by continuous electronic ICP monitoring, highlighting substantial discordance that may impact clinical decision-making and research outcomes; this suggests a need for improved standardization and reliability in ICP crisis detection.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Bedside identification of intracranial pressure (ICP) crises in severe TBI patients often does not align with electronic ICP data, suggesting a need for improved, synchronized electronic detection methods.

- Study type: Post hoc comparison study using data from a randomized trial
- Population/setting: Seventy severe TBI patients with complete datasets from ten U.S. ICUs in the BOOST II trial
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Bedside-identified ICP episodes versus background-collected electronic ICP data
- Primary endpoints: Concordance between bedside-identified ICP episodes and electronic ICP data during defined ICP crisis periods
- Key results: Only 47% of bedside-identified ICP episodes were confirmed by electronic ICP data; 38% of concordant episodes and 76% of discordant episodes had average electronic ICP values below 20 mm Hg.
- Limitations:
- Lack of a rigid end-of-episode definition
- Inability to fully control for temporal synchrony confounding
- Insufficient data to evaluate temporal asynchrony between bedside and electronic data points
- Why this matters: Accurate identification of ICP crises is critical for TBI management and research; current bedside methods may overestimate true sustained ICP episodes, highlighting the need for better electronic monitoring protocols.

deep_dives.title: Improving End-of-Life Screening in the Emergency Department With Collaborative Artificial Intelligence.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this prospective cohort study of older ED patients, an AI-based tool (GEST) outperformed physician judgment (Surprise Question) in predicting 6-month mortality, and combining both (GEST+SQ) further improved predictive accuracy, suggesting that collaborative AI can enhance end-of-life screening and potentially guide timely interventions in the emergency department.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: The GEST AI model modestly outperformed the physician-answered surprise question (SQ) for predicting 6-month mortality in older ED patients, and a collaborative GEST+SQ model improved calibration but not discrimination, while a sequential approach could greatly reduce physician screening burden.

- Study type: Prospective cohort study
- Population/setting: Patients aged 65 years and older at a tertiary academic emergency department (single-site, Nov 2022 to June 2023)
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: GEST AI model, physician-answered surprise question (SQ), and a combined GEST+SQ model
- Primary endpoints: Prediction of 6-month mortality; sensitivity, specificity, ROC-AUC, and calibration error
- Key results: GEST had higher specificity (61.5% vs. 50.8%) and sensitivity (90.0% vs. 83.8%) than SQ at matched thresholds; GEST ROC-AUC was 0.79, GEST+SQ ROC-AUC was 0.80; GEST+SQ improved calibration error (0.01 vs. 0.042); sequential screening reduced need for SQ to 5% of patients.
- Limitations:
- Single-site study limits generalizability
- SQ responses were available for only 37.6% of eligible patients
- Potential selection bias due to incomplete SQ data
- Why this matters: Integrating AI-based screening with targeted physician input can improve mortality risk assessment in the ED and significantly reduce clinician workload.

pubmed.title: Standardized low-flow ventilation with PEEP maintenance during bronchoscopy in mechanically ventilated ICU patients: a pilot trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This single-center pilot trial found that a standardized low-flow ventilation protocol with PEEP maintenance during bronchoscopy in mechanically ventilated ICU patients significantly reduced peak inspiratory pressures and ventilator alarms without compromising minute ventilation or CO2 clearance, suggesting improved procedural safety and feasibility, though larger studies are needed to confirm these findings.

foamed.title: PulmCrit: Is oseltamivir assassinating ICU patients?
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is a critical commentary discussing methodological flaws and potential bias in the REMAP-CAP oseltamivir arm, highlighting that non-randomized site participation and imbalanced patient severity may invalidate the primary analysis suggesting harm, and that subgroup analyses do not show statistically significant differences in mortality between oseltamivir and no antiviral therapy.

foamed.title: Bronchoscopy for Percutaneous Tracheostomy
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational overview describes the use of bronchoscopy during percutaneous dilatational tracheostomy to enhance procedural safety, accuracy, and supervision by providing real-time visualization for confirming tube position, guidewire placement, ventilation safety, and early detection of complications, but does not present new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.title: VAFI and FARSI
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational overview describes VAFI (video-assisted flexible intubation) and FARSI (flexiscope-assisted rapid sequence intubation) as hybrid airway management techniques combining videolaryngoscopy and flexible bronchoscopy, clarifies their indications, and distinguishes their roles in managing supraglottic and subglottic airway pathology, without presenting new clinical data.

foamed.title: If this, then that
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational commentary emphasizing the complexity of clinical decision-making in pediatric care, highlighting that unlike binary programming logic, effective assessment and management require integrating diverse clinical perspectives and expertise, as illustrated by the collaborative development of the Irish College of GPs Paediatric Sepsis General Practice Update.

pubmed.title: Computed Tomography-Based Body Composition Assessment for Preoperative Cardiovascular Risk Prediction: A Prospective Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this prospective multicenter cohort study of 1,594 patients, adding CT-based body composition metrics (skeletal muscle area, muscle radiodensity, and subcutaneous fat radiodensity) to standard risk models significantly improved prediction of 30-day postoperative cardiovascular events, suggesting that preoperative CT analysis may enhance cardiovascular risk stratification in patients undergoing major noncardiac surgery.

pubmed.title: PEEP and alveolar recruitment after 60 years of acute respiratory distress syndrome.
pubmed.bottom_line: This review highlights that optimal PEEP in ARDS should be individualized based on lung recruitability, with higher PEEP reserved for patients with PaO2/FiO2 ≤ 200 mmHg and significant recruitability, while lower PEEP suffices for others; traditional bedside markers are unreliable for guiding PEEP in individuals, underscoring the need for physiologic assessment tools to minimize ventilator-induced lung injury and improve outcomes.

pubmed.title: Machine learning vs. traditional methods for predicting postoperative cardiac complications after non-cardiac surgery: a systematic review and Bayesian network meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large systematic review and Bayesian network meta-analysis found that machine learning models, particularly automated machine learning and gradient boosting, more accurately predict postoperative cardiac complications after non-cardiac surgery than traditional risk scores, suggesting these approaches could improve perioperative risk stratification, though external validation is still needed.

pubmed.title: Diastolic blood pressure and end-tidal carbon dioxide during adult ICU cardiopulmonary resuscitation: association with return of spontaneous circulation.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective observational study found that higher mean diastolic blood pressure and end-tidal CO2 during CPR in adult ICU cardiac arrest were significantly associated with return of spontaneous circulation, with diastolic blood pressure showing stronger predictive value; these findings support monitoring these parameters to guide resuscitation quality, though the evidence is limited by the small sample size.

foamed.title: Rocuronium vs Succinylcholine for RSI: Awareness, Paralysis, and Post-Intubation Sedation
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In a retrospective observational study, rocuronium was associated with slightly delayed post-intubation sedation and analgesia compared to succinylcholine in adult ED patients, with uncertain clinical significance, but its longer duration raises concern for awareness during paralysis, highlighting the need to plan sedation and analgesia before RSI, especially with long-acting paralytics.

foamed.title: Perioperative diabetes care in 2026
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article provides an educational overview of evolving recommendations and challenges in perioperative diabetes care, particularly regarding newer antidiabetic therapies, but does not present new clinical study data or original research findings.

foamed.title: DFTB26: Doing the Fundamentals Extraordinarily Well
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is a reflective commentary on the importance of consistently performing fundamental aspects of pediatric emergency and acute care with excellence, based on experiences and discussions at the DFTB 2026 conference, and does not present new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.title: The Pancreatitis Case File: From Suspects to Sentencing — An ED Guide to Pancreatitis

deep_dives.title: Computed Tomography-Based Body Composition Assessment for Preoperative Cardiovascular Risk Prediction: A Prospective Cohort Study.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this prospective multicenter cohort study of 1,594 patients, adding CT-based body composition metrics (skeletal muscle area, muscle radiodensity, and subcutaneous fat radiodensity) to standard risk models significantly improved prediction of 30-day postoperative cardiovascular events, suggesting that preoperative CT analysis may enhance cardiovascular risk stratification in patients undergoing major noncardiac surgery.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: CT-derived body composition metrics enhance the prediction of postoperative cardiovascular events beyond conventional clinical predictors in patients undergoing major noncardiac surgery.

- Study type: Prospective multicenter cohort study
- Population/setting: Patients with cardiovascular disease or risk factors undergoing major noncardiac surgery in the PREVENGE-CB and Nanfang cohorts (N=1594).
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Preoperative CT-based body composition metrics (skeletal muscle area, muscle radiodensity, subcutaneous fat radiodensity) compared to guideline-recommended clinical predictors (e.g., Revised Cardiac Risk Index, Gupta risk calculator).
- Primary endpoints: Composite cardiovascular events within 30 days after surgery.
- Key results: Larger skeletal muscle and adipose areas were associated with lower risk, while higher adipose radiodensity and lower muscle radiodensity indicated higher risk; adding three CT-based metrics improved discrimination over the Revised Cardiac Risk Index (ΔAUC = 0.136; 95% CI, 0.083 to 0.188) and other models, with findings validated in an independent cohort.
- Limitations:
- Potential selection bias due to inclusion criteria.
- Generalizability may be limited to similar surgical populations.
- CT imaging may not be routinely available for all patients.
- Why this matters: Incorporating CT-based body composition analysis into preoperative assessment may improve cardiovascular risk stratification and guide perioperative management.

deep_dives.title: PEEP and alveolar recruitment after 60 years of acute respiratory distress syndrome.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This review highlights that optimal PEEP in ARDS should be individualized based on lung recruitability, with higher PEEP reserved for patients with PaO2/FiO2 ≤ 200 mmHg and significant recruitability, while lower PEEP suffices for others; traditional bedside markers are unreliable for guiding PEEP in individuals, underscoring the need for physiologic assessment tools to minimize ventilator-induced lung injury and improve outcomes.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: PEEP strategies in ARDS should be individualized based on lung recruitability, with higher PEEP reserved for patients with significant recruitable lung and lower PEEP for those with limited recruitability or PaO2/FiO2 > 200 mmHg.

- Study type: Narrative review
- Population/setting: Patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) in intensive care settings
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Higher versus lower PEEP strategies, tailored by lung recruitability and PaO2/FiO2 ratio
- Primary endpoints: Narrative review
- Key results: Patients with PaO2/FiO2 > 200 mmHg generally do not benefit from higher PEEP, while those with PaO2/FiO2 ≤ 200 mmHg and significant recruitability may benefit from higher PEEP; traditional clinical markers are unreliable for individual PEEP titration.
- Limitations:
- No new clinical trial data presented
- Recommendations are based on synthesis of prior studies and expert opinion
- Lack of quantitative outcome data
- Why this matters: This review highlights the importance of individualized PEEP titration in ARDS, emphasizing physiologic assessment over population-based protocols to optimize lung protection and minimize harm.

pubmed.title: Guidelines: Diagnosis and management of perioperative immediate hypersensitivity reactions 2025.
pubmed.bottom_line: These new French guidelines provide 70 expert consensus recommendations—6 with high-level evidence—on diagnosing and managing perioperative immediate hypersensitivity reactions, offering clinicians an updated, evidence-based framework to improve patient safety and outcomes during anesthesia.

pubmed.title: 4% Tetrasodium EDTA to Prevent Central Venous Access Device-Associated Complications: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter, triple-blind randomized trial, 4% tetrasodium EDTA locking fluid did not significantly reduce the composite rate of central venous access device-associated infections, occlusions, or thrombolytic use compared to standard locking solutions in ICU patients, suggesting no clear clinical benefit for routine use of t-EDTA in this setting.

pubmed.bottom_line: In this double-blind, randomized controlled trial, continuous serratus anterior plane block after VATS did not significantly improve quality of recovery scores but did result in modestly reduced opioid consumption, suggesting a potential opioid-sparing benefit without clear impact on broader recovery outcomes.

foamed.title: Bronchoscopic Foreign Body Removal
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: Flexible bronchoscopy is the first-line approach for airway foreign body removal in most adults, achieving over 90% success, while rigid bronchoscopy is reserved for large, sharp, proximal, or asphyxiating objects, with pre-procedural planning tailored to foreign body characteristics and patient factors to optimize safety and outcomes.

deep_dives.title: 4% Tetrasodium EDTA to Prevent Central Venous Access Device-Associated Complications: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter, triple-blind randomized trial, 4% tetrasodium EDTA locking fluid did not significantly reduce the composite rate of central venous access device-associated infections, occlusions, or thrombolytic use compared to standard locking solutions in ICU patients, suggesting no clear clinical benefit for routine use of t-EDTA in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** In this large, multicenter, triple-blind randomized trial, 4% tetrasodium EDTA locking fluid did not significantly reduce the composite rate of central venous access device-associated infections, occlusions, or thrombolytic use compared to standard locking solutions in ICU patients, suggesting no clear clinical benefit for routine use of t-EDTA in this setting.

BOTTOM LINE: Use of 4% tetrasodium EDTA locking solution for CVADs in ICU adults reduced the incidence of a composite of CVAD-associated bloodstream infection, catheter occlusion requiring alteplase, and catheter removal due to occlusion compared to control locking solutions.

- Study type: Pragmatic, triple-blind, multicenter, cluster-randomized crossover trial
- Population/setting: Adults (>18 years) in ICUs with a CVAD and at least one unused lumen at 6 Canadian hospitals (3 community, 3 academic)
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: 4% tetrasodium EDTA locking fluid versus control locking fluid (saline or 4% citrate for hemodialysis lines)
- Primary endpoints: Composite incidence rate of CVAD-associated bloodstream infection, catheter occlusion requiring alteplase use, and/or catheter removal due to occlusion
- Key results: The incidence rate of primary outcome events was 13.1 per 1000 catheter-days (74 events) in the t-EDTA group vs 19.9 per 1000 catheter-days (126 events) in the control group (rate ratio, 0.68; 95% CI, 0.47-0.96; P = .03); only CVAD occlusion requiring alteplase was significantly lower in the t-EDTA group (rate ratio, 0.66; 95% CI, 0.46-0.96).
- Limitations:
- Cluster-randomized crossover design may introduce carryover effects.
- Study limited to ICU patients in Canadian hospitals, which may affect generalizability.
- Only patients with at least one unused CVAD lumen were included.
- Why this matters: This study provides evidence that 4% tetrasodium EDTA locking solution can reduce important CVAD-related complications in critically ill adults, potentially improving patient outcomes and reducing the need for thrombolytic interventions.

deep_dives.title: Impact of continuous serratus plane analgesia on quality of recovery after video-assisted thoracic surgery: a prospective double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this double-blind, randomized controlled trial, continuous serratus anterior plane block after VATS did not significantly improve quality of recovery scores but did result in modestly reduced opioid consumption, suggesting a potential opioid-sparing benefit without clear impact on broader recovery outcomes.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** In this double-blind, randomized controlled trial, continuous serratus anterior plane block after VATS did not significantly improve quality of recovery scores but did result in modestly reduced opioid consumption, suggesting a potential opioid-sparing benefit without clear impact on broader recovery outcomes.

BOTTOM LINE: Continuous serratus anterior plane block (SAPB) infusion did not significantly improve quality of recovery (QoR-40) after VATS, though it reduced opioid consumption modestly.

- Study type: Prospective, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial
- Population/setting: Adults undergoing elective video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) at a single center (N=173)
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Continuous SAPB catheter infusion with 0.2% ropivacaine versus saline placebo, both with standardized multimodal analgesia
- Primary endpoints: QoR-40 score on postoperative day 1
- Key results: Median QoR-40 scores on POD 1 were higher in the SAPB group (176 vs 172; p=0.1036) but not statistically significant; SAPB significantly reduced opioid consumption in recovery (8.0±7.4 vs 10.6±9.3 IV MME; p=0.0388) and on POD 1 (12.73±10.75 vs 16.45±12.17 IV MME; p=0.0349); no significant differences in pain scores, complications, or 30-day readmissions.
- Limitations:
- Single-center study
- Sample size may be insufficient to detect small differences in QoR-40
- Secondary outcomes were exploratory
- No significant difference in primary endpoint
- Why this matters: This study informs clinicians that continuous SAPB infusion may not improve overall recovery quality after VATS, though it can reduce opioid requirements modestly.

pubmed.title: Severe asthma and remission prospects in Europe (SHARP): insights from a multicentre observational study based on the European Severe Asthma Registry.

pubmed.title: Ethical Implications of the Slow Code: A Systematic Review of Ethics of Slow Codes in U.S. Hospitals.

pubmed.title: Hypotension and pulmonary atelectasis during general anaesthesia: a randomised crossover laboratory study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This randomized crossover laboratory study in anaesthetized pigs found that profound hypotension during anaesthesia induction significantly increased pulmonary atelectasis compared to normotension, likely due to altered pulmonary blood flow and lower oxygen saturation; these findings suggest that maintaining adequate blood pressure during anaesthesia induction may help reduce atelectasis risk, though clinical studies in humans are needed.

pubmed.title: Prevalence and disease trajectories of pulmonary fibrosis of childhood interstitial lung disease: a register-based, multicentre observational study.
