deep_dives.title: Prevalence and disease trajectories of pulmonary fibrosis of childhood interstitial lung disease: a register-based, multicentre observational study.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large, multicenter observational study found that pulmonary fibrosis occurs in about 12–21% of children with interstitial lung disease and is associated with persistently reduced lung function and worse survival, highlighting the need for early identification and targeted management in this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Pulmonary fibrosis affects a significant proportion of children with chILD, is associated with lower lung function and increased mortality, and may identify patients who could benefit from antifibrotic therapies.

- Study type: Multicentre observational cohort study
- Population/setting: Children and adolescents (aged 0-18 years) with childhood interstitial lung disease from 23 European countries, N=1071
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Children with pulmonary fibrosis (as defined by fibrosisregister or fibrosistrial criteria) versus those without fibrosing lung disease
- Primary endpoints: Presence or absence of fibrosing lung disease; pulmonary function (percent predicted forced vital capacity); survival (death or lung transplantation)
- Key results: 20.5% met fibrosisregister criteria and 11.6% met fibrosistrial criteria; children with fibrosis had 15-20% lower ppFVC across 8 years; survival was lower in fibrosisregister group (HR 1.64, 95% CI 1.18-2.28, p=0.0029); higher BMI z-score was protective (HR 0.80).
- Limitations:
- Ongoing study with possible incomplete follow-up
- Potential for selection bias from register-based data
- Limited generalizability outside European settings
- Why this matters: Standardized identification of pulmonary fibrosis in chILD can guide prognosis and highlight the need for targeted antifibrotic interventions in affected children.

deep_dives.title: A Pragmatic Trial of a 6-Month Strategy for Rifampicin-Resistant Tuberculosis.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this phase 3 randomized trial, a 6-month regimen for rifampicin-resistant tuberculosis was noninferior to the standard 9-month regimen in terms of efficacy and had a similar safety profile, supporting the use of shorter, all-oral treatment strategies to improve patient outcomes and reduce treatment burden.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: A 6-month regimen for rifampicin-resistant tuberculosis was noninferior to the 9-month standard-of-care regimen in terms of efficacy and had a similar safety profile.

- Study type: Phase 3 open-label pragmatic randomized controlled noninferiority trial
- Population/setting: Participants aged 6 years or older with pulmonary rifampicin-resistant tuberculosis in South Africa, including pregnant or breastfeeding individuals and those with fluoroquinolone-resistant TB; N=403 randomized.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: 6-month regimen of bedaquiline, linezolid, delamanid, and levofloxacin or clofazimine or both (trial-strategy group) versus 9-month standard-of-care regimen (control group), with treatment adjustments based on drug susceptibility testing.
- Primary endpoints: Successful outcome (cure or completion of treatment) at end of treatment and at 76 weeks after randomization; primary safety endpoint was adverse event of grade 3 or higher.
- Key results: Successful outcomes occurred in 86.1% (174/202) of the trial-strategy group and 86.0% (172/200) of the control group (adjusted risk difference -0.2 percentage points, 95% CI -6.9 to 6.5; P=0.001 for noninferiority). Grade 3 or higher adverse events occurred in 31.2% (63/202) of the trial-strategy group and 37.0% (74/200) of the control group; 10 deaths occurred in each group.
- Limitations:
- Open-label design may introduce bias.
- Study conducted in a single country (South Africa), which may limit generalizability.
- Noninferiority margin selection may affect interpretation.
- Why this matters: This study supports the use of a shorter, 6-month regimen for rifampicin-resistant tuberculosis, potentially improving treatment adherence and reducing resource use without compromising efficacy or safety.

deep_dives.title: Severe asthma and remission prospects in Europe (SHARP): insights from a multicentre observational study based on the European Severe Asthma Registry.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large multicenter European registry study found that severe asthma remains highly burdensome, with true remission rare despite widespread use of biologics, highlighting the persistent unmet need for more effective therapies and long-term management strategies in this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Severe asthma in Europe remains highly burdensome and heterogeneous, with persistent type 2 inflammation and limited remission despite widespread use of biological therapies.

- Study type: Cross-sectional observational study
- Population/setting: 13,455 adults with severe asthma from the European Severe Heterogeneous Asthma Registry (SHARP CRC) across multiple European countries
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Biological therapy and maintenance oral corticosteroid use; comparison of patients with and without biological therapy, and analysis of type 2 biomarker elevation
- Primary endpoints: Burden of severe asthma and remission-related clinical domains (exacerbations, asthma control, airflow limitation, maintenance oral corticosteroid use); relationship with disease duration, biological therapy, and type 2 biomarkers
- Key results: Despite 79% receiving biological therapy, over 66% still had at least two active disease domains, and 89% had at least one elevated type 2 biomarker; 35% of biologic-naive patients accumulated significant disease burden within 10 years.
- Limitations:
- Cross-sectional design limits causal inference
- Potential heterogeneity in national registry enrollment criteria
- Missing data for some endpoints
- Exclusion of 3% of patients due to consent or missing medication data
- Why this matters: The study highlights the need for improved strategies to achieve remission and early identification of high-risk patients with severe asthma, as current therapies may be insufficient for many.

pubmed.title: The frequent exacerbator phenotype in bronchiectasis revisited: Data from EMBARC registry.
pubmed.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, multinational EMBARC registry study shows that each prior exacerbation, especially severe ones, incrementally increases the risk of future exacerbations in bronchiectasis, supporting the prognostic value of exacerbation history across etiologies and regions; this underscores the importance of aggressive prevention strategies in patients with any prior exacerbations.

pubmed.title: Analgesic efficacy of locoregional anesthesia techniques in cardiac surgery: a systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomized trials.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large network meta-analysis of 133 randomized trials found that intrathecal opioids, erector spinae plane block, and parasternal intercostal plane block significantly reduce opioid consumption and pain after cardiac surgery, with intrathecal opioids providing the greatest pain relief; some techniques also shortened ICU and hospital stays, supporting their clinical use as effective alternatives or adjuncts to opioids.

pubmed.title: Pilot study of feasibility, acceptability, and changes in clinical outcomes following a brief trauma-focused intervention for chronic pain.
pubmed.bottom_line: This single-arm pilot study found that a brief, remotely delivered trauma-focused intervention (STEPP) for adults with chronic pain and trauma exposure was highly feasible and acceptable, with strong completion rates and positive participant ratings; while preliminary, these findings suggest potential for scalable trauma-informed pain management, warranting further research in controlled trials.

foamed.title: Cold Kids, Hot Virus: Six Cases of Hypothermia in Pediatric Influenza
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This case series describes six pediatric patients with laboratory-confirmed influenza who presented with hypothermia rather than fever, highlighting that hypothermia is an uncommon but recognized manifestation of severe influenza in children and should prompt careful assessment for complications and provision of supportive care.

deep_dives.title: Hospital Policy of Tranexamic Acid to Reduce Transfusion in Major Noncardiac Surgery.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, multicenter, cluster-randomized trial, implementing a hospital policy of intraoperative tranexamic acid in major noncardiac surgery significantly reduced red-cell transfusion rates without increasing venous thromboembolism risk, providing strong evidence for its safe, routine use to minimize transfusion needs in high-risk surgical patients.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: A hospital policy of intraoperative tranexamic acid administration reduces red-cell transfusion rates in major noncardiac surgery without increasing venous thromboembolism risk.

- Study type: Multicenter, double-blind, cluster-randomized, placebo-controlled trial
- Population/setting: 8273 patients at high risk for red-cell transfusion undergoing major noncardiac surgery across 10 Canadian hospitals
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Hospital-wide policy of intraoperative tranexamic acid versus placebo
- Primary endpoints: Red-cell transfusion during index hospitalization (effectiveness) and diagnosis of venous thromboembolism within 90 days (safety)
- Key results: Red-cell transfusion occurred in 7.4% of the tranexamic acid group versus 9.8% of the placebo group (RR 0.73, 95% CI 0.61–0.86); venous thromboembolism occurred in 2.1% of both groups (RR 0.96, 95% CI 0.65–1.38), meeting noninferiority criteria.
- Limitations:
- Study conducted only in Canadian hospitals
- Majority of surgeries were oncologic, which may limit generalizability
- Cluster-crossover design may introduce complexity in analysis
- Why this matters: Implementing a hospital-wide tranexamic acid policy can safely reduce transfusion needs in major noncardiac surgery, potentially improving patient outcomes and resource utilization.

deep_dives.title: Analgesic efficacy of locoregional anesthesia techniques in cardiac surgery: a systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomized trials.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large network meta-analysis of 133 randomized trials found that intrathecal opioids, erector spinae plane block, and parasternal intercostal plane block significantly reduce opioid consumption and pain after cardiac surgery, with intrathecal opioids providing the greatest pain relief; some techniques also shortened ICU and hospital stays, supporting their clinical use as effective alternatives or adjuncts to opioids.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Intrathecal opioids provide the greatest reduction in opioid consumption and pain scores after cardiac surgery, with ESPB and PIPB as alternative options when neuraxial techniques are contraindicated, though their effects are smaller.

- Study type: Systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomized trials
- Population/setting: Adult patients undergoing cardiac surgery; 133 randomized trials with 9816 patients included
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Locoregional analgesic techniques (intrathecal opioids, erector spinae plane block, parasternal intercostal plane block, thoracic epidural anesthesia) compared to control
- Primary endpoints: Opioid consumption, pain scores, and time to first rescue analgesia within 24 hours postoperatively
- Key results: Intrathecal opioids reduced 24-hour opioid consumption by -14.8 MME, ESPB by -9.7 MME, and PIPB by -6.3 MME compared to control; IT also reduced pain scores by -1.2 cm on the visual analog scale at 24 hours; ESPB, PIPB, and TEA reduced ICU length of stay, but only TEA reduced hospital length of stay; no major block-related complications were reported.
- Limitations:
- Substantial interstudy heterogeneity
- Effect sizes for some techniques were below clinical relevance thresholds
- Need for more rigorous, high-quality trials to standardize strategies
- Why this matters: This analysis informs clinicians about the relative efficacy and safety of different locoregional analgesic techniques for pain management after cardiac surgery, supporting evidence-based choices to minimize opioid use and improve recovery.

pubmed.title: Pulmonary artery catheters or central venous catheters for cardiac surgery: the PUMA Pilot randomised clinical trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This well-conducted pilot randomized trial found high protocol compliance and feasibility for comparing pulmonary artery catheters versus central venous catheters in low-risk cardiac surgery patients, paving the way for a larger, adequately powered trial to determine if pulmonary artery catheters impact clinical outcomes. This matters because current practice lacks strong randomized evidence to guide catheter choice in cardiac surgery.

pubmed.title: Decoding candidemia in critically ill patients: unsupervised clustering identifies three unique phenotypes.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective multicenter study, unsupervised clustering identified three distinct clinical phenotypes of ICU candidemia with significantly different 90-day mortality rates, highlighting that cirrhosis, age, and severity scores—but not immunosuppression—are key mortality predictors, and that catheter-related candidemia and echinocandin use are associated with improved outcomes; these findings may help tailor risk stratification and management in critically ill patients with candidemia

foamed.title: Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL)
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) is a bronchoscopic technique that enables direct sampling of the distal airways for microbiological, cytological, and immunological analysis, and is particularly valuable for diagnosing pneumonia, diffuse infiltrates, alveolar hemorrhage, and infections in immunocompromised patients, but should be avoided in cases of severe hypoxemia or hemodynamic instability.

foamed.title: Sodium Bicarbonate for Metabolic Acidosis in the Intensive Care Unit (SODa-BIC) trial
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This summary provides background and design details of the SODa-BIC trial investigating whether sodium bicarbonate reduces major adverse kidney events within 30 days in critically ill adults with metabolic acidosis, but does not present new outcome data or results from the trial itself.

foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article discusses a retrospective study analyzing medical boarding across three EDs in Bristol, North Somerset, and South Gloucestershire, highlighting both downstream impacts on patient outcomes and upstream effects on ED crowding, but serves primarily as commentary and summary without presenting new original data within the blog post itself.

pubmed.title: HIgh versus STAndard blood Pressure target in hypertensive high-risk patients undergoing elective major abdominal surgery: the HISTAP multicenter randomized clinical trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this multicenter randomized trial of high-risk hypertensive patients undergoing major abdominal surgery, targeting a higher intraoperative MAP (≥80 mmHg) versus standard (≥65 mmHg) significantly reduced postoperative organ dysfunction and mortality (RR 0.78, 95% CI 0.65–0.93), particularly lowering rates of acute kidney injury, suggesting that higher MAP targets may improve outcomes in this population.

pubmed.title: Ketamine Infusion and Risk of Postoperative Nausea and Vomiting and Adverse Outcomes in Surgical Patients: A Secondary Analysis of the IMPAKT Randomized Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This secondary analysis of the IMPAKT randomized trial found that perioperative ketamine infusion does not increase the risk of postoperative nausea and vomiting or other adverse outcomes in surgical patients, supporting its safety for intraoperative use.

foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This UK national audit found that data on extubation practices following emergency intubation for paediatric status epilepticus are scarce, and the study aimed to address this gap by retrospectively analyzing outcomes and predictors of successful local extubation in children, but specific results or recommendations are not provided in the excerpt.

foamed.title: MDCalc Wars: Massive Transfusion in Trauma – ABC vs. TASH
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational commentary compares the ABC and TASH scores for predicting massive transfusion in trauma, highlighting that the ABC Score offers rapid, sensitive bedside assessment for protocol activation, while the TASH Score provides greater precision once laboratory and imaging data are available; both tools are complementary and should be used sequentially as clinical information evolves.

foamed.title: The Pyramid Dressing
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational article describes the "pyramid dressing," a focused haemorrhage control technique designed to transition from proximal tourniquet use to local pressure application, thereby controlling bleeding while restoring distal perfusion and minimizing the risk of limb ischaemia; no new clinical trial data are presented.

deep_dives.title: HIgh versus STAndard blood Pressure target in hypertensive high-risk patients undergoing elective major abdominal surgery: the HISTAP multicenter randomized clinical trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this multicenter randomized trial of high-risk hypertensive patients undergoing major abdominal surgery, targeting a higher intraoperative MAP (≥80 mmHg) versus standard (≥65 mmHg) significantly reduced postoperative organ dysfunction and mortality (RR 0.78, 95% CI 0.65–0.93), particularly lowering rates of acute kidney injury, suggesting that higher MAP targets may improve outcomes in this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: In high-risk hypertensive patients undergoing elective major abdominal surgery, targeting an intraoperative MAP ≥ 80 mmHg reduced major organ dysfunction and acute kidney injury compared to a target of ≥ 65 mmHg.

- Study type: Multicenter randomized clinical trial
- Population/setting: Patients aged ≥ 60 years with chronic hypertension and at least one additional high-risk criterion, undergoing elective major abdominal surgery at 18 Italian centers.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Intraoperative MAP target ≥ 80 mmHg (intervention) versus ≥ 65 mmHg (control).
- Primary endpoints: Composite of postoperative mortality and at least one major organ dysfunction.
- Key results: The primary composite outcome occurred in 38.1% of the higher MAP group versus 48.9% in the standard MAP group (RR 0.78; 95% CI 0.65-0.93; P = 0.006); acute kidney injury was less frequent in the higher MAP group (23.5% vs. 33.7%; P = 0.005).
- Limitations:
- Study limited to Italian centers, which may affect generalizability.
- Exclusion of patients whose surgery was canceled after randomization.
- Details on long-term outcomes and other organ dysfunctions not fully reported.
- Why this matters: This study informs intraoperative blood pressure management in high-risk hypertensive patients, suggesting that a higher MAP target may reduce postoperative complications, particularly acute kidney injury.

pubmed.title: Perioperative Antibiotic Prophylaxis in Burn Patients: Protocol for a Systematic Review.
pubmed.bottom_line: This protocol outlines a forthcoming systematic review and meta-analysis to assess the benefits and harms of perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis in burn surgery patients; while no new clinical data are presented yet, the planned rigorous methodology aims to clarify current uncertainties and could significantly inform future clinical practice regarding infection prevention in this high-risk group.

pubmed.title: Technology-Enhanced Strategies to Optimize Positive End-Expiratory Pressure in Patients Receiving Invasive Mechanical Ventilation: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis found that technology-enhanced PEEP optimization strategies, including esophageal balloon measurement and electrical impedance tomography, did not reduce the duration of mechanical ventilation compared to standard care (very low-certainty evidence), suggesting limited clinical benefit for these advanced approaches in current practice.

pubmed.title: Efficacy and safety of different vasopressor infusions on feto-maternal outcomes in normotensive patients undergoing caesarean delivery: a systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large network meta-analysis of 55 RCTs (n=5487) found that infusions of metaraminol, noradrenaline, phenylephrine, and adrenaline are clearly superior to no vasopressor for preventing maternal hypotension during caesarean delivery, with mephentermine and metaraminol best preserving fetal acid-base status, supporting consideration of these agents for optimizing feto-maternal outcomes.

pubmed.title: Regional anaesthesia in Video or Robot assisted thoracic surgery: A French multicentre prospective observational cohort study (ARTIST study).
pubmed.bottom_line: This large French multicentre prospective cohort study found that regional anaesthesia is used in over 90% of minimally invasive thoracic surgeries, with significant variability in block types and combinations; while multiple-block strategies are common, further research is needed to clarify their impact on postoperative recovery, highlighting the need for standardized RA protocols in this setting.

pubmed.title: Quality of care received by children with suspected appendicitis in the UK National Health Service.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large multicentre UK study found significant variation and deficiencies in adherence to care standards for children with suspected appendicitis, particularly in pre-operative imaging, analgesia, and timely surgery, with higher postoperative complications observed in specialist paediatric centres; targeted improvements in these areas could enhance outcomes and patient experience.

pubmed.title: Sex-related disparities in post-cardiac arrest: a cohort study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large prospective cohort study found that while women had worse unadjusted neurological outcomes after cardiac arrest, sex was not independently associated with mortality or neurological recovery after adjusting for arrest characteristics, suggesting that outcome disparities are due to underlying clinical factors rather than sex itself. This evidence supports focusing on modifiable arrest characteristics rather than sex in post-cardiac arrest prognostication and care.

pubmed.title: The Effect of Immersive Virtual Reality on Anterograde Amnesia and Subjective Pain During Procedures: A Within-Subject Randomized Controlled Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This small, within-subject randomized controlled study in healthy adults found that immersive virtual reality (VR) significantly reduced both subjective pain and memory recall of painful stimuli, suggesting VR distraction may not only alleviate procedural pain but also diminish patients' memory of the experience, which could have important implications for procedural anxiety and trauma.

pubmed.title: Agreement Between Methods Assessing Changes in Plasma Volume During Fluid Therapy-A Post Hoc Analysis of a Randomized Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this post hoc analysis of a randomized trial in postoperative patients, both anthropometric formulas and hematocrit-based methods showed poor agreement and low precision compared to radiolabeled albumin for assessing plasma volume changes during fluid therapy, indicating these simpler methods are unreliable for guiding individual fluid management.

pubmed.title: Rhythm-specific association of epinephrine with outcomes in non-shockable cardiac arrest: a nationwide propensity-matched study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, nationwide propensity-matched study, early epinephrine administration was more strongly associated with return of spontaneous circulation in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest patients with asystole compared to those with pulseless electrical activity, though both groups benefited; however, this did not translate into improved neurological outcomes at 30 days, highlighting the need to consider initial rhythm when evaluating epinephrine's impact.

pubmed.title: Electrical Impedance Tomography for Real-Time PEEP Monitoring and Atelectasis During Mask Ventilation: A Randomized Controlled Physiological Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This randomized controlled physiological trial demonstrates that real-time electrical impedance tomography (EIT) can effectively monitor and guide PEEP settings during mask ventilation, showing that higher PEEP levels significantly reduce atelectasis without causing notable hemodynamic compromise; this supports EIT as a valuable bedside tool for optimizing ventilation and potentially reducing perioperative pulmonary complications.

pubmed.title: Prophylactic esketamine to reduce postpartum depression in primiparae: A multicentre, double-blind, randomised clinical trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this multicentre, double-blind RCT of primiparae undergoing elective caesarean section, prophylactic esketamine significantly reduced the incidence of postpartum depression at 7 days and overall within 3 months, though not at individual 1, 2, or 3 month timepoints, suggesting esketamine may offer short-term PPD prevention in low-risk women, with mostly mild central nervous system side effects.

pubmed.title: Feasibility Pilot Trial for the Trajectories of Recovery after Intravenous Propofol versus Inhaled Volatile Anesthesia (THRIVE) Pragmatic Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This pilot randomized trial demonstrated high feasibility, with strong rates of patient enrollment, intervention adherence, and data completion for comparing intravenous propofol versus inhaled volatile anesthesia, supporting the viability of a large-scale comparative effectiveness trial; however, no clinical outcome data are reported yet.

pubmed.title: Magnesium Sulfate to Prevent Perioperative Atrial Fibrillation in Cardiac Surgery: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This well-conducted randomized controlled trial found that perioperative magnesium sulfate infusion did not reduce the incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation in cardiac surgery patients, indicating that routine prophylactic magnesium supplementation for POAF prevention is not supported.

pubmed.title: Agreement of minimally invasive pulse wave analysis with pulmonary artery and transpulmonary thermodilution cardiac output measurements in perioperative and intensive care medicine: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, low-bias meta-analysis found that minimally invasive pulse wave analysis for cardiac output and index has high percentage errors (44–49%) compared to thermodilution methods in perioperative and ICU patients, indicating limited clinical interchangeability and caution in relying solely on these devices for critical hemodynamic decisions.

pubmed.title: Prone Positioning in a North American Cohort of Hypoxemic Patients on Mechanical Ventilation.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective cohort study across 37 North American hospitals found that use of prone positioning for mechanically ventilated patients with moderate-to-severe hypoxemia increased significantly during the COVID-19 pandemic but declined post-pandemic, with substantial variation between hospitals; these findings highlight ongoing gaps in the sustained adoption of this guideline-recommended, potentially life-saving intervention.

pubmed.title: A Systematic Review and Expert Evaluation of Perioperative SGLT2 Inhibitor-Associated Ketoacidosis Case Reports.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review of 128 perioperative case reports found that SGLT2 inhibitor-associated ketoacidosis (SAPKA) was considered likely or possible in 71% of cases, with common risk factors including type 2 diabetes, impaired perioperative intake, and insufficient insulin; while insulin treatment was effective, significant morbidity occurred, underscoring the importance of perioperative vigilance and appropriate SGLT2i management in surgical patients.

pubmed.title: Critical care research: Which outcomes matter? Results of a national survey.
pubmed.bottom_line: This national survey of French intensivists found consensus on the need to prioritize and further develop patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs), such as quality of life and absence of sequelae, over traditional endpoints like mortality or variably defined ventilator-free days in critical care research, highlighting a shift toward outcomes more meaningful to patients.

pubmed.title: Epidemiology, ventilation management, and clinical outcomes in children (PRoVENT-PED): first results from the 10-year, investigator-initiated, international, multicentre, prospective cohort study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, international, prospective cohort study provides the first robust, pediatric-specific data on ventilation management and outcomes in critically ill children, highlighting that PARDS occurs in 11% of invasively ventilated cases and is most common in preschool-aged children; these findings fill a major evidence gap and can inform age-appropriate ventilation strategies in pediatric intensive care.

pubmed.title: A randomised controlled trial of defibrillation with manual pressure augmentation during out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, randomized controlled trial, manual pressure augmentation during defibrillation for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest significantly reduced transthoracic impedance but did not improve survival or neurologic outcomes compared to standard defibrillation; low protocol compliance and early trial termination limit the strength of conclusions, suggesting no clear clinical benefit for routine use.

pubmed.title: Effect on neonatal outcomes of maintenance of maternal blood pressure targets with noradrenaline after spinal anaesthesia for caesarean delivery: a multicentre, randomised, controlled trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large multicenter randomized controlled trial, maintaining maternal systolic blood pressure within 90% (vs. 80%) of baseline using noradrenaline after spinal anesthesia for cesarean delivery reduced the incidence of neonatal acidosis and maternal hypotension without increasing adverse effects, supporting a higher blood pressure target for better maternal and neonatal outcomes.

pubmed.title: Intravenous iron administration and management of adverse events: a systematic review and Network for the Advancement of Patient Blood Management, Haemostasis and Thrombosis consensus recommendations.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and consensus statement provides updated, evidence-based recommendations for diagnosing, preventing, and managing adverse events associated with intravenous iron therapy, aiming to improve its safe and effective use in treating and preventing anaemia; these guidelines are clinically important for addressing underuse due to safety concerns.

pubmed.title: Poor control of pulmonary viral replication in COVID-19 patients with early respiratory failure.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective cohort study found that critically ill COVID-19 patients requiring early mechanical ventilation often have high pulmonary SARS-CoV-2 RNA loads and active viral replication, along with suppressed innate immune responses, suggesting that poor early control of viral replication may contribute to rapid respiratory failure; these findings highlight the potential importance of early antiviral interventions in severe COVID-19.

pubmed.title: Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Agonist vs. Placebo and Pulmonary Decline After Open-Heart Surgery: A Substudy of the GLORIOUS Randomised Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized substudy of 878 patients undergoing open-heart surgery, perioperative infusion of the GLP-1 agonist exenatide did not reduce the mild-to-moderate decline in pulmonary function at 3 months compared to placebo, indicating no clinical benefit for lung protection in this context.

pubmed.title: Secondary Analysis of the CAN-Study, a Randomised Controlled Trial - Local Anaesthesia and Overall Survival.
pubmed.bottom_line: This secondary analysis of a large randomized controlled trial found no association between local or regional anaesthetic use during breast cancer surgery and long-term overall survival, suggesting that local anaesthetic infiltration does not impact cancer outcomes; the evidence is moderate but limited by the non-preplanned, observational nature of the analysis.

pubmed.title: Withholding or continuing glucose-lowering drugs for elective surgery in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus: a secondary analysis of the MOPED international, prospective, observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, prospective, observational study, continuing metformin (but not SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 receptor agonists) on the day of elective surgery in patients with type 2 diabetes was associated with a small but statistically significant increase in days alive at home at 30 days, suggesting potential benefit to not withholding metformin perioperatively.

foamed.title: Latest in Pediatric Emergency Care Journals - JournalFeed
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational summary reviews the recognition and stepwise management of pediatric beta blocker and calcium channel blocker toxicity, emphasizing early identification, initial therapies (activated charcoal, atropine, calcium, glucagon), escalation to vasopressors and high-dose insulin, and consideration of ECMO or intralipid rescue in refractory cases, without presenting new clinical data.

foamed.source_name: WFSA Anaesthesia Tutorial of the Week
foamed.title: Perioperative Care of Transgender and Gender-Diverse Individuals
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational tutorial outlining key considerations in the perioperative anaesthetic management of transgender and gender-diverse individuals, including preoperative assessment, hormone therapy effects, airway and pharmacokinetic challenges, pain management, DVT prophylaxis, and ethical issues, but does not present new clinical research data.

deep_dives.title: Effect on neonatal outcomes of maintenance of maternal blood pressure targets with noradrenaline after spinal anaesthesia for caesarean delivery: a multicentre, randomised, controlled trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large multicenter randomized controlled trial, maintaining maternal systolic blood pressure within 90% (vs. 80%) of baseline using noradrenaline after spinal anesthesia for cesarean delivery reduced the incidence of neonatal acidosis and maternal hypotension without increasing adverse effects, supporting a higher blood pressure target for better maternal and neonatal outcomes.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Maintaining maternal systolic blood pressure above 90% of baseline with noradrenaline during caesarean delivery under spinal anaesthesia reduces neonatal acidaemia and maternal hypotension compared to a target of 80%.

- Study type: Multicentre, randomised, controlled trial
- Population/setting: Women with term pregnancies undergoing caesarean delivery under spinal anaesthesia at multiple centres (N=1183)
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Maintenance of systolic blood pressure within 90% of baseline (SBP-90% group) versus 80% of baseline (SBP-80% group) using noradrenaline boluses
- Primary endpoints: Umbilical artery pH
- Key results: No difference in mean umbilical artery pH between groups (7.33 vs. 7.33, p=0.11), but fewer cases of pH < 7.2 in SBP-90% group (0.5% vs. 2.2%, p=0.020); SBP-90% group had less maternal hypotension (36.1% vs. 70.5%, p<0.001), severe hypotension (3.9% vs. 7.8%, p=0.006), and nausea/vomiting (9.8% vs. 18.4%, p<0.001), but received more noradrenaline boluses.
- Limitations:
- Long-term neonatal outcomes not reported
- Study limited to term pregnancies
- Potential for unmeasured confounders
- Why this matters: Optimizing maternal blood pressure targets during caesarean delivery can improve neonatal and maternal outcomes, informing anaesthetic management protocols.

deep_dives.title: Technology-Enhanced Strategies to Optimize Positive End-Expiratory Pressure in Patients Receiving Invasive Mechanical Ventilation: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis found that technology-enhanced PEEP optimization strategies, including esophageal balloon measurement and electrical impedance tomography, did not reduce the duration of mechanical ventilation compared to standard care (very low-certainty evidence), suggesting limited clinical benefit for these advanced approaches in current practice.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** This systematic review and meta-analysis found that technology-enhanced PEEP optimization strategies, including esophageal balloon measurement and electrical impedance tomography, did not reduce the duration of mechanical ventilation compared to standard care (very low-certainty evidence), suggesting limited clinical benefit for these advanced approaches in current practice.

BOTTOM LINE: Technology-enhanced PEEP optimization strategies may reduce 28-day mortality in mechanically ventilated ICU patients, but do not reduce duration of mechanical ventilation, and evidence certainty is very low.

- Study type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized studies
- Population/setting: Adults and children receiving invasive mechanical ventilation in ICU settings (34 studies, 2951 patients); no evidence found in children.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Technology-enhanced PEEP optimization strategies (e.g., esophageal balloon, electrical impedance tomography, pressure-volume curve analysis, closed-loop ventilation) versus standard care or alternative PEEP optimization strategies.
- Primary endpoints: Duration of mechanical ventilation (primary); secondary endpoints included 28-day mortality and other clinical and efficacy outcomes.
- Key results: No reduction in duration of mechanical ventilation (mean difference -0.06 days; 95% CI, -0.20 to 0.09; very low-certainty evidence); technology-enhanced PEEP optimization reduced 28-day mortality (risk ratio 0.69; 95% CI, 0.52-0.93; very low-certainty evidence); no significant differences for other outcomes; no cost-effectiveness data.
- Limitations:
- Very low or low certainty of evidence
- Few studies reported primary outcome
- No evidence in children
- No cost-effectiveness data
- Majority of studies at risk of bias
- Why this matters: Clinicians should be cautious in adopting technology-enhanced PEEP optimization strategies as current evidence for improved outcomes is uncertain and further high-quality trials are needed.

deep_dives.title: Efficacy and safety of different vasopressor infusions on feto-maternal outcomes in normotensive patients undergoing caesarean delivery: a systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large network meta-analysis of 55 RCTs (n=5487) found that infusions of metaraminol, noradrenaline, phenylephrine, and adrenaline are clearly superior to no vasopressor for preventing maternal hypotension during caesarean delivery, with mephentermine and metaraminol best preserving fetal acid-base status, supporting consideration of these agents for optimizing feto-maternal outcomes.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Continuous infusions of α-agonists with mild β-activity, such as noradrenaline and metaraminol, are preferable to mixed adrenergic agonists like ephedrine for preventing maternal hypotension during caesarean delivery, but evidence for fetal outcomes is limited.

- Study type: Systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials
- Population/setting: Normotensive adult patients undergoing caesarean delivery under spinal or combined spinal and epidural anaesthesia (55 trials, 5487 patients)
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Prophylactic vasopressor infusions (metaraminol, noradrenaline, phenylephrine, adrenaline, mephentermine, ephedrine) versus control (no active vasopressor infusion)
- Primary endpoints: Incidence of maternal hypotension and umbilical artery base excess
- Key results: Metaraminol, noradrenaline, phenylephrine, and adrenaline were definitely superior to control for preventing hypotension; mephentermine and metaraminol provided superior preservation of umbilical arterial and venous acid-base balance.
- Limitations:
- Evidence base for fetal outcomes remains limited.
- Heterogeneity in vasopressor regimens and outcome reporting across included trials.
- Why this matters: Optimizing vasopressor choice during caesarean delivery can improve maternal haemodynamic stability and potentially fetal acid-base status.

deep_dives.title: Epidemiology, ventilation management, and clinical outcomes in children (PRoVENT-PED): first results from the 10-year, investigator-initiated, international, multicentre, prospective cohort study.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large, international, prospective cohort study provides the first robust, pediatric-specific data on ventilation management and outcomes in critically ill children, highlighting that PARDS occurs in 11% of invasively ventilated cases and is most common in preschool-aged children; these findings fill a major evidence gap and can inform age-appropriate ventilation strategies in pediatric intensive care.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Ventilation management in critically ill children varies by age and PARDS status, with PEEP, driving pressure, and FiO2 identified as modifiable factors associated with 28-day ICU mortality.

- Study type: International, multicentre, observational prospective cohort study
- Population/setting: 1427 children under 18 years old, invasively ventilated for at least 12 hours in 83 ICUs across 34 countries
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Ventilation management strategies (including PEEP, driving pressure, FiO2) across age groups and PARDS status; comparison between children with and without PARDS
- Primary endpoints: 28-day intensive care unit (ICU) mortality
- Key results: 28-day ICU mortality was 14% overall, lowest in neonates (3%) and higher in PARDS patients (27%) compared to non-PARDS (12%); PEEP, driving pressure, and FiO2 were independently associated with ICU mortality.
- Limitations:
- Observational design limits causal inference
- Data limited to standard clinical care and medical records
- Exclusion of preterm infants and those on ECMO
- Why this matters: Identifying modifiable ventilation factors associated with mortality can inform strategies to improve outcomes in critically ill, invasively ventilated children.

deep_dives.title: Prophylactic esketamine to reduce postpartum depression in primiparae: A multicentre, double-blind, randomised clinical trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this multicentre, double-blind RCT of primiparae undergoing elective caesarean section, prophylactic esketamine significantly reduced the incidence of postpartum depression at 7 days and overall within 3 months, though not at individual 1, 2, or 3 month timepoints, suggesting esketamine may offer short-term PPD prevention in low-risk women, with mostly mild central nervous system side effects.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Peri-operative adjunctive administration of esketamine reduces the short-term incidence of postpartum depression in primiparae without prenatal depression predisposition, but the effect does not persist beyond 7 days postpartum.

- Study type: Prospective, double-blind, multicentre, randomised controlled trial
- Population/setting: Primiparae scheduled for elective caesarean section with EPDS score <10 at three academic hospitals
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Single dose i.v. esketamine (0.25 mg/kg) plus 80 mg esketamine in 24-h PCIA versus equal volume saline
- Primary endpoints: Total incidence of postpartum depression within 3 months postpartum
- Key results: Esketamine group had a lower total PPD incidence (11.59%) compared to control (20.89%) [adjusted RR 0.57; 95% CI 0.35–0.94; P=0.028]; significant reduction at 7 days postpartum (4.89% vs 15.19%; adjusted RR 0.32; 95% CI 0.15–0.72; P=0.005); no significant differences at 1, 2, and 3 months; mild central nervous events were more common with esketamine.
- Limitations:
- Effect did not persist beyond 7 days postpartum
- Study limited to primiparae without prenatal depression predisposition
- Adverse events such as dizziness, hallucination, and dissociation observed
- Why this matters: Esketamine may offer a short-term preventive strategy for postpartum depression in women at low risk, potentially improving early postpartum mental health.

deep_dives.title: Sex-related disparities in post-cardiac arrest: a cohort study.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large prospective cohort study found that while women had worse unadjusted neurological outcomes after cardiac arrest, sex was not independently associated with mortality or neurological recovery after adjusting for arrest characteristics, suggesting that outcome disparities are due to underlying clinical factors rather than sex itself. This evidence supports focusing on modifiable arrest characteristics rather than sex in post-cardiac arrest prognostication and care.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Sex was not independently associated with neurological recovery or mortality after cardiac arrest when adjusting for arrest characteristics.

- Study type: Prospective cohort study
- Population/setting: 944 consecutive comatose adults admitted after cardiac arrest to Lausanne University Hospital (CHUV), Switzerland, between January 2016 and February 2025, surviving the first 24 hours.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Comparison of outcomes between women and men after cardiac arrest.
- Primary endpoints: ICU mortality, 3-month mortality, and favorable neurological outcome (modified Rankin Scale 0-3 at 3 months).
- Key results: Women had lower unadjusted rates of favorable neurological outcome (39.6% vs. 48.0%, p=0.013), but after adjustment, sex was not independently associated with ICU mortality (OR 1.08, 95% CI: 0.80-1.46), 3-month mortality (OR 1.05, 95% CI: 0.77-1.43), or favorable outcome (OR 1.00, 95% CI: 0.73-1.37).
- Limitations:
- Single-center study
- Potential residual confounding
- Only patients surviving first 24 hours included
- Why this matters: Understanding that sex is not an independent predictor of post-cardiac arrest outcomes can help guide equitable prognostication and management.

pubmed.title: Effectiveness of McGRATH MAC Video Laryngoscopy for First-Attempt Intubation by Anesthesia Trainees in Infants: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized controlled trial of 124 infants, anesthesia trainees achieved significantly higher first-attempt intubation success rates using the McGrath MAC video laryngoscope compared to direct laryngoscopy, suggesting that video laryngoscopy may improve intubation outcomes for trainees in this challenging population. This evidence supports considering video laryngoscopy as a preferred tool for infant intubation training.

pubmed.title: Aroxybutynin and atomoxetine (AD109) for obstructive sleep apnea: a randomized phase 3 trial (SynAIRgy).
pubmed.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, randomized, placebo-controlled phase 3 trial, the oral combination AD109 (aroxybutynin/atomoxetine) significantly reduced apnea-hypopnea index and improved oxygenation measures over 26 weeks in OSA patients intolerant to PAP, but had higher discontinuation rates due to adverse events; this provides moderate-strength evidence for AD109 as an alternative OSA therapy, though tolerability may limit its clinical use.

pubmed.title: Effects of an early restrictive versus liberal fluid strategy on long-term patient-centered outcomes in sepsis-induced hypotension: an open-label, randomized controlled trial.
pubmed.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized controlled trial, early restrictive versus liberal fluid resuscitation strategies in sepsis-induced hypotension resulted in no significant differences in long-term cognitive, functional, or quality-of-life outcomes at 6 or 12 months, suggesting that either approach is reasonable from a patient-centered perspective. This evidence supports flexibility in fluid management strategies for sepsis-induced hypotension without concern for long-term patient-centered harm.

pubmed.title: Routine tracheal extubation location after anesthesia in adult patients: a nationwide French mixed-methods survey of current practice.
pubmed.bottom_line: This nationwide French survey found significant variability in where anesthesia professionals perform routine tracheal extubation (operating room vs. PACU), highlighting a lack of standardization and suggesting that local staffing and workflow considerations drive practice; these findings underscore the need for clearer guidelines to optimize patient safety and operating room efficiency.

pubmed.title: General anesthesia with intubation and emergency endoscopy outcomes in patients with upper gastrointestinal bleeding: an inverse probability of treatment weighting study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this retrospective, single-center study using inverse probability of treatment weighting, general anesthesia with intubation for emergency upper GI endoscopy in bleeding patients was associated with faster hemostasis, better tolerance, and fewer repeat procedures, without increased complications or mortality, suggesting it may be a safe and effective option in selected cases.

pubmed.title: Association of Chronotropic Competence With Intraoperative Hypotension and Vasopressor Requirement: A Retrospective Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This retrospective cohort study found that preoperative chronotropic incompetence, as assessed by CPET, was not associated with increased intraoperative hypotension or vasopressor requirements, suggesting that chronotropic incompetence may not be a useful predictor for these intraoperative outcomes in surgical patients.

pubmed.title: Pulmonary complications with individualised vs. fixed positive end-expiratory pressure in older patients recovering from lung cancer surgery: a randomised trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized trial of 400 older patients recovering from lung cancer surgery, individualized PEEP improved intraoperative lung mechanics and oxygenation but did not reduce the incidence of postoperative pulmonary complications compared to fixed PEEP, suggesting no clinical benefit for individualized PEEP in this context.

pubmed.title: Prehospital Use of Non-Invasive Ventilation for Acute Respiratory Failure due to Acute Exacerbation of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease. A Randomised Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This small, prematurely terminated randomized trial suggests that prehospital non-invasive ventilation for acute COPD exacerbations may improve early physiological outcomes (arterial pH), but the evidence is very limited due to low recruitment and logistical challenges, indicating that routine prehospital use is not yet supported and remains difficult to implement.

pubmed.title: Bedside identification of subphenotypes in acute respiratory failure (PHIND): a multicentre, observational cohort study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, prospective multicenter study demonstrates that rapid bedside measurement of IL-6, soluble TNFR1, and plasma bicarbonate can reliably identify hyperinflammatory and hypoinflammatory subphenotypes in acute respiratory failure, which are associated with significantly different 60-day mortality rates; this enables timely, individualized risk stratification and may guide future targeted therapies in ARDS.

pubmed.title: Effects of inflammatory phenotypes in acute respiratory distress syndrome on mortality and partitioning of lung and chest wall mechanics in patients in the USA and Canada: a retrospective cohort study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective cohort study, patients with ARDS and a hyperinflammatory phenotype had significantly higher 60-day mortality and distinct lung mechanics compared to those with a hypoinflammatory phenotype, suggesting that inflammatory phenotyping may help tailor ventilation strategies and improve outcomes.

pubmed.title: Multisensory virtual reality reduces postoperative delirium in elderly patients undergoing laparoscopic gastrointestinal surgery: A single-centre randomised controlled trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this single-centre randomized controlled trial of 342 elderly patients undergoing laparoscopic gastrointestinal surgery, a preoperative 20-minute multisensory virtual reality intervention significantly reduced postoperative delirium incidence compared to standard care (9.4% vs. 19.2%), suggesting a promising, non-pharmacological strategy to lower delirium risk in this population.

pubmed.title: Comparison of analgesia efficacy between single-orifice and multiorifice wire-reinforced catheters for labour analgesia with the dural puncture epidural technique: A double-blind randomised controlled trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this double-blind randomized controlled trial, single-orifice wire-reinforced catheters provided significantly faster onset of effective labour analgesia compared to multiorifice catheters using the dural puncture epidural technique, suggesting catheter design impacts analgesic efficacy and may influence clinical choice for optimal pain management in labour.

pubmed.title: Proactive haemodynamic management using the hypotension prediction index during caesarean section: a randomised controlled study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized controlled study of 171 patients undergoing elective caesarean section, proactive haemodynamic management using the hypotension prediction index significantly reduced intra-operative hypotension compared to standard oscillometric and continuous non-invasive arterial pressure monitoring, suggesting that this machine learning-based approach may improve maternal outcomes by better preventing hypotension-related adverse effects.

pubmed.title: Pantoprazole Use in Invasively Ventilated Patients With Septic Shock: A Protocol and Statistical Analysis Plan.
pubmed.bottom_line: This protocol outlines a planned post hoc subgroup analysis of the large, randomized REVISE trial to assess the efficacy and safety of pantoprazole versus placebo for stress ulcer prophylaxis in invasively ventilated patients with septic shock, addressing a significant evidence gap; results will inform clinical decisions on acid suppression in this high-risk population, but no new outcome data are presented yet.

pubmed.title: Developing a Core Outcome Measurement Set for Adult ICU Patients, the CoreMS-ICU-A Protocol.
pubmed.bottom_line: This protocol outlines the planned development of a standardized core outcome measurement set (COMS) for adults acutely admitted to the ICU, aiming to improve consistency and comparability in clinical research; while it does not present new evidence yet, its implementation could enhance the quality and relevance of ICU trial outcomes.

pubmed.title: Association of Suspected Aspiration During of Out-Of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest With Development of Early-Onset Pneumonia.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this small, single-center prospective study, prehospital suspicion of aspiration during out-of-hospital cardiac arrest was not significantly associated with early-onset pneumonia in ICU survivors, but the study may have been underpowered to detect a clinically meaningful difference, so larger studies are needed to confirm these findings.

pubmed.title: Safety and antimicrobial efficacy of CSL787, a novel nebulized IgG therapy, in patients with bronchiectasis: results from a randomized phase 1 trial.
pubmed.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized phase 1 trial, nebulized CSL787 (IgG therapy) was found to be safe and demonstrated antimicrobial activity in patients with bronchiectasis, providing early evidence that supports further investigation of this novel inhaled immunotherapy for airway infections.

pubmed.title: A statistical model for lung function trajectory and mortality in patients with fibrotic interstitial lung disease.
pubmed.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: A new Bayesian joint mixed-effects disease progression model more accurately estimates lung function decline and mortality risk in fibrotic interstitial lung disease by accounting for both FVC trajectory and death, reducing bias compared to traditional models; this approach can improve the precision and validity of treatment effect estimates in clinical trials.

pubmed.title: Glucocorticoids for Patients With Acute Decompensated Heart Failure: A Protocol for a Systematic Review With Meta-Analysis and Trial Sequential Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This is a protocol for a forthcoming systematic review and meta-analysis that will evaluate the effects of adding glucocorticoids to standard care in acute decompensated heart failure; no new clinical evidence is presented yet, but the results will help clarify the efficacy and safety of glucocorticoids in this setting.

pubmed.title: Early Versus Late Wake-Up Call After Out-Of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: Protocol for a Multicenter Randomized Comparison Within the Danish Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (DANOHCA) Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This protocol describes a forthcoming multicenter randomized trial comparing early (≤6 h) versus late (28–36 h) wake-up calls after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, aiming to determine the optimal timing for sedation interruption and neurological assessment; results will provide high-quality evidence to guide post-resuscitation care and potentially improve survival and neurological outcomes.

pubmed.title: Biological subphenotypes in severe acute hypoxaemic respiratory failure and acute respiratory distress syndrome using rapid prospective classification (SPARC) in the USA: a multicentre, observational, study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This multicenter prospective study demonstrates that rapid, real-time classification of biological subphenotypes in severe acute hypoxaemic respiratory failure and ARDS using plasma biomarkers and clinical variables is feasible, potentially enabling more personalized and timely treatment strategies in critical care settings.

pubmed.title: Association between postoperative delirium in the postanaesthesia care unit and subsequent ward delirium: A retrospective cohort study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective cohort study of older surgical patients, postoperative delirium detected in the PACU was strongly and independently associated with a higher risk of subsequent ward delirium, in-hospital falls, and 30-day mortality, highlighting the clinical importance of early delirium screening and intervention in the immediate postoperative period.

pubmed.title: Prospective Study of Postoperative Respiratory Depression Metrics After Cesarean Delivery Among Women Receiving Intrathecal Morphine.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this prospective cohort study of women receiving intrathecal morphine after cesarean delivery, both 50 µg and 150 µg doses were associated with frequent bradypnea events, but event rates and durations were similar between groups, suggesting that current SOAP monitoring recommendations may not need to differ by dose; however, hypoxemia duration was longer with 150 µg, highlighting the need for vigilant postoperative respiratory monitoring regardless of morphine

pubmed.title: The Influence of Low-Dose Dexmedetomidine on Recovery Times Following Sedation for Vitreoretinal Surgery: A Retrospective Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective cohort study found that low-dose dexmedetomidine for sedation during vitreoretinal surgery did not prolong postanesthesia care unit discharge times compared to other sedatives, suggesting it is a safe option without delaying recovery in this setting. This evidence supports the clinical use of dexmedetomidine for ophthalmic procedures without concern for extended recovery.

pubmed.title: Modeling the Cost-Effectiveness of Incorporating Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Strategy in Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest Patients.
pubmed.bottom_line: This simulation-based cost-effectiveness analysis found that implementing extracorporeal cardiopulmonary resuscitation (ECPR) for select out-of-hospital cardiac arrest patients in Singapore is likely cost-effective (ICER $34,320/QALY, below the local threshold), suggesting ECPR could be a viable addition to current resuscitation strategies if resources allow.

foamed.title: TTL Tip 15: Alternative vascular access
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational article reviews alternative vascular access options in trauma resuscitation—such as external jugular cannulation, ultrasound-guided access, central lines, and intraosseous access—but does not present new clinical data, instead providing practical considerations for clinicians when standard peripheral access is challenging.

foamed.title: Multiplanar gastric ultrasound assessment may improve aspiration risk prediction before anesthesia
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: A prospective observational study found that neither the abdominal aorta nor inferior vena cava (IVC) plane is consistently superior for gastric ultrasound assessment before anesthesia, but using the higher-measured gastric volume from either plane may provide the most accurate estimate of actual gastric volume and aspiration risk.

foamed.title: Bubble Wrap PLUS – June 2026
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational resource providing a curated list of recent pediatric journal articles and discussion questions for journal clubs, without presenting new clinical data or study results.

foamed.title: Persistent Post-Concussion Syndrome
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article discusses the challenges of managing persistent post-concussion syndrome (PPCS) in children and adolescents, highlighting that while most recover quickly, about 30% experience prolonged symptoms, and there remains limited high-quality evidence to guide optimal treatment for this group, with recent research exploring multimodal, symptom-targeted approaches.

deep_dives.title: Effects of an early restrictive versus liberal fluid strategy on long-term patient-centered outcomes in sepsis-induced hypotension: an open-label, randomized controlled trial.
deep_dives.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized controlled trial, early restrictive versus liberal fluid resuscitation strategies in sepsis-induced hypotension resulted in no significant differences in long-term cognitive, functional, or quality-of-life outcomes at 6 or 12 months, suggesting that either approach is reasonable from a patient-centered perspective. This evidence supports flexibility in fluid management strategies for sepsis-induced hypotension without concern for long-term patient-centered harm.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: In patients with sepsis-induced hypotension, early restrictive and liberal fluid resuscitation strategies resulted in similar long-term cognitive and physical outcomes.

- Study type: Prospective, open-label, randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: Patients with sepsis-induced hypotension enrolled in the NIH NHLBI PETAL Network's CLOVERS trial; 898 participants included, 702 analyzed at 6 months.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Restrictive fluid resuscitation strategy versus liberal fluid resuscitation strategy.
- Primary endpoints: Long-term functional outcomes at 6 and 12 months, including cognitive function, executive function, disability status, mobility, and health-related quality of life.
- Key results: No significant differences between groups at 6 months in cognitive function (trimmed mean difference 0.11, 95% CI -1.44 to 1.70), executive function (0.38, 95% CI -0.97 to 1.76), disability status (0.03, 95% CI -0.84 to 0.90), mobility (0.72, 95% CI -2.20 to 3.64), or health-related quality of life (-0.01, 95% CI -0.07 to 0.06); outcomes were also similar at 12 months.
- Limitations:
- Loss to follow-up for 196 participants.
- Only 57% of the original CLOVERS trial participants were included.
- Open-label design may introduce bias.
- Why this matters: This study provides evidence that the choice between restrictive and liberal fluid strategies in early sepsis-induced hypotension does not impact long-term patient-centered outcomes, informing fluid management decisions in sepsis care.

deep_dives.title: Pulmonary complications with individualised vs. fixed positive end-expiratory pressure in older patients recovering from lung cancer surgery: a randomised trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized trial of 400 older patients recovering from lung cancer surgery, individualized PEEP improved intraoperative lung mechanics and oxygenation but did not reduce the incidence of postoperative pulmonary complications compared to fixed PEEP, suggesting no clinical benefit for individualized PEEP in this context.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** In this randomized trial of 400 older patients recovering from lung cancer surgery, individualized PEEP improved intraoperative lung mechanics and oxygenation but did not reduce the incidence of postoperative pulmonary complications compared to fixed PEEP, suggesting no clinical benefit for individualized PEEP in this context.

BOTTOM LINE: Individualised positive end-expiratory pressure did not reduce postoperative pulmonary complications compared to fixed PEEP in older patients after lung cancer surgery.

- Study type: Randomised trial
- Population/setting: 400 patients aged ≥60 years undergoing lung cancer surgery
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Individualised PEEP (PEEPIND group) vs. fixed PEEP of 5 cmH2O (PEEP5 group)
- Primary endpoints: Incidence of postoperative pulmonary complications
- Key results: Postoperative pulmonary complications occurred in 28% of the PEEPIND group and 25% of the PEEP5 group (risk ratio 1.09, 95% CI 0.79-1.52, p = 0.60); individualised PEEP reduced driving pressures and improved intra-operative oxygenation but did not affect complication rates.
- Limitations:
- Single-center study design not specified
- No long-term follow-up reported
- Potential for unmeasured confounders
- Why this matters: Optimising intra-operative ventilation with individualised PEEP does not translate into fewer pulmonary complications post-lung cancer surgery in older adults.

deep_dives.title: Multisensory virtual reality reduces postoperative delirium in elderly patients undergoing laparoscopic gastrointestinal surgery: A single-centre randomised controlled trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this single-centre randomized controlled trial of 342 elderly patients undergoing laparoscopic gastrointestinal surgery, a preoperative 20-minute multisensory virtual reality intervention significantly reduced postoperative delirium incidence compared to standard care (9.4% vs. 19.2%), suggesting a promising, non-pharmacological strategy to lower delirium risk in this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Pre-operative multisensory virtual reality significantly reduces the incidence of postoperative delirium in elderly patients undergoing elective laparoscopic gastrointestinal surgery.

- Study type: Randomised controlled trial
- Population/setting: 342 patients aged 60 years or older undergoing elective laparoscopic gastrointestinal surgery at a single tertiary hospital in China.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Multisensory virtual reality session plus standard care versus standard care alone, both administered pre-operatively for 20 minutes.
- Primary endpoints: Incidence of postoperative delirium (POD) assessed twice daily for three postoperative days using the Confusion Assessment Method (CAM).
- Key results: POD incidence was 9.4% (16/170) in the multisensory virtual reality group versus 19.2% (33/172) in the standard care group (P = 0.010); anxiety scores were also significantly reduced (P = 0.001); no serious adverse events occurred.
- Limitations:
- Single-centre study limits generalizability.
- Short follow-up period of only three postoperative days.
- No long-term cognitive outcomes reported.
- Why this matters: This study suggests a simple, non-pharmacological intervention can effectively reduce postoperative delirium risk in elderly surgical patients, addressing a common and serious complication.

deep_dives.title: Aroxybutynin and atomoxetine (AD109) for obstructive sleep apnea: a randomized phase 3 trial (SynAIRgy).
deep_dives.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, randomized, placebo-controlled phase 3 trial, the oral combination AD109 (aroxybutynin/atomoxetine) significantly reduced apnea-hypopnea index and improved oxygenation measures over 26 weeks in OSA patients intolerant to PAP, but had higher discontinuation rates due to adverse events; this provides moderate-strength evidence for AD109 as an alternative OSA therapy, though tolerability may limit its clinical use.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: AD109 significantly reduced apnea-hypopnea index and improved oxygenation in OSA patients unable to use PAP, but was associated with higher rates of adverse events compared to placebo.

- Study type: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-arm phase 3 trial
- Population/setting: Adults with mild-to-severe obstructive sleep apnea intolerant to or refusing PAP therapy, enrolled across 69 centers (N=646, median age 58, 49.3% female, median BMI 32.4 kg/m2).
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: AD109 (aroxybutynin 2.5 mg/atomoxetine 75 mg) vs placebo, both administered orally for 26 weeks.
- Primary endpoints: Change from baseline to week 26 in apnea-hypopnea index (AHI).
- Key results: At week 26, AD109 reduced AHI by a mean of -4.0 events/hour (95% CI, -6.4 to -1.6; P = .001), with a 44.1% vs 17.6% decrease from baseline (P < .0001) compared to placebo; improvements were also seen in ODI and hypoxic burden, but not in PROMIS-Fatigue; 21.2% of AD109 and 3.1% of placebo participants discontinued due to adverse events.
- Limitations:
- High discontinuation rate due to adverse events in the AD109 group.
- No significant improvement in patient-reported fatigue.
- Long-term safety beyond 26 weeks not assessed.
- Why this matters: AD109 offers a potential oral alternative for OSA patients unable to tolerate PAP, addressing an unmet clinical need.

pubmed.title: A Comparative Study of Radiation Exposure in Conventional and Robotic Bronchoscopy.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective study found that robotic bronchoscopy using C-arm-based tomosynthesis exposes operators to significantly higher radiation than conventional bronchoscopy or robotic bronchoscopy with standard fluoroscopy, while staff exposure remains minimal; these findings highlight the need for radiation safety considerations when selecting bronchoscopic techniques, especially with advanced imaging modalities.

pubmed.title: Association of a Polygenic Risk Score with Diagnosis and Outcomes in Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis.
pubmed.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: This large, multi-cohort observational study shows that a polygenic risk score (PRS) is strongly associated with both the diagnosis of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) and worse outcomes (mortality or lung transplant), suggesting that PRS could help identify individuals at higher risk for IPF and poor prognosis, though further research is needed before clinical implementation.

pubmed.title: Patients' Perspectives and Experiences of Participating in Anesthesia Research: A Qualitative Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This qualitative study found that patients generally viewed participation in anesthesia comparative effectiveness research as positive and non-intrusive, but identified modifiable barriers such as survey language and blinding; understanding these patient perspectives can help improve recruitment and retention in future anesthesia trials.

pubmed.title: Variation in Emergency Department Experience With Pediatric Critical Illness.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective study found that most emergency departments (EDs) rarely manage critically ill children, and only a small proportion of such cases at low-volume EDs are close enough to be diverted to high-volume centers, suggesting that universal direct transport to high-volume EDs is not a feasible strategy for improving pediatric critical care access. This highlights the need for alternative approaches to ensure high-quality emergency care for critically ill children in diverse settings.

pubmed.title: Angiography-Based Physiology to Guide Coronary Revascularization.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, randomized noninferiority trial, angiography-based vessel fractional flow reserve (vFFR) was as safe and effective as traditional pressure-wire-based FFR for guiding revascularization in patients with intermediate coronary lesions, suggesting vFFR is a viable, less invasive alternative for clinical decision-making.

pubmed.title: Rituximab versus Ocrelizumab in Newly Diagnosed Relapsing Multiple Sclerosis.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this phase 3, double-blind, noninferiority trial, rituximab was shown to be noninferior to ocrelizumab in suppressing MRI disease activity in newly diagnosed relapsing multiple sclerosis, with similar relapse and disability outcomes but a higher overall infection rate with rituximab; this provides strong evidence supporting rituximab as an effective alternative to ocrelizumab in this population.

pubmed.title: Angiography-Derived Fractional Flow Reserve to Guide PCI.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large international noninferiority trial, angiography-derived FFR (FFRangio) was as safe and effective as pressure-wire-based FFR for guiding PCI in patients with intermediate coronary stenoses, suggesting that FFRangio can simplify physiological assessment without compromising 1-year clinical outcomes. This evidence supports broader adoption of FFRangio in clinical practice to guide revascularization decisions.

pubmed.title: Adjuvant Pembrolizumab plus Belzutifan for Renal-Cell Carcinoma.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large phase 3 trial, adding belzutifan to adjuvant pembrolizumab significantly improved disease-free survival in patients with resected clear-cell renal-cell carcinoma at high risk for recurrence, though overall survival benefit was not yet observed at interim analysis; this combination may offer a new standard for reducing recurrence risk, but increased toxicity should be considered.

foamed.title: Fascial plane blocks for rib fractures: a new era in trauma analgesia
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational overview discussing the potential benefits and expanding role of thoracic fascial plane blocks for rib fracture analgesia, referencing a scoping review by Niazi et al.; it does not present new primary clinical data but summarizes current evidence and evolving practices in trauma pain management.

foamed.title: Want to write for DFTB?
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an informational and educational resource outlining how to contribute written content to Don’t Forget The Bubbles, including submission guidelines and the value of participation, and does not present new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.title: EMCrit Wee – A Second Opinion on Hyperbaric Oxygen (HBO) for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning (CO)
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This EMCrit episode provides an expert discussion highlighting that carbon monoxide poisoning is primarily an inflammatory disease affecting the brain and heart, and suggests that hyperbaric oxygen therapy at pressures of 2.5–3.0 atmospheres may be necessary to blunt this cascade, while blood carboxyhemoglobin levels do not reliably indicate tissue injury severity.

foamed.title: Contrast Controversies: Contrast Media Reactions in Emergency Medicine
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational blog post reviews updated 2025 Canadian, American, and European guidelines on the prevention and management of contrast media hypersensitivity reactions in emergency medicine, providing a practical, evidence-based approach but does not present new clinical study data or original research findings.

deep_dives.title: Angiography-Based Physiology to Guide Coronary Revascularization.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, randomized noninferiority trial, angiography-based vessel fractional flow reserve (vFFR) was as safe and effective as traditional pressure-wire-based FFR for guiding revascularization in patients with intermediate coronary lesions, suggesting vFFR is a viable, less invasive alternative for clinical decision-making.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: vFFR-guided revascularization is noninferior to FFR-guided revascularization for patients with intermediate coronary lesions regarding major adverse cardiac events at 1 year.

- Study type: International, open-label, randomized, noninferiority trial
- Population/setting: Patients with intermediate coronary-artery lesions (30-80% diameter stenosis) presenting with chronic or acute coronary syndromes at 37 sites in Europe (N=2211)
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: vFFR-guided revascularization versus FFR-guided revascularization
- Primary endpoints: Composite of death from any cause, any myocardial infarction, or any revascularization at 1 year
- Key results: Primary endpoint occurred in 7.5% of both vFFR and FFR groups (risk difference, -0.02 percentage points; 95% CI, -2.25 to 2.21; P = 0.004 for noninferiority); serious adverse events were similar between groups.
- Limitations:
- Open-label design may introduce bias
- Study population limited to European centers
- Follow-up limited to 1 year
- Why this matters: vFFR offers a less invasive alternative to traditional FFR, potentially simplifying physiological assessment and guiding coronary revascularization without compromising clinical outcomes.

deep_dives.title: Adjuvant Pembrolizumab plus Belzutifan for Renal-Cell Carcinoma.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
