deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large phase 3 trial, adding belzutifan to adjuvant pembrolizumab significantly improved disease-free survival in patients with resected clear-cell renal-cell carcinoma at high risk for recurrence, though overall survival benefit was not yet observed at interim analysis; this combination may offer a new standard for reducing recurrence risk, but increased toxicity should be considered.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Adjuvant pembrolizumab plus belzutifan significantly improves disease-free survival compared to pembrolizumab alone in patients with resected clear-cell renal-cell carcinoma at increased risk for recurrence, but with higher rates of severe adverse events.

- Study type: Phase 3, double-blind, randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: Patients with resected clear-cell renal-cell carcinoma at increased risk for recurrence; multicenter trial with 1841 participants
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Pembrolizumab plus belzutifan versus pembrolizumab plus placebo
- Primary endpoints: Disease-free survival as assessed by the investigator
- Key results: Pembrolizumab-belzutifan group had a hazard ratio for disease recurrence or death of 0.72 (95% CI, 0.59 to 0.87; P<0.001) and estimated 24-month disease-free survival of 80.7% versus 73.7% for pembrolizumab-placebo; grade 3 or higher adverse events occurred in 52.1% versus 30.2%, respectively.
- Limitations:
- Interim analysis with only 29% of final-analysis events observed
- Overall survival difference not statistically significant at interim analysis
- Increased toxicity in the combination group
- Why this matters: This study suggests that adding belzutifan to adjuvant pembrolizumab may further reduce recurrence risk in high-risk renal-cell carcinoma, but clinicians must weigh the increased risk of severe adverse events.

deep_dives.title: Rituximab versus Ocrelizumab in Newly Diagnosed Relapsing Multiple Sclerosis.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this phase 3, double-blind, noninferiority trial, rituximab was shown to be noninferior to ocrelizumab in suppressing MRI disease activity in newly diagnosed relapsing multiple sclerosis, with similar relapse and disability outcomes but a higher overall infection rate with rituximab; this provides strong evidence supporting rituximab as an effective alternative to ocrelizumab in this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Rituximab was noninferior to ocrelizumab in suppressing MRI-detected disease activity in newly diagnosed relapsing multiple sclerosis over 24 months, with similar rates of serious adverse events.

- Study type: Phase 3 multicenter double-blind noninferiority randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: 218 adults with newly diagnosed relapsing multiple sclerosis and recent disease activity at multiple centers
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Rituximab every 6 months versus ocrelizumab every 6 months for 24 months
- Primary endpoints: Absence of new or enlarging lesions on T2-weighted MRI from month 6 to month 24
- Key results: 92.2% of rituximab and 94.8% of ocrelizumab participants had no new or enlarging T2 lesions (risk difference -2.6 percentage points; 95% CI, -9.4 to 4.3), meeting noninferiority; relapse rates, disability, and cognitive outcomes were similar; infections were more common with rituximab (82% vs 69%), but serious adverse events were similar (8% vs 7%).
- Limitations:
- Short follow-up duration (24 months)
- Infection rates higher in rituximab group
- Study population limited to newly diagnosed patients
- Why this matters: This study provides direct comparative evidence supporting rituximab as a noninferior and potentially more accessible alternative to ocrelizumab for initial treatment of relapsing multiple sclerosis.

pubmed.title: Efficacy of higher-dose versus lower-dose corticosteroids in community-acquired pneumonia: a systematic review and network meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and network meta-analysis of 32 RCTs (9,746 patients) found that both higher- and lower-dose corticosteroids reduce short-term mortality in hospitalized community-acquired pneumonia compared to placebo or usual care, but there is no clear mortality difference between the two dosing strategies; evidence is moderate to low quality, so clinicians can use lower-dose regimens without compromising efficacy.

pubmed.title: 2026 ESAIC Consensus Document on Mitigation Strategies in Intensive Care Medicine: Consensus document of the European Society of Anaesthesiology and Intensive Care.
pubmed.bottom_line: This expert consensus document from the ESAIC provides 37 evidence-informed recommendations to reduce the environmental impact of intensive care medicine in Europe, achieving strong agreement (>80%) among 37 experts from 20 countries; its adoption could guide sustainable practices in energy use, waste management, medication, and ethics in ICUs, addressing both climate and health concerns.

pubmed.title: Analyses of gender disparities in receipt of bystander cardiopulmonary resuscitation after out-of-hospital cardiac arrests by patient's age group and bystander category.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large nationwide Japanese registry study found that while overall females were slightly more likely to receive bystander CPR after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, significant gender disparities persisted in certain age groups and bystander categories, with bystander type (especially non-family) being a much stronger determinant of CPR receipt than patient gender; this highlights the need for targeted public education to reduce disparities and improve CPR rates.

foamed.title: Regional Anesthesia
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This content from NYSORA serves as an educational overview promoting regional anesthesia, pain management, and perioperative medicine, but does not present new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.title: Followership
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational article emphasizes that effective followership—characterized by active engagement, adaptability, assertiveness, and the ability to both lead and follow as needed—is essential for optimizing team performance, patient safety, and error prevention in critical care, but presents no new clinical study data.

pubmed.title: Mortality effect of albumin fluid resuscitation in adults with septic shock: a systematic review and dual frequentist-bayesian meta-analysis of randomised trials.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis of seven randomized trials (n=3273) found that albumin-based fluid resuscitation in adults with septic shock is associated with a statistically significant 10% reduction in all-cause mortality compared to crystalloids, suggesting a plausible mortality benefit that may influence fluid choice in septic shock management.

pubmed.title: Prevention of arterial catheter-related bloodstream infections: current evidence and future directions.
pubmed.bottom_line: Current evidence for preventing arterial catheter-related bloodstream infections is largely extrapolated from central venous catheter data, with direct studies limited; established measures such as chlorhexidine antisepsis, catheter stewardship, and standardized insertion bundles are recommended, but further research is needed to clarify best practices for site selection, securement devices, and other interventions, highlighting the need for dedicated guidelines to reduce infection risk in clinical practice.

pubmed.title: Incidence and Outcomes of Emergency Physician-Performed Awake Intubations: A Report From the Airway Interventions Registry and Observational Database.
pubmed.bottom_line: This single-center, prospective observational study found that emergency physician-performed awake intubations were relatively common (7.2% of ED intubations), with high overall success (92%) and low rates of major adverse events, suggesting that awake intubation is a safe and effective option for difficult airways in the emergency department.

pubmed.title: Hepatitis C Screening Among Persons Experiencing Homelessness in the Emergency Department: A Secondary Analysis of the Determining Effective Testing in Emergency Departments and Care Coordination on Treatment Outcomes (DETECT) for Hepatitis C (Hep C) Screening Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large secondary analysis found that persons experiencing homelessness in the ED were more likely to accept hepatitis C screening and had significantly higher HCV seropositivity rates than housed patients, supporting the clinical importance of ED-based, especially nontargeted, HCV screening to identify and address infection in this high-risk, underserved population.

pubmed.title: Advancing Perioperative Medication Safety: Consensus Recommendations From the 2024 Anesthesia Patient Safety Foundation Stoelting Conference.
pubmed.bottom_line: This consensus statement from the 2024 APSF Stoelting Conference provides updated, expert-driven recommendations to improve perioperative medication safety, offering guidance that can inform institutional policies and clinical practice, though the strength of evidence is based on expert consensus rather than new empirical data.

pubmed.title: An Analysis of Quality Assurance Events in Nonoperating Room Anesthetizing Locations: A Qualitative Retrospective Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective cohort study of over 100,000 nonoperating room anesthesia (NORA) cases identified frequent quality assurance events related to organizational factors, equipment issues, and communication/teamwork challenges, highlighting specific safety vulnerabilities unique to NORA settings; these findings underscore the need for targeted interventions to improve patient and staff safety in these environments.

pubmed.title: Troponin T clearance via continuous renal replacement therapies in the ICU.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective observational study found that troponin T clearance is minimal and not significantly different across CRRT modalities in ICU patients, suggesting that CRRT is unlikely to meaningfully affect troponin T interpretation in the context of suspected acute coronary syndrome. This supports the clinical reliability of troponin T measurements in patients undergoing CRRT.

pubmed.title: Use and safety of corticosteroid injections in joints and musculoskeletal soft tissue: guidelines from the American Society of Regional Anesthesia and Pain Medicine, the American Academy of Pain Medicine, the American Society of Interventional Pain Physicians, and the International Pain and Spine Intervention Society.
pubmed.bottom_line: These new multidisciplinary guidelines, based on expert consensus and graded evidence, recommend lower corticosteroid doses than commonly used for joint and soft tissue injections, highlight short-term benefits but mixed long-term efficacy, and emphasize awareness of potential adverse events, guiding safer and more effective clinical practice.

pubmed.title: Comparison of femoral triangle plus iPACK blocks with femoral triangle block alone for anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction: a randomized controlled clinical trial on postoperative pain and knee function.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized controlled trial, adding an iPACK block to femoral triangle block and local infiltration analgesia did not reduce opioid use, pain scores, or improve knee function after ACL reconstruction, indicating no additional clinical benefit of the iPACK block in this multimodal analgesia setting.

pubmed.title: ASRA pain medicine narrative review and expert practice recommendations for gastric point-of-care ultrasound to assess aspiration risk in medically complex patients undergoing regional anesthesia and pain procedures.
pubmed.bottom_line: This expert consensus review recommends gastric point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) to assess aspiration risk in medically complex patients—particularly those in active labor, undergoing urgent cesarean, or with diabetes—while offering conditional support for other high-risk groups; evidence strength varies, and clinical judgment remains essential, highlighting POCUS as a useful adjunct but not a replacement for standard assessment.

pubmed.title: Factors associated with poor pain experience after surgery.
pubmed.bottom_line: This multicenter secondary analysis found that intraoperative use of remifentanil and sufentanil is strongly and independently associated with poorer postoperative pain experience, suggesting clinicians should carefully consider opioid choice during anesthesia to optimize patient pain outcomes.

pubmed.title: Perineuromal hydrodissection for acute postamputation pain? An observational study in a time of war.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this observational study of war-related amputees, early perineuromal hydrodissection added to opioid therapy significantly reduced residual limb pain and opioid use at 12 weeks compared to opioids alone, though effects on phantom limb pain were short-lived; while not definitive, these findings suggest hydrodissection may be a valuable adjunct for acute postamputation pain management.

foamed.title: Advances in Critical Care Medicine Insights - JournalFeed
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In the ARISE FLUIDS multicenter RCT of 1000 emergency department patients with septic shock, a restrictive fluid strategy with early vasopressors compared to a liberal fluid approach with later vasopressors showed no difference in days alive and out of the hospital at day 90, mortality, renal replacement therapy, or invasive ventilation.

foamed.title: Frailty in anesthesia
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational review highlights that frailty, distinct from chronological age, is a critical predictor of adverse perioperative outcomes—including increased complications, mortality, and longer hospital stays—underscoring the growing importance of frailty assessment and management in anesthesia and perioperative risk evaluation for older adults.

foamed.title: Essential Updates from POCUS Journals - JournalFeed
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: Recent studies summarized in POCUS journals indicate that emergency medicine residents achieve initial competency in eFAST after a median of 8 exams, transtracheal ultrasound offers the fastest confirmation of endotracheal tube placement among three techniques, and ultrasound-guided subclavian vein puncture yields higher success and fewer complications than landmark-based methods.

foamed.title: Bubble Wrap PLUS – July 2026
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational summary highlights recent pediatric research, including a large Chilean cohort study showing that food labeling and advertising laws were associated with modest but meaningful reductions in childhood excess weight, and a retrospective cohort study analyzing non-primary maternal CMV infections as contributors to congenital CMV; no new primary data are presented here.

foamed.title: You Don’t Always Need to Fix It: Active Listening as a Human Factors Skill for Pre-hospital and Emergency Clinicians
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational commentary emphasizing the importance of active listening as a critical human factors skill for pre-hospital and emergency clinicians, highlighting its role in patient safety, team performance, and well-being, but it does not present new clinical data or research findings.

deep_dives.title: Hepatitis C Screening Among Persons Experiencing Homelessness in the Emergency Department: A Secondary Analysis of the Determining Effective Testing in Emergency Departments and Care Coordination on Treatment Outcomes (DETECT) for Hepatitis C (Hep C) Screening Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large secondary analysis found that persons experiencing homelessness in the ED were more likely to accept hepatitis C screening and had significantly higher HCV seropositivity rates than housed patients, supporting the clinical importance of ED-based, especially nontargeted, HCV screening to identify and address infection in this high-risk, underserved population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Nontargeted hepatitis C screening in the emergency department is more effective at engaging persons experiencing homelessness, who have higher rates of HCV seropositivity and viremia than housed individuals.

- Study type: Secondary analysis of a multicenter randomized pragmatic clinical trial
- Population/setting: 67,223 emergency department visits at Denver Health Medical Center, including persons experiencing homelessness and housed individuals
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Nontargeted versus targeted hepatitis C virus (HCV) screening strategies
- Primary endpoints: HCV test offer, test acceptance, HCV seropositivity, and viremia
- Key results: Persons experiencing homelessness were more likely to accept nontargeted HCV screening (21.7% vs 15.7%) and had higher HCV seropositivity (14.2% vs 2.6%) and viremia (aRR up to 3.70) compared to housed individuals; homelessness was not associated with test offer.
- Limitations:
- Single-site secondary analysis may limit generalizability
- Potential unmeasured confounders
- Homelessness status may be misclassified
- Why this matters: Implementing nontargeted HCV screening in emergency departments can improve detection and linkage to care for high-risk, underserved populations such as persons experiencing homelessness.

deep_dives.title: Comparison of femoral triangle plus iPACK blocks with femoral triangle block alone for anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction: a randomized controlled clinical trial on postoperative pain and knee function.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized controlled trial, adding an iPACK block to femoral triangle block and local infiltration analgesia did not reduce opioid use, pain scores, or improve knee function after ACL reconstruction, indicating no additional clinical benefit of the iPACK block in this multimodal analgesia setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Adding an iPACK block to femoral triangle block and local infiltration analgesia does not provide additional analgesic or functional benefits after ACL reconstruction.

- Study type: Randomized controlled clinical trial
- Population/setting: Patients (n=90) scheduled for anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction under general anesthesia
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Femoral triangle block plus iPACK block versus femoral triangle block alone (both with local infiltration analgesia)
- Primary endpoints: Cumulative oral morphine equivalent (OME) consumption during the first two postoperative days
- Key results: Median OME consumption was similar between groups (FTB+iPACK: 60 mg [IQR 32-89] vs FTB: 50 mg [IQR 14-103]; median difference 5 mg, 95% CI -14 to 28, p=0.49); no significant differences in pain scores, opioid side effects, or knee function at 3, 6, or 9 months.
- Limitations:
- Single-center study
- Sample size may limit detection of small differences
- Results may not generalize to other surgical or analgesic protocols
- Why this matters: This study informs clinicians that adding an iPACK block to a multimodal analgesic regimen for ACL reconstruction does not improve pain control or functional outcomes, potentially simplifying postoperative pain management.

pubmed.title: Preoperative health status assessed with different scales and postoperative cardiac and cerebrovascular complications in older patients: A retrospective study of a large multicentre cohort.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective multicenter study of older surgical patients, adding preoperative health status scales (ADL, EQ-5D-5L, MET, FRAIL) to the Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) modestly improved prediction of 30-day major cardiac and cerebrovascular events, suggesting that incorporating these assessments may enhance perioperative risk stratification in this population.

pubmed.title: Lung- and diaphragm-protective mechanical ventilation in acute respiratory distress syndrome.
pubmed.bottom_line: Emerging evidence suggests that lung- and diaphragm-protective (LDP) ventilation, which balances respiratory effort to prevent both lung injury and diaphragm dysfunction in ARDS, is a promising new strategy, but robust clinical trials are still needed to determine its impact on patient outcomes. This approach may lead to more individualized and safer ventilation practices in ARDS management.

pubmed.title: 2025 ESAIC and ESPA Guidelines on neuromuscular block in children.
pubmed.bottom_line: The 2025 ESAIC and ESPA guidelines provide updated, evidence-based recommendations for the use of neuromuscular blocking agents in children, aiming to standardize practice and improve safety in pediatric anesthesia; the strength of evidence and specific clinical implications depend on the detailed content of the guidelines.

pubmed.title: Association Between Time Spent in the Emergency Department and 30-Day Mortality: A Population-Level Observational Study in England.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large observational study from England found that longer time spent in the emergency department for nonimmediate cases is associated with higher 30-day all-cause mortality, but causality is uncertain due to potential residual confounding; clinicians should be aware of this association, though further research is needed to clarify underlying mechanisms.

pubmed.title: Diminished rest-activity rhythm is associated with postoperative complications and mortality: A prospective cohort study of UK Biobank participants.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large prospective cohort study found that diminished rest-activity rhythm is associated with increased postoperative complications and mortality, suggesting that assessing circadian rhythm may help identify surgical patients at higher risk for adverse outcomes.

foamed.title: Haemoptysis
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational overview explains that haemoptysis is coughing up blood, with massive haemoptysis defined by life-threatening airway obstruction or blood loss (often >600 mL in 4 hours), most commonly originating from bronchial circulation, and typically caused by chronic inflammatory lung diseases or malignancies; no new clinical data are presented.

foamed.title: The limits of holding on to realism and hope alone
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is a reflective commentary on the challenges of maintaining realism and hope in emergency medicine, using the Stockdale paradox as a framework, and does not present new clinical data or research findings.

pubmed.title: An Optimization Randomized Clinical Trial to Identify an Effective, Efficient Smoking Cessation Intervention in the Context of Lung Cancer Screening: Cessation and Screening to Save Lives (CASTL).
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, multi-site randomized clinical trial among smokers undergoing lung cancer screening, the combination of loss-framed messaging plus nicotine patch and lozenge yielded the highest value-efficient 6-month abstinence rate (30%), while gain-framed messaging alone was a lower-cost but less effective alternative (13% abstinence), providing strong evidence to guide cost-effective smoking cessation strategies in this high-risk population.

pubmed.title: Ensartinib in Resected ALK-Positive Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this phase 3 randomized trial, adjuvant ensartinib significantly improved 24-month disease-free survival compared to placebo in patients with completely resected stage IB–IIIB ALK-positive NSCLC, though with higher rates of grade 3 or higher adverse events; this provides strong evidence supporting ensartinib as a new standard adjuvant therapy option in this population.

pubmed.title: Phase 3 Trials of Inhaled Treprostinil for Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In two phase 3 randomized trials, inhaled treprostinil significantly slowed the decline in forced vital capacity and reduced clinical worsening in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis compared to placebo, providing strong evidence for its potential as an effective adjunctive therapy in this population.

pubmed.title: Meningococcal B Vaccine to Prevent Neisseria gonorrhoeae Infection.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized, placebo-controlled trial among men who have sex with men, the 4CMenB (meningococcal B) vaccine did not reduce the incidence of Neisseria gonorrhoeae infection, providing strong evidence that this vaccine is not effective for gonorrhea prevention in this high-risk population. This finding is clinically important as it indicates that 4CMenB should not be used for gonorrhea prevention strategies.

pubmed.title: Setmelanotide for the Treatment of Acquired Hypothalamic Obesity.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this phase 3 randomized trial, setmelanotide led to significant reductions in BMI and hunger scores compared to placebo in patients with acquired hypothalamic obesity, providing strong evidence that setmelanotide is an effective treatment option for this challenging condition.

foamed.title: Updated pain management after elective cesarean section under neuraxial anesthesia
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article summarizes updated, evidence-based guidelines from the PROSPECT Working Group (2026) for pain management after elective cesarean section under neuraxial anesthesia, emphasizing the importance of effective analgesia for optimizing maternal recovery, infant care, and reducing risks of chronic pain and postpartum depression, based on systematic review of 61 randomized controlled trials.

foamed.source_name: WFSA Anaesthesia Tutorial of the Week
foamed.title: 基础超声心动图在ICU中的应用：麻醉医师及重症医学医师实用指南 第1部分
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational tutorial providing practical guidance for anesthesiologists and intensivists on the use of basic echocardiography in the ICU, focusing on equipment use, image acquisition, cardiac structure identification, and interpretation of parasternal long- and short-axis views, without presenting new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.title: The 103rd Bubble wrap x Wrexham Park Hospital
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational summary reviewing five clinically relevant papers in pediatric emergency medicine, including topics such as pulse oximetry in bronchiolitis, procedural sedation timing in URTIs, and antibiotic use in pediatric appendicitis, but does not present new original research data.

deep_dives.title: Phase 3 Trials of Inhaled Treprostinil for Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In two phase 3 randomized trials, inhaled treprostinil significantly slowed the decline in forced vital capacity and reduced clinical worsening in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis compared to placebo, providing strong evidence for its potential as an effective adjunctive therapy in this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Inhaled treprostinil reduced the decline in forced vital capacity and the rate of clinical worsening in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis compared to placebo over 52 weeks.

- Study type: Double-blind, randomized, phase 3 trial
- Population/setting: 598 patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, mean age 73 years, 77.3% men, 77.6% on background antifibrotic therapy, multicenter
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Inhaled treprostinil (12 breaths four times daily) versus placebo
- Primary endpoints: Change in forced vital capacity (FVC) at week 52
- Key results: Median FVC change at week 52 was -43.3 ml with treprostinil and -196.2 ml with placebo (difference 130.1 ml; 95% CI, 82.2 to 178.1; P<0.001); clinical worsening occurred in 31.8% of treprostinil group vs 44.5% of placebo (hazard ratio 0.67; 95% CI, 0.52 to 0.88; P=0.003); no significant difference in time to IPF exacerbation; cough was the most frequent adverse event.
- Limitations:
- High discontinuation rates due to adverse events
- No significant difference in time to IPF exacerbation
- No further inferences regarding secondary endpoints due to prespecified analysis order
- Why this matters: This study provides evidence that inhaled treprostinil may slow lung function decline and reduce clinical worsening in IPF, offering a potential new treatment option.

deep_dives.title: Ensartinib in Resected ALK-Positive Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this phase 3 randomized trial, adjuvant ensartinib significantly improved 24-month disease-free survival compared to placebo in patients with completely resected stage IB–IIIB ALK-positive NSCLC, though with higher rates of grade 3 or higher adverse events; this provides strong evidence supporting ensartinib as a new standard adjuvant therapy option in this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Ensartinib significantly improved 24-month disease-free survival compared to placebo in patients with completely resected ALK-positive stage IB to IIIB NSCLC after adjuvant chemotherapy.

- Study type: Phase 3, double-blind, randomized trial
- Population/setting: 274 patients with completely resected ALK-positive stage IB to IIIB NSCLC after adjuvant chemotherapy
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Ensartinib 225 mg once daily for 24 months versus placebo
- Primary endpoints: Disease-free survival in patients with stage II to IIIB NSCLC
- Key results: At 24 months, 86.4% of ensartinib group versus 53.5% of placebo group with stage II-IIIB disease were alive and disease-free (hazard ratio 0.20; 95% CI, 0.11–0.38; P<0.001); grade 3 or higher adverse events occurred in 35.8% (ensartinib) vs 18.2% (placebo).
- Limitations:
- Overall survival data were immature.
- Adverse events were more frequent with ensartinib.
- Why this matters: This study supports the use of ensartinib as adjuvant therapy to reduce recurrence risk in patients with resected ALK-positive NSCLC.

deep_dives.title: An Optimization Randomized Clinical Trial to Identify an Effective, Efficient Smoking Cessation Intervention in the Context of Lung Cancer Screening: Cessation and Screening to Save Lives (CASTL).
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, multi-site randomized clinical trial among smokers undergoing lung cancer screening, the combination of loss-framed messaging plus nicotine patch and lozenge yielded the highest value-efficient 6-month abstinence rate (30%), while gain-framed messaging alone was a lower-cost but less effective alternative (13% abstinence), providing strong evidence to guide cost-effective smoking cessation strategies in this high-risk population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: In the context of lung cancer screening, a combination of loss-framed messaging with nicotine patch and lozenge was the most value-efficient smoking cessation intervention, achieving a 30% abstinence rate at moderate cost.

- Study type: Optimization randomized clinical trial
- Population/setting: Adults aged 50-80 who currently smoke and were scheduled for lung cancer screening at 17 sites in the United States (N=758).
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Sixteen combinations of tobacco treatment components: enhanced standard care, motivational interviewing, nicotine patch/lozenge, and message framing (gain- or loss-framed).
- Primary endpoints: Self-reported smoking abstinence at 6 months follow-up.
- Key results: No significant differences among intervention components; the most value-efficient was loss-framed messaging plus nicotine patch and lozenge (30% abstinence, $287.41 mean cost), while gain-framed messaging alone yielded 13% abstinence at $51.29.
- Limitations:
- Self-reported abstinence may be subject to bias.
- No significant differences detected among intervention components.
- Cost estimates may not generalize to all settings.
- Why this matters: Identifying cost-effective smoking cessation strategies in the lung cancer screening setting can improve quit rates and optimize resource allocation for a high-risk population.

pubmed.title: Aspiration and GERD in Bronchiectasis: Bridging Pulmonology, Gastroenterology, and Otolaryngology in an At-Risk Population.
pubmed.bottom_line: This narrative review highlights the underrecognized role of aspiration and GERD as modifiable contributors to bronchiectasis, emphasizing the need for multidisciplinary evaluation and tailored diagnostic strategies; while not providing new primary evidence, it underscores the clinical importance of identifying and managing these comorbidities to potentially improve outcomes in bronchiectasis patients.

pubmed.title: Regional anaesthesia and chronic postsurgical pain: a systematic review and network meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large systematic review and network meta-analysis of 158 RCTs (18,794 patients) found that regional anaesthesia modestly reduces the incidence of chronic postsurgical pain (CPSP) up to 12 months after surgery, particularly in mastectomy, thoracotomy, VATS, and knee arthroplasty, but does not significantly reduce long-term opioid use; however, the certainty of evidence for CPSP reduction is low

pubmed.title: Manual versus Automatic Perioperative QTc Interval Assessment: A Prospective Observational Investigation.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective observational study compares manual and automatic perioperative QTc interval assessments, providing new evidence on their agreement and potential discrepancies; accurate QTc measurement is clinically important for identifying patients at risk of perioperative arrhythmias.

pubmed.title: Inhaled Treprostinil for Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large phase 3 trial, inhaled treprostinil significantly slowed FVC decline and reduced clinical worsening in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis over 52 weeks, suggesting a clinically meaningful benefit when added to standard antifibrotic therapy.

pubmed.title: Evaluating the effectiveness of bilateral erector spinae plane blocks in addition to standard multimodal analgesia at reducing opioid consumption in patients undergoing elective mammoplasty in an ambulatory surgical center: a randomized clinical trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized trial of patients undergoing elective mammoplasty, adding bilateral erector spinae plane blocks to standard multimodal analgesia resulted in a statistically significant but clinically insignificant reduction in opioid use and did not improve pain or satisfaction, suggesting ESP blocks offer limited additional benefit in this setting.

foamed.title: Patient preparation for ICU bronchoscopy
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article provides an educational overview emphasizing that thorough patient preparation—including assessment of physiological reserve, coagulation status, contraindications, and equipment readiness—is essential to minimize predictable and preventable adverse events during ICU bronchoscopy, as complications more often result from inadequate preparation than from the procedure itself.

foamed.title: Anatomy for bronchoscopy
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article provides an educational overview of airway anatomy relevant to bronchoscopy and airway management, emphasizing the importance of anatomical orientation, recognition of normal variants, and identification of abnormalities during bronchoscopic procedures, but does not present new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.source_name: WFSA Anaesthesia Tutorial of the Week
foamed.title: 基础超声心动图在ICU中的应用：麻醉医师及重症医学医师实用指南 第 2 部分
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational tutorial providing practical guidance for anesthesiologists and intensivists on the use of basic echocardiography in the ICU, focusing on equipment operation, image acquisition, and identification of cardiac structures, without presenting new clinical data or original research findings.

deep_dives.title: Inhaled Treprostinil for Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large phase 3 trial, inhaled treprostinil significantly slowed FVC decline and reduced clinical worsening in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis over 52 weeks, suggesting a clinically meaningful benefit when added to standard antifibrotic therapy.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Inhaled treprostinil reduced the decline in forced vital capacity and clinical-worsening events compared to placebo in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis over 52 weeks.

- Study type: Phase 3, double-blind, randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: 593 patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, mean age 71.7 years, 80.1% men, across multiple centers
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Inhaled treprostinil (12 breaths four times daily) versus placebo for 52 weeks
- Primary endpoints: Change from baseline in absolute forced vital capacity (FVC) at week 52
- Key results: Median FVC change at week 52 was -49.9 ml with treprostinil and -136.4 ml with placebo (between-group difference 95.6 ml; 95% CI, 52.2 to 139.0; P<0.001); clinical worsening occurred in 27.2% of treprostinil group vs 39.0% of placebo (hazard ratio 0.71; 95% CI, 0.53 to 0.95; P=0.02); cough was the most common adverse event (48.3% vs 24.1%).
- Limitations:
- No significant difference in time to IPF exacerbation, limiting further secondary endpoint analysis
- High discontinuation rates, especially in the treprostinil group
- Adverse events led to discontinuation in a substantial proportion of patients
- Why this matters: This study provides evidence that inhaled treprostinil may slow lung function decline and reduce clinical worsening in IPF, offering a potential new therapeutic option.

deep_dives.title: Evaluating the effectiveness of bilateral erector spinae plane blocks in addition to standard multimodal analgesia at reducing opioid consumption in patients undergoing elective mammoplasty in an ambulatory surgical center: a randomized clinical trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized trial of patients undergoing elective mammoplasty, adding bilateral erector spinae plane blocks to standard multimodal analgesia resulted in a statistically significant but clinically insignificant reduction in opioid use and did not improve pain or satisfaction, suggesting ESP blocks offer limited additional benefit in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Adding bilateral erector spinae plane (ESP) blocks to standard multimodal analgesia for elective mammoplasty did not result in a clinically meaningful reduction in postoperative opioid use.

- Study type: Randomized clinical trial
- Population/setting: 78 patients undergoing elective breast reduction mammoplasty at an ambulatory surgical center
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Bilateral ultrasound-guided ESP block plus standard analgesia versus standard analgesia alone
- Primary endpoints: Mean cumulative dose of oral morphine milligram equivalents used in the 24 hours postsurgery
- Key results: The ESP block group had a lower mean opioid consumption (10.6 vs 17.5 mg; p=0.014), but the reduction was not clinically significant; PACU discharge was faster in the block group (170 vs 213 min; p=0.012); fewer patients in the block group required opioids in PACU (59% vs 86.8%; p=0.006); no significant differences in pain scores, nausea/vomiting, or satisfaction.
- Limitations:
- Single-center study
- Relatively small sample size
- Clinical significance of opioid reduction was not achieved
- Why this matters: ESP blocks may not provide substantial opioid-sparing benefits in breast reduction surgery, questioning their routine use for this indication.

pubmed.title: YEARS Algorithm for Diagnosis of Suspected Pulmonary Embolism in Patients With Cancer: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large multicenter randomized trial, the YEARS algorithm was noninferior to CTPA-only strategies for ruling out pulmonary embolism in patients with active cancer, reducing unnecessary CTPA scans without compromising safety; this supports the use of the YEARS algorithm to safely streamline PE diagnosis in this high-risk population.

pubmed.title: Rivaroxaban Then Aspirin vs. Aspirin Alone after Total Hip or Knee Arthroplasty.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, double-blind randomized trial, aspirin alone was noninferior to rivaroxaban followed by aspirin for preventing symptomatic venous thromboembolism after total hip or knee arthroplasty, with similar or lower bleeding rates, supporting aspirin as a safe and effective single-agent prophylaxis option in this setting.

deep_dives.title: Rivaroxaban Then Aspirin vs. Aspirin Alone after Total Hip or Knee Arthroplasty.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, double-blind randomized trial, aspirin alone was noninferior to rivaroxaban followed by aspirin for preventing symptomatic venous thromboembolism after total hip or knee arthroplasty, with similar or lower bleeding rates, supporting aspirin as a safe and effective single-agent prophylaxis option in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Aspirin alone is not inferior to rivaroxaban followed by aspirin for preventing symptomatic venous thromboembolism after total hip or knee arthroplasty, with similar bleeding risks.

- Study type: Multicenter, double-blind, randomized, controlled trial
- Population/setting: 5429 patients undergoing total hip or total knee arthroplasty at multiple centers
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Aspirin 81 mg daily alone vs. rivaroxaban 10 mg daily for 5 days followed by aspirin 81 mg daily
- Primary endpoints: Symptomatic venous thromboembolism (proximal deep-vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism) and bleeding complications
- Key results: Venous thromboembolism occurred in 0.48% of the aspirin group and 0.45% of the rivaroxaban-aspirin group (risk difference, 0.02 percentage points; 95% CI, -0.34 to 0.39; P<0.001 for noninferiority). Major or clinically relevant nonmajor bleeding occurred in 1.66% of the aspirin group and 2.04% of the rivaroxaban-aspirin group (risk difference, -0.38%; 95% CI, -1.11 to 0.34).
- Limitations:
- Follow-up limited to 90 days.
- Study population may not represent all surgical or high-risk patients.
- Long-term outcomes not assessed.
- Why this matters: This study supports the use of aspirin alone as a simpler and equally effective thromboprophylaxis strategy after hip or knee arthroplasty, potentially reducing complexity and cost.

pubmed.title: Clinical Effectiveness of Acute Normovolemic Hemodilution in Cardiac Surgery: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 19 studies (including 15 RCTs) found that acute normovolemic hemodilution (ANH) modestly reduces allogeneic red blood cell transfusion rates in adult cardiac surgery, particularly with crystalloid-based protocols and on-pump procedures, without increasing adverse outcomes; however, the benefit was not statistically significant in RCT-only analysis, suggesting the evidence is suggestive but not definitive

pubmed.title: High-flow nasal cannula oxygen during breaks from noninvasive ventilation after extubation: an observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large observational study of high-risk patients after extubation, high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) during breaks from noninvasive ventilation (NIV) was associated with lower unadjusted extubation failure rates compared to standard oxygen, but this benefit was not statistically significant after adjustment for confounders; further randomized trials are needed to clarify its clinical value.

pubmed.title: Data-driven subtypes of postoperative delirium in surgical ICU patients: a secondary analysis of a prospective cohort study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This secondary analysis identified three distinct, data-driven subtypes of postoperative delirium in surgical ICU patients, each with unique clinical profiles and outcomes, notably with one subtype (urgent surgery, high inflammation, poor oxygenation) associated with the worst prognosis; these findings suggest that tailored management strategies based on delirium subtype may improve patient outcomes, though further validation is needed.

pubmed.title: Nutrition and Exercise in Critical Illness (NEXIS) trial: randomized trial of combined in-bed cycling and intravenous amino acid plus usual care.
pubmed.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this multicenter phase 2 randomized trial, early combined in-bed cycling and intravenous amino acid supplementation in ICU patients with acute respiratory failure was safe but did not improve physical function (6-minute walk distance) or secondary outcomes compared to usual care, suggesting no added clinical benefit from this intervention.

pubmed.title: The ARDS, Pneumonia, and Sepsis (APS) Consortium: Rationale, Design, and Feasibility of a National Platform for Phenotyping Critical Illness Syndromes.
pubmed.bottom_line: The APS Consortium successfully demonstrated the feasibility of rapidly enrolling and deeply phenotyping a large, diverse cohort of critically ill adults with ARDS, pneumonia, and sepsis, providing a robust national platform that will enable future research into biologically-based subgroups and targeted therapies; this foundational infrastructure is clinically important for advancing precision medicine in critical care.

pubmed.title: Multimodal Machine Learning Model Predicting Postoperative Delirium Based on Heart Rate Variability: A Prospective Observational Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large prospective study found that adding heart rate variability and ECG features to clinical data significantly improved machine learning prediction of postoperative delirium (AUC 0.728 vs. 0.673 for clinical-only), suggesting that multimodal models could enhance early identification of at-risk surgical patients.

pubmed.title: Discordant Neuron-Specific Enolase and Neurologic Outcomes in Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Decade-Long Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective study shows that while a peak NSE ≥60 μg/L is highly specific for poor neurological outcomes after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, a significant proportion of patients with normal or intermediate NSE still have poor outcomes driven by factors such as age, non-shockable rhythm, organ dysfunction, and cerebral edema, underscoring the need for multimodal prognostication rather than reliance on NSE alone.

pubmed.title: Comparison of supramaximal stimulation current determined by an electromyography-based neuromuscular monitor between obese and non-obese patients: A prospective observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This prospective observational study found that obese patients require significantly higher supramaximal stimulation currents for EMG-based neuromuscular monitoring, and initial device calibration may be insufficient in some cases, highlighting the need for manual adjustment to ensure accurate monitoring in this population. This finding is clinically important for optimizing neuromuscular blockade assessment in obese patients during anesthesia.

foamed.title: The TASC Trial: Therapeutic vs Prophylactic Anticoagulation for Acute Chest Syndrome
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In the phase 3 TASC trial, therapeutic-dose tinzaparin significantly reduced the duration of acute chest syndrome and opioid consumption compared to prophylactic dosing in sickle cell disease patients, without increased bleeding risk in a selected population, though findings are considered hypothesis-generating due to borderline statistical significance and exclusion of higher-risk patients.

foamed.title: Critical Kids: PRoMPT Bolus
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational summary discussing pediatric sepsis, highlighting that mortality rates remain high despite advances, and reviews evidence from adult trials suggesting balanced crystalloids may reduce kidney injury compared to normal saline, but does not present new pediatric clinical trial data or original research findings.

foamed.title: Perioperative management of SGLT2 inhibitors
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: A new international consensus statement recommends an individualized, risk-based approach to perioperative management of SGLT2 inhibitors, balancing the risk of euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis against the cardiovascular and renal benefits of continuing these medications in selected surgical patients, rather than applying a uniform strategy to all.

foamed.title: Anaphylaxis: Do all children really need 4 hours of observation?
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article discusses evolving guidance on observation periods after pediatric anaphylaxis, highlighting a new large multicenter study (Dribin et al., 2025) that may help individualize observation durations, but does not itself present new primary data; recommendations have historically been inconsistent and based on limited evidence.

deep_dives.title: High-flow nasal cannula oxygen during breaks from noninvasive ventilation after extubation: an observational study.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large observational study of high-risk patients after extubation, high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) during breaks from noninvasive ventilation (NIV) was associated with lower unadjusted extubation failure rates compared to standard oxygen, but this benefit was not statistically significant after adjustment for confounders; further randomized trials are needed to clarify its clinical value.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) during breaks from noninvasive ventilation (NIV) after extubation may reduce extubation failure, but adjusted analyses did not confirm a significant benefit over standard oxygen.

- Study type: Observational study based on two multicenter clinical trials
- Population/setting: 1077 high-risk post-extubation patients (>65 years or with cardiac/respiratory disease) from multicenter trials
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: NIV alternating with HFNC (NIV/HFNC) versus NIV alternating with standard oxygen (NIV/O2)
- Primary endpoints: Proportion of patients with extubation failure (reintubation or death within 7 days post-extubation)
- Key results: Extubation failure at day 7 was lower with NIV/HFNC than NIV/O2 (13.7% vs. 18.5%; difference -4.7%, 95% CI -9.4% to -0.3%, p=0.036), but adjusted analysis showed no significant difference (adjusted difference -3.9%, 95% CI -8.7% to 0.9%). Reintubation rates were lower with NIV/HFNC at 48h but not beyond.
- Limitations:
- Observational design limits causal inference
- Potential residual confounding despite adjustment
- Results may not generalize beyond high-risk populations
- Why this matters: Identifying optimal oxygenation strategies during NIV breaks could improve outcomes for high-risk patients after extubation.

deep_dives.title: Nutrition and Exercise in Critical Illness (NEXIS) trial: randomized trial of combined in-bed cycling and intravenous amino acid plus usual care.
deep_dives.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this multicenter phase 2 randomized trial, early combined in-bed cycling and intravenous amino acid supplementation in ICU patients with acute respiratory failure was safe but did not improve physical function (6-minute walk distance) or secondary outcomes compared to usual care, suggesting no added clinical benefit from this intervention.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Early combined in-bed cycling and intravenous amino acid supplementation in ICU patients with acute respiratory failure did not improve physical functioning compared to usual care.

- Study type: Multicenter phase 2 randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: 115 ICU patients with acute respiratory failure (median age ~55 years, >90% mechanically ventilated) at multiple centers
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Protocolized in-bed cycling (45 min/day) plus intravenous amino acid (total protein 2.0-2.5 g/kg/day) plus usual care versus usual care alone
- Primary endpoints: 6-minute walk distance (6MWD) at hospital discharge
- Key results: Median 6MWD was 108 (15-242) m in the intervention group and 95 (0-196) m in the control group (P = .51; median difference, 10; 95% CI, -75 to 84); secondary outcomes were also similar between groups.
- Limitations:
- Enrollment stopped early due to slow recruitment
- Sample size smaller than planned
- Phase 2 trial may be underpowered to detect small differences
- Why this matters: This study suggests that early combined exercise and nutritional interventions may not improve physical recovery in critically ill patients, informing ICU rehabilitation strategies.

pubmed.title: When Pneumonia Leads to Sepsis: Clinical Phenotypes and Therapeutic Challenges.
pubmed.bottom_line: This narrative review highlights that pneumonia-induced sepsis is biologically heterogeneous, with distinct host-response phenotypes influencing outcomes, and emphasizes that integrating molecular diagnostics with host-response biomarkers can improve risk stratification and guide therapy; however, evidence is primarily from recent observational studies and evolving clinical trials, underscoring the need for personalized approaches in critically ill patients.

pubmed.title: Perioperative Interventions in Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Systematic Review of Randomized Trials.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review of randomized trials highlights that evidence for perioperative interventions in patients with autism spectrum disorder is limited and heterogeneous, underscoring the need for more rigorous studies to guide clinical practice in this population.

pubmed.title: Continuous Erector Spinae Plane Blocks to Treat Pain after Percutaneous Nephrolithotomy: A Randomized, Triple-masked, Placebo-controlled Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized, triple-masked, placebo-controlled trial, adding a continuous erector spinae plane block to a single-injection block after percutaneous nephrolithotomy did not significantly reduce pain scores or opioid consumption over the first two postoperative days, suggesting no clinical benefit from the continuous technique in this setting.

pubmed.title: Ventilation parameters during Advanced Life Support in cardiac arrest (CAvent): A multicentre observational cohort study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large multicenter observational study found significant variation in ventilation parameters during CPR depending on ventilation mode and airway device, with bag-valve-mask (BVM) providing low tidal volumes, supraglottic airway devices (SAD) showing consistent but leaky performance, and asynchronous endotracheal tube (ETT) ventilation producing high airway pressures; these findings underscore the need for standardized ventilation monitoring during resuscitation to optimize patient outcomes.

pubmed.title: Procedure-Dependent Relationship Between Procedural Volume and Self-Perceived Mastery: A Two-Year Analysis of a Military-Civilian Emergency Training Program.
pubmed.bottom_line: This cross-sectional study of military emergency medicine trainees found that the number of procedures required to achieve self-perceived mastery varies significantly by procedure, with some skills (e.g., FAST) showing a stronger volume-mastery relationship than others (e.g., endotracheal intubation); this suggests that training programs should tailor procedural volume requirements to specific skills rather than applying uniform thresholds.

foamed.title: Can’t Intubate Can’t Oxygenate (CICO)
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational resource reviews the definition, principles, and emergency management of Can’t Intubate Can’t Oxygenate (CICO) scenarios, emphasizing early help, continuous reassessment, preparation for emergency front-of-neck access, and maintaining oxygen delivery, but does not present new clinical data or original research findings.

foamed.title: JournalFeed Blog | High-Yield Medical Research Summaries
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In adult trauma patients with hemorrhagic shock transported by helicopter EMS, prehospital transfusion of low-titer group O whole blood did not provide a statistically significant mortality benefit compared to blood component therapy, with similar short-term mortality outcomes and no significant difference in 30-day mortality between the groups.

foamed.source_name: WFSA Anaesthesia Tutorial of the Week
foamed.title: Traditional, complementary and alternative medicine: anaesthestic implications
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational tutorial discussing the anaesthetic implications of traditional, complementary, and alternative medicines (TCAMs), focusing on issues such as non-disclosure, herb–drug interactions, anaesthetic risks, and perioperative management, with the aim of increasing anaesthetists’ awareness and promoting evidence-based integration into conventional healthcare.

deep_dives.title: Continuous Erector Spinae Plane Blocks to Treat Pain after Percutaneous Nephrolithotomy: A Randomized, Triple-masked, Placebo-controlled Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized, triple-masked, placebo-controlled trial, adding a continuous erector spinae plane block to a single-injection block after percutaneous nephrolithotomy did not significantly reduce pain scores or opioid consumption over the first two postoperative days, suggesting no clinical benefit from the continuous technique in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Adding a continuous erector spinae plane block to a single-injection block after percutaneous nephrolithotomy did not reduce pain severity or opioid consumption over the first two postoperative days.

- Study type: Randomized, triple-masked, placebo-controlled clinical trial
- Population/setting: Adults undergoing outpatient percutaneous nephrolithotomy with preoperative ultrasound-guided perineural catheter placement
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Continuous ESPB with bupivacaine 0.25% versus placebo (normal saline) after single-injection ESPB
- Primary endpoints: Average daily pain intensity and cumulative oxycodone consumption over the first 2 postoperative days
- Key results: Median pain intensity was 3.5 for active versus 3.0 for placebo (estimated difference 0.25; 95% CI, -1.0 to 1.5; P = 0.538); cumulative oxycodone consumption was 10 mg for active versus 15 mg for placebo (estimated difference 0; 95% CI, -15 to 5; P = 0.358); no significant differences in secondary outcomes.
- Limitations:
- Small sample size (n=50)
- Single-center study
- Short follow-up duration (2 days)
- Potential for underpowering to detect small differences
- Why this matters: This study suggests that continuous ESPB may not provide additional analgesic benefit over single-injection ESPB for patients undergoing outpatient percutaneous nephrolithotomy.

pubmed.title: Awake tracheal intubation in the era of videolaryngoscopy: an international survey of 4728 clinicians.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large international survey found that use of awake tracheal intubation has declined with the rise of videolaryngoscopy, with significant variability in practice and many clinicians lacking familiarity with guidelines and flexible bronchoscopy skills; this highlights a need for updated training and reassessment of indications to ensure safe airway management in difficult cases.

pubmed.title: Effect of Early Administration of Shenfu Injection on Hemodynamics in Septic Patients: A Multicenter, Randomized, Controlled Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this multicenter randomized controlled trial, early administration of Shenfu injection in septic ICU patients did not reduce vasopressor requirements but was associated with significantly lower ICU and 28-day mortality and improved microcirculation; these secondary findings are promising but require further validation before changing clinical practice.

pubmed.title: Consent for anaesthesia: guidelines from the Association of Anaesthetists.
pubmed.bottom_line: These updated UK guidelines on consent for anaesthesia reflect recent legal and ethical changes, emphasizing individualized, high-standard patient counselling and aligning with current national standards; while not based on new clinical evidence, they provide essential legal clarity for anaesthetists, though applicability is limited outside the UK and Ireland.

pubmed.title: Lidocaine Versus Amiodarone in Patients With Shockable Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Target Trial Emulation.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, adjusted observational study emulating a target trial, lidocaine was associated with a non-significant 2.8% higher survival to hospital discharge compared to amiodarone in adults with shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, suggesting no clear superiority of either drug and supporting current guideline flexibility in antiarrhythmic choice.

pubmed.title: Efficacy and safety of riociguat in early pulmonary vascular disease (ESRA): a prospective, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase IIa trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this small phase IIa randomized controlled trial, riociguat significantly reduced pulmonary vascular resistance compared to placebo in patients with early pulmonary vascular disease, though no significant improvements were seen in secondary clinical endpoints; the drug was well tolerated, suggesting potential benefit in early intervention, but larger studies are needed to confirm clinical impact.

pubmed.title: Andes Virus - A Clinical Review.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: This review highlights that Andes virus is unique among orthohantaviruses for its human-to-human transmission, summarizes current expert consensus on clinical management, and discusses emerging therapies and vaccine prospects; while evidence is largely based on expert opinion and limited trials, awareness is crucial for infection control and guiding future treatment strategies.

pubmed.title: Extended Dual Antiplatelet Therapy for Multivessel Coronary Artery Disease.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large randomized trial, extending dual antiplatelet therapy to 24 months after drug-eluting stent implantation in event-free patients with multivessel coronary artery disease modestly reduced major cardiovascular events compared to aspirin alone, without significantly increasing bleeding risk, suggesting extended DAPT may benefit this population.

pubmed.title: Efficacy and Safety of Obinutuzumab in Active Systemic Lupus Erythematosus.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this phase 3, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, obinutuzumab significantly improved SLE disease activity response rates compared to placebo in patients with active systemic lupus erythematosus (without active nephritis), demonstrating strong evidence for its efficacy and supporting its potential as a new treatment option for non-renal SLE.

pubmed.title: Continuous or Fixed-Duration Maintenance Therapy in Multiple Myeloma.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large phase 3 trial of standard-risk multiple myeloma patients not undergoing upfront transplant, continuous (indefinite) lenalidomide maintenance did not improve overall survival compared to fixed-duration (2 years) maintenance, but was associated with more adverse events and second primary cancers, suggesting fixed-duration therapy is sufficient and may reduce toxicity.

pubmed.title: Comparison of costoclavicular and paracoracoid approaches for infraclavicular perineural catheter placement in outpatient hand and wrist surgeries: a randomized controlled trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: This randomized controlled trial found no significant difference between the costoclavicular and paracoracoid approaches for infraclavicular perineural catheter placement in terms of sensory blockade or analgesic outcomes in outpatient hand and wrist surgery, suggesting that either technique can be chosen based on operator preference without compromising efficacy.

foamed.title: TTL tips 16: Ketamine in trauma patients.
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article provides educational tips on the use of ketamine in trauma patients, highlighting its roles in analgesia and procedural sedation, dosing strategies, and the importance of monitoring, but does not present new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.title: Updates from Emergency Medicine Journals - JournalFeed
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In adult trauma patients with hemorrhagic shock transported by helicopter EMS, prehospital transfusion of low-titer group O whole blood (LTOWB) did not provide a mortality benefit compared to blood component therapy, with similar 30-day and early mortality rates observed between the two groups in this pragmatic cluster-randomized trial.

foamed.title: Dulcor
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article provides a historical overview of the classical signs of inflammation—rubor, tumor, calor, and dolor—originally described by Celsus, and discusses the later, likely apocryphal, attribution of the fifth sign, functio laesa, to Galen, without presenting new clinical data or research findings.

deep_dives.title: Effect of Early Administration of Shenfu Injection on Hemodynamics in Septic Patients: A Multicenter, Randomized, Controlled Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this multicenter randomized controlled trial, early administration of Shenfu injection in septic ICU patients did not reduce vasopressor requirements but was associated with significantly lower ICU and 28-day mortality and improved microcirculation; these secondary findings are promising but require further validation before changing clinical practice.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** In this multicenter randomized controlled trial, early administration of Shenfu injection in septic ICU patients did not reduce vasopressor requirements but was associated with significantly lower ICU and 28-day mortality and improved microcirculation; these secondary findings are promising but require further validation before changing clinical practice.

BOTTOM LINE: Early administration of Shenfu injection did not reduce vasopressor requirements in septic ICU patients but may reduce mortality and improve microcirculation, warranting further study.

- Study type: Prospective, multicenter randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: ICU patients aged 18-75 years with sepsis (Sepsis-3 criteria) in four tertiary care hospitals in central China
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Shenfu injection versus normal saline, both administered at 20 mL/hr for 5 hours, twice daily for 3 days
- Primary endpoints: Amount of vasopressors administered (norepinephrine equivalents)
- Key results: No significant difference in vasopressor dose between groups (median NEE: 22.00 vs. 20.00; p=0.16); SFI group had lower ICU mortality (3.5% vs. 11.6%; p=0.04) and 28-day mortality (15.1% vs. 27.9%; p=0.04), and improved sublingual microcirculation; no significant drug-related adverse events.
- Limitations:
- Primary endpoint not met
- Secondary outcomes require further validation
- Sample size may limit generalizability
- Why this matters: This study suggests Shenfu injection may improve survival and microcirculation in sepsis despite no effect on vasopressor use, highlighting a potential adjunctive therapy that needs further investigation.

deep_dives.title: Extended Dual Antiplatelet Therapy for Multivessel Coronary Artery Disease.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large randomized trial, extending dual antiplatelet therapy to 24 months after drug-eluting stent implantation in event-free patients with multivessel coronary artery disease modestly reduced major cardiovascular events compared to aspirin alone, without significantly increasing bleeding risk, suggesting extended DAPT may benefit this population.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Extending dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) with clopidogrel and aspirin for an additional 12 months after drug-eluting stent implantation in stable multivessel coronary artery disease patients reduces major cardiovascular events without increasing bleeding risk compared to aspirin alone.

- Study type: Open-label, randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: 8250 patients aged 18-75 years with multivessel coronary artery disease, event-free for 12 months post-drug-eluting stent implantation, at 97 centers in China
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Extended DAPT (clopidogrel plus aspirin for 12 additional months) versus aspirin monotherapy
- Primary endpoints: Composite of death from cardiovascular causes, nonfatal myocardial infarction, or nonfatal stroke (efficacy); clinically relevant or major bleeding (BARC type ≥2) (safety)
- Key results: Primary efficacy endpoint occurred in 5.8% of the DAPT group vs. 6.8% of the aspirin group (HR 0.82; 95% CI, 0.69–0.98; P=0.03); clinically relevant or major bleeding occurred in 1.4% vs. 1.5% (HR 0.89; 95% CI, 0.61–1.30; P=0.54).
- Limitations:
- Open-label design may introduce bias.
- Study conducted only in China, which may limit generalizability.
- Why this matters: These findings support the use of extended DAPT in stable patients with multivessel coronary artery disease after stenting to further reduce major cardiovascular events without increasing bleeding risk.

deep_dives.title: Efficacy and safety of riociguat in early pulmonary vascular disease (ESRA): a prospective, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase IIa trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this small phase IIa randomized controlled trial, riociguat significantly reduced pulmonary vascular resistance compared to placebo in patients with early pulmonary vascular disease, though no significant improvements were seen in secondary clinical endpoints; the drug was well tolerated, suggesting potential benefit in early intervention, but larger studies are needed to confirm clinical impact.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** In this small phase IIa randomized controlled trial, riociguat significantly reduced pulmonary vascular resistance compared to placebo in patients with early pulmonary vascular disease, though no significant improvements were seen in secondary clinical endpoints; the drug was well tolerated, suggesting potential benefit in early intervention, but larger studies are needed to confirm clinical impact.

BOTTOM LINE: Riociguat was safe and significantly reduced pulmonary vascular resistance compared to placebo in patients with early pulmonary vascular disease over 24 weeks.

- Study type: Prospective, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase IIa trial
- Population/setting: Adults with early pulmonary vascular disease (N=35; 97% female; mean age 65.5 years; 77.1% connective tissue disease-associated PAH) at multiple centers
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Riociguat versus placebo for 24 weeks
- Primary endpoints: Change in pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) from baseline to week 24
- Key results: Riociguat led to a significant reduction in PVR (-0.73±0.67 WU) compared to placebo (-0.02±0.67 WU), p=0.043 (27% placebo-adjusted reduction); no significant differences in secondary endpoints; only mild to moderate adverse events observed, with no serious events considered treatment related.
- Limitations:
- Small sample size (N=35)
- Majority female and connective tissue disease-associated PAH, limiting generalizability
- No significant effects on secondary endpoints
- Why this matters: This study provides evidence that riociguat may be a safe and effective option for reducing pulmonary vascular resistance in early pulmonary vascular disease, a population not previously well studied.

pubmed.title: Real-world effectiveness of adjuvanted RSVPreF3 vaccination in the prevention of RSV-related hospitalization among US adults aged ≥60 years.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective cohort study of US adults aged ≥60 years, the adjuvanted RSVPreF3 vaccine demonstrated strong real-world effectiveness (VE ~76%) in preventing RSV-related hospitalization, including in high-risk subgroups, supporting its clinical use to reduce severe RSV outcomes in older adults.

foamed.title: Pour Some Colour On Me: A Guide to PoCUS Valve Assessment
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article provides an educational overview and practical guidance for emergency physicians on using point-of-care ultrasound (PoCUS) to assess cardiac valves, aiming to help clinicians recognize significant valvular abnormalities at the bedside rather than presenting new clinical data or research findings.

foamed.title: Study compares intrathecal morphine and paravertebral block for VATS
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In a randomized non-inferiority trial, thoracic paravertebral block remained the preferred regional analgesic technique for video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery, as intrathecal morphine, while providing similar pain scores and recovery quality, did not demonstrate non-inferiority in reducing postoperative opioid consumption, supporting current guideline recommendations.

foamed.title: Shift Pearls: Anticoagulation with Heparin in PE
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational commentary providing clinical pearls on anticoagulation with heparin in pulmonary embolism (PE) and does not present new clinical study data.

pubmed.title: Hemoperfusion during extracorporeal membrane oxygenation: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis of 8,151 patients.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large systematic review and meta-analysis (8,151 patients, including 3 RCTs) found that adding hemoperfusion to ECMO does not reduce mortality and may increase it based on RCT data, suggesting no clinical benefit and possible harm, so routine use of hemoperfusion with ECMO is not supported.

pubmed.title: Sodium bicarbonate therapy in severe metabolic acidemia: an individual patient data meta-analysis of the BICAR-ICU and BICAR-ICU2 trials.
pubmed.bottom_line: This individual patient data meta-analysis of over 1,000 ICU patients with severe metabolic acidemia found that intravenous sodium bicarbonate did not reduce 90-day mortality overall, but did significantly reduce the need for renal replacement therapy; importantly, a mortality benefit was observed in the subgroup with pH ≤ 7.10, suggesting targeted use in the most acidemic patients may improve outcomes.

pubmed.title: Short- and Long-term Efficacy and Safety of Levosimendan in Veno-arterial Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation Weaning: The WEANILEVO Randomized Double-Blind Multicenter Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this multicenter randomized controlled trial, levosimendan did not improve VA-ECMO weaning success compared to placebo and was associated with higher rates of acute kidney injury and longer ICU stays, suggesting no benefit and potential harm in this setting; however, the study was underpowered due to early termination.

deep_dives.title: Short- and Long-term Efficacy and Safety of Levosimendan in Veno-arterial Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation Weaning: The WEANILEVO Randomized Double-Blind Multicenter Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this multicenter randomized controlled trial, levosimendan did not improve VA-ECMO weaning success compared to placebo and was associated with higher rates of acute kidney injury and longer ICU stays, suggesting no benefit and potential harm in this setting; however, the study was underpowered due to early termination.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Levosimendan did not reduce VA-ECMO weaning failure in patients who met weaning criteria and was associated with more acute kidney injury and longer ICU stays.

- Study type: Multicenter, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial
- Population/setting: 80 adult patients from five French centers on VA-ECMO who met predefined weaning criteria
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Single dose of levosimendan (0.2 μg·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ for 24 h) versus placebo
- Primary endpoints: VA-ECMO weaning failure, defined as absence of weaning within 48 h, need for another circulatory assistance device, or death within 7 days of weaning
- Key results: VA-ECMO weaning failure occurred in 25% of both groups (odds ratio 1.00, 95% CI 0.36–2.75; P=1.00); acute kidney injury at day 30 was higher in the levosimendan group (62.5% vs 38.5%; odds ratio 2.67, 95% CI 1.08–6.62; P=0.03); levosimendan group had longer ICU stays (median 12 vs 6 days; P=0.02); mortality at 30 days and 1 year did not differ significantly.
- Limitations:
- Study was underpowered due to halted funding and early termination
- Small sample size limits generalizability
- Findings regarding acute kidney injury and ICU stay are hypothesis generating
- Why this matters: This study provides the first randomized controlled data on levosimendan for VA-ECMO weaning, suggesting no benefit and potential safety concerns, highlighting the need for larger trials before routine use.

deep_dives.title: Sodium bicarbonate therapy in severe metabolic acidemia: an individual patient data meta-analysis of the BICAR-ICU and BICAR-ICU2 trials.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This individual patient data meta-analysis of over 1,000 ICU patients with severe metabolic acidemia found that intravenous sodium bicarbonate did not reduce 90-day mortality overall, but did significantly reduce the need for renal replacement therapy; importantly, a mortality benefit was observed in the subgroup with pH ≤ 7.10, suggesting targeted use in the most acidemic patients may improve outcomes.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
**BOTTOM LINE:** This individual patient data meta-analysis of over 1,000 ICU patients with severe metabolic acidemia found that intravenous sodium bicarbonate did not reduce 90-day mortality overall, but did significantly reduce the need for renal replacement therapy; importantly, a mortality benefit was observed in the subgroup with pH ≤ 7.10, suggesting targeted use in the most acidemic patients may improve outcomes.

BOTTOM LINE: Sodium bicarbonate therapy did not reduce overall 90-day mortality in severe metabolic acidemia but reduced renal replacement therapy (RRT) use and showed a mortality benefit in patients with pH ≤ 7.10.

- Study type: Individual patient data meta-analysis of two randomized controlled trials
- Population/setting: Adults with severe metabolic acidemia (pH ≤ 7.20) from the BICAR-ICU and BICAR-ICU2 trials
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate titrated to pH ≥ 7.30 versus no sodium bicarbonate
- Primary endpoints: 90-day mortality
- Key results: No significant reduction in 90-day mortality overall (58.3% vs. 60.6%; RR 0.96; 95% CI 0.86-1.07; p=0.51), but significant reduction in RRT initiation (34.8% vs. 50.7%; RR 0.69; 95% CI 0.60-0.79; p<0.001) and increased dialysis-free days; mortality benefit observed in patients with pH ≤ 7.10 (RR 0.80; 95% CI 0.68-0.93; p=0.004).
- Limitations:
