- Only two trials included
- Potential for unmeasured confounding
- Subgroup findings may be hypothesis-generating
- Why this matters: These results suggest that sodium bicarbonate therapy may help avoid RRT and improve survival in patients with the most severe acidemia, informing clinical decisions for critically ill patients with metabolic acidemia.

deep_dives.title: Hemoperfusion during extracorporeal membrane oxygenation: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis of 8,151 patients.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This large systematic review and meta-analysis (8,151 patients, including 3 RCTs) found that adding hemoperfusion to ECMO does not reduce mortality and may increase it based on RCT data, suggesting no clinical benefit and possible harm, so routine use of hemoperfusion with ECMO is not supported.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Hemoperfusion during ECMO does not reduce mortality and may increase mortality based on randomized controlled trial data; routine use is not supported.

- Study type: Systematic review and meta-analysis (including RCTs and non-RCTs)
- Population/setting: Adults receiving extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) in ICU settings; 8,151 patients from 13 studies (3 RCTs, 10 non-RCTs).
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: ECMO plus hemoperfusion (ECMO + HP) versus ECMO alone.
- Primary endpoints: All-cause mortality.
- Key results: Overall, ECMO + HP did not significantly reduce all-cause mortality compared to ECMO alone (OR 0.98, 95% CI 0.49-1.94; P = 0.95). In RCTs, mortality was higher with ECMO + HP (OR 4.96, 95% CI 1.67-14.77; P = 0.004), while non-RCTs showed no significant difference (OR 0.58, 95% CI 0.25-1.33; P = 0.20). No significant differences were found in subgroup or secondary outcomes.
- Limitations:
- Most included studies had moderate to serious risk of bias.
- High heterogeneity (I2 up to 79%) in several analyses.
- Insufficient evidence per trial sequential analysis.
- Limited number of high-quality RCTs.
- Why this matters: This analysis suggests that adding hemoperfusion to ECMO does not improve survival and may be harmful, informing clinicians to avoid routine use of this intervention in critically ill patients.

pubmed.title: Prone positioning in ARDS.
pubmed.bottom_line: Prone positioning is now a well-established, evidence-based intervention for intubated ARDS patients with moderate to severe hypoxemia, improving oxygenation and reducing ventilator-induced lung injury; its use in non-intubated (awake) patients shows promise but requires further validation, especially outside COVID-19 contexts.

pubmed.title: PROSPECT guideline for total hip arthroplasty: updated systematic review and procedure-specific postoperative pain management recommendations.
pubmed.bottom_line: This updated systematic review reaffirms scheduled paracetamol, NSAIDs, and single-dose dexamethasone as core postoperative analgesia for total hip arthroplasty, and highlights that supra-inguinal fascia iliaca and pre-operative pericapsular nerve group blocks offer the most reliable regional analgesia, while quadratus lumborum and lumbar erector spinae plane blocks are not recommended due to inconsistent efficacy and motor weakness risk

pubmed.title: Clinical Utility of Next Generation Sequencing in Concurrent EBUS-TBNA and Liquid Biopsies in NSCLC.
pubmed.journal: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this prospective cohort study of NSCLC patients, EBUS-TBNA detected significantly more clinically relevant mutations via NGS than liquid biopsy (39.7% vs. 29.6%), but liquid biopsy uniquely identified mutations in a subset of patients, indicating that blood-based NGS is a useful complementary tool; these findings support combined tissue and liquid biopsy approaches to maximize mutation detection for targeted therapy selection.

foamed.title: Impact of shorter preoperative fluid fasting
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: A large multicenter study found that reducing preoperative clear fluid fasting to 2–4 hours, as recommended by guidelines, was associated with improved patient comfort, lower rates of postoperative delirium, shorter hospital stays, and more efficient healthcare resource utilization compared to prolonged fasting before surgery.

foamed.title: emDOCs – Episode 145: Post Bariatric Surgery Complications Part 2
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This educational podcast episode reviews major post-bariatric surgery complications, emphasizing that anastomotic/staple line leaks are a leading early postoperative cause of death, often presenting with tachycardia, fever, and leukocytosis, and require prompt diagnosis (CT imaging) and management (resuscitation, antibiotics, surgical consultation), with treatment tailored to patient stability.

foamed.title: HFNC vs. NIV in Acute Cardiogenic Pulmonary Edema: Go with the Flow?
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: In patients with acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema, high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) performed similarly to non-invasive ventilation (NIV) in reducing respiratory rates, dyspnea scores, and arterial blood gas parameters over two hours, and may offer greater patient comfort, making HFNC a viable alternative for those intolerant of NIV masks.

deep_dives.title: Prone positioning in ARDS.
deep_dives.bottom_line: Prone positioning is now a well-established, evidence-based intervention for intubated ARDS patients with moderate to severe hypoxemia, improving oxygenation and reducing ventilator-induced lung injury; its use in non-intubated (awake) patients shows promise but requires further validation, especially outside COVID-19 contexts.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Prone positioning is now an evidence-based, integral part of ARDS management, improving oxygenation and reducing ventilator-induced lung injury, with expanding use in both intubated and non-intubated patients.

- Study type: Narrative review
- Population/setting: Patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), including both intubated and non-intubated (awake) patients, in ICU settings
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Prone positioning versus standard supine positioning
- Primary endpoints: Improvement in oxygenation, reduction in ventilator-induced lung injury, and hemodynamic effects
- Key results: Prone positioning consistently improves oxygenation in ARDS patients and reduces lung stress and strain; awake prone positioning shows promise but requires further validation in non-COVID and routine ICU populations.
- Limitations:
- Lack of new primary data; review article
- Awake prone positioning evidence is mainly from COVID-19 patients and not yet confirmed in other populations
- Practical implementation and generalizability in routine ICU practice remain uncertain
- Why this matters: Understanding the benefits and practical considerations of prone positioning can improve ARDS management and patient outcomes in both intubated and non-intubated populations.

deep_dives.title: PROSPECT guideline for total hip arthroplasty: updated systematic review and procedure-specific postoperative pain management recommendations.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This updated systematic review reaffirms scheduled paracetamol, NSAIDs, and single-dose dexamethasone as core postoperative analgesia for total hip arthroplasty, and highlights that supra-inguinal fascia iliaca and pre-operative pericapsular nerve group blocks offer the most reliable regional analgesia, while quadratus lumborum and lumbar erector spinae plane blocks are not recommended due to inconsistent efficacy and motor weakness risk
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Updated PROSPECT guidelines recommend scheduled paracetamol and NSAIDs, single-dose dexamethasone, and motor-sparing regional blocks (supra-inguinal fascia iliaca or pre-operative pericapsular nerve group block) for postoperative pain management after total hip arthroplasty.

- Study type: Systematic review and expert consensus guideline
- Population/setting: Patients undergoing elective primary total hip arthroplasty; studies included were randomized controlled trials and systematic reviews published between January 2020 and June 2024.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Analgesic and surgical interventions including paracetamol, NSAIDs, dexamethasone, low-dose intrathecal morphine, various regional anesthesia techniques, and local infiltration analgesia.
- Primary endpoints: Pain within 24 hours, opioid consumption, functional recovery, and adverse effects.
- Key results: Supra-inguinal fascia iliaca compartment block and pre-operative pericapsular nerve group block provided the most consistent analgesia; quadratus lumborum and lumbar erector spinae plane blocks were less effective and increased motor weakness; core recommendations for paracetamol, NSAIDs, and dexamethasone remain unchanged.
- Limitations:
- Did not include non-randomized studies.
- Recommendations based on available evidence up to June 2024.
- Expert consensus may introduce subjectivity.
- Why this matters: These updated, evidence-based recommendations help clinicians optimize pain management and functional recovery after total hip arthroplasty using the most effective and safest techniques.

pubmed.title: Health-Economic Value of Transvenous Diaphragm Neurostimulation in Ventilator Weaning.
pubmed.bottom_line: This exploratory cost-consequence analysis, using data from the RESCUE-3 randomized trial and real-world claims, suggests that transvenous diaphragm neurostimulation (tDNS) can improve ventilator weaning success, reduce ventilation duration, and generate significant cost savings and quality-adjusted life-year gains compared to standard care; while promising, these findings require confirmation in further studies before routine clinical adoption.

pubmed.title: Pulmonary Embolism Reporting and Data System (PE-RADS™) v2026 for the Diagnosis of Acute Pulmonary Embolism on CT and MR Angiography.
pubmed.bottom_line: PE-RADS v2026 introduces a standardized lexicon and structured reporting system for acute pulmonary embolism diagnosis on CT and MR angiography, aiming to improve clarity, consistency, and clinical decision-making; while this consensus-based framework is not yet validated by outcome studies, it addresses a key gap in imaging interpretation and multidisciplinary communication.

pubmed.title: Postoperative anaemia and long-term survival following cardiac surgery: a multicentre, retrospective, observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large retrospective study of cardiac surgery patients, moderate-severe anaemia at hospital discharge was only weakly associated with worse long-term survival after adjusting for baseline factors, suggesting that discharge haemoglobin adds limited prognostic value beyond preoperative status and surgical complexity; haemoglobin levels typically remained below baseline for at least a year postoperatively.

pubmed.title: Socioeconomic Disparities and the Role of Comorbidity in Hospital Mortality: A Dutch Nationwide Critical Care Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large Dutch nationwide ICU cohort study found that lower socioeconomic status is independently associated with higher hospital mortality, even after adjusting for comorbidities, underscoring persistent health disparities in critical care despite universal healthcare and highlighting the need for targeted interventions.

pubmed.title: Psychometric Properties of the Patient Dignity Inventory in a Longitudinal Cohort of Acute Respiratory Failure Survivors.
pubmed.bottom_line: The Patient Dignity Inventory demonstrated excellent reliability and validity in a longitudinal cohort of acute respiratory failure survivors, supporting its use to assess dignity-related distress in this population and enabling future dignity-centered interventions to address post-intensive care syndrome.

pubmed.title: Concomitant beta blocker therapy, intra-operative dexmedetomidine and postoperative cardiac events in older patients: a secondary analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This secondary analysis suggests that in older patients, the combination of beta blocker therapy and intra-operative dexmedetomidine may influence the risk of postoperative cardiac events; however, as a secondary analysis, the evidence is hypothesis-generating and should be interpreted with caution until confirmed by prospective studies.

pubmed.title: Optimal intrathecal morphine dosing for revision hip arthroplasty: an observational study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This observational study identifies optimal intrathecal morphine dosing for revision hip arthroplasty, providing evidence to guide safer and more effective analgesia in this patient population. The findings may help clinicians balance pain control with the risk of opioid-related side effects.

foamed.title: Anorectal Malformations
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article from Don't Forget the Bubbles provides an educational overview of anorectal malformations in newborns, emphasizing the importance of thorough examination to identify abnormal anatomy or fistulae, but does not present new clinical data or research findings.

deep_dives.title: Health-Economic Value of Transvenous Diaphragm Neurostimulation in Ventilator Weaning.
deep_dives.bottom_line: This exploratory cost-consequence analysis, using data from the RESCUE-3 randomized trial and real-world claims, suggests that transvenous diaphragm neurostimulation (tDNS) can improve ventilator weaning success, reduce ventilation duration, and generate significant cost savings and quality-adjusted life-year gains compared to standard care; while promising, these findings require confirmation in further studies before routine clinical adoption.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Transvenous diaphragm neurostimulation (tDNS) may provide significant cost savings and quality-of-life benefits in ventilator weaning compared to standard care, supporting its potential as a cost-effective intervention.

- Study type: Cost-consequence analysis with exploratory lifetime projection modeling based on clinical trial and real-world data.
- Population/setting: Mechanically ventilated patients in acute and long-term care hospital settings in the United States, informed by the RESCUE-3 randomized trial and real-world claims data.
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Transvenous diaphragm neurostimulation (tDNS) versus standard of care (SoC) for ventilator weaning.
- Primary endpoints: Stakeholder-specific incremental costs and quality-adjusted life-year (QALY) gain with tDNS.
- Key results: tDNS led to a 2.8-day reduction in mechanical ventilation, 10.5% higher weaning success, projected cost savings of $12,102 (acute) and $11,999 (long-term care) before tDNS cost, and 0.10-0.68 QALYs gained over a patient's lifetime.
- Limitations:
- Exploratory analysis with modeling assumptions.
- Cost of tDNS not included in base case cost savings.
- Long-term projections require further validation.
- Future lifetime cost-effectiveness analyses are needed.
- Why this matters: This analysis suggests that tDNS could reduce healthcare costs and improve patient outcomes in ventilator weaning, informing decision-making for hospitals and payers.

deep_dives.title: Pulmonary Embolism Reporting and Data System (PE-RADS™) v2026 for the Diagnosis of Acute Pulmonary Embolism on CT and MR Angiography.
deep_dives.bottom_line: PE-RADS v2026 introduces a standardized lexicon and structured reporting system for acute pulmonary embolism diagnosis on CT and MR angiography, aiming to improve clarity, consistency, and clinical decision-making; while this consensus-based framework is not yet validated by outcome studies, it addresses a key gap in imaging interpretation and multidisciplinary communication.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: The PE-RADS v2026 aims to standardize the interpretation and reporting of acute pulmonary embolism on CT and MR angiography to improve diagnostic accuracy and communication among care teams.

- Study type: Consensus guideline/Reporting system development
- Population/setting: Patients undergoing CT or MR angiography for suspected acute pulmonary embolism; multispecialty care teams
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Implementation of the PE-RADS structured reporting and lexicon versus prior unstandardized reporting practices
- Primary endpoints: Creation of a standard lexicon and structured, hierarchical reporting system for acute PE diagnosis and management
- Key results: PE-RADS provides a standardized approach for describing acute PE findings, classifying clot location, and assessing right heart features to inform management decisions.
- Limitations:
- No clinical validation or outcome data presented
- Implementation and impact on workflow not assessed
- Why this matters: Standardized reporting for acute PE can enhance communication, facilitate risk stratification, and support timely, appropriate management across multidisciplinary teams.
